This is probably the least expensive way to get a quality set of pure nickel strings. You could order from boutique string makers, but you definitely won't be disappointed with these D'Addarios. Strings, in many ways, are the heart of your tone and feel of the guitar itself. For my taste these pure nickels get it just right. They are listed as "mellow" but don't read that as dull, nor are they noticeably lower in overall output. I think they're just beautiful. The tone is magic and very consistent from the first time you tune them up and for many hours afterwards. They just feel quality, and as far as I'm concerned are exactly what I need on my Standard American Telecaster. I play a blend of styles in a recording environment where it's never a bad thing to have that nice round chime, and sweet sounding note definition. It's just too bad these aren't more available at local guitar shops.

Cher is "Living Proof" that there is life after the smash "Believe." This CD is full of full-blown arrangements which for the most part don't overpower the songs. Though Cher's vocals are way over-produced on the catchy title track, it's still a grand dance anthem, and the entire album has a vibrant sound. "Love is a Lonely Place Without You", "Real Love" and "Love One Another" are standouts do you detect a theme here? It can't be easy to follow-up an international megaseller like Cher's "Believe" but "Living Proof" shows it can be done, with the usual flare from a living legend, Cher. Long may she sing! And by the way, when is "3614 Jackson Highway" going to be available on CD? "Walk On Guilded Splinters" continues to rock my dreams.

I am the senior operator in a small planetarium in Ohio, the Clark Planetarium at Shawnee State University. We recently updated our system, but one of the main issues we had is the 60hz hum through the ground loop. Our system is sprawled out across several circuits, so it was very difficult to isolate and eliminate the noise problem until we purchased the Hum Eliminator. We purchased just one of them to try it out because the costs are rather high , but now that we have field tested that one, we will be purchasing more for our other input systems. If you have noise problems, this is a HIGHLY recommended product.

In my book there are two definitive recordings of "Carnival of the Animals". One is the fantastic one narrated by Leonard Bernstein Bernstein Favorites Children's Classics nbsp who explains the fantastic musical background of this piece. This recording also features many gifted young musicians as soloists whose names are now world renowned including Paula Robeson flute , Ruth Naomi Siegal piano and others. The other definitive recording is this one narrated by Noel Coward who is reciting the now-famous Ogden Nash poems. These two recordings will always be linked in my mind for the following reason. The first time I heard "Carnival of the Animals" was the Berstein version. In his explanation about "The Aquarium" Mr. Bernstein mentions that one of the instruments which the piece was originally written for was the glass Harmonica, but this instrument had been replaced in most performances by a Glockenschpiel. As far as I know, the Noel Coward recording is the ONLY one where "The Aquarium" is actually played with a real glass Harmonica - and thus is the only recording of the piece which captures exactly what Camille Saint-Sans originally intended for the music. For this reason, this recording is a must-have for lovers and students of classical music, as well as fans of "Carnival of the Animals".

nbsp There are many reviews of the Joyo amp simulator series available online if you look. Inevitably people are impressed by their versatility and cost-effectiveness. Many of the pedals are claimed to be clones of the Tech21 "SansAmp" series. I found that many of the settings on Tech21's website for their "Liverpool" pedal translated very well onto this Joyo pedal. Mentally swap "character" for "voice". The pedal itself has a 9 volt battery inside when shipped, something I didn't realize until I went to put velcro on the bottom to mount it. For my demonstration, I used a 9 volt power supply to drive the pedal. Initially I thought my unit was defective, as it would not turn on. I then realized you have to have something plugged into the input side for the pedal to power up, even when running on the wall current. Otherwise, I had no problems. In use, the sound goes from clean to overdrive to "melt your face" fairly easily The range is largely controlled by the "drive" and "voice" knobs, and the "mid" knob also steers the sound very aggressively depending on where you set it. The level knob is useful to set unity gain, but as this is mostly designed to be an "always on" pedal, you probably will set it and forget it. My experience with a few different guitars, is that the extremes of the knob ranges are mostly unusable or at least not to my taste. Pretty much every knob has it's sweet spot of tones from 8 00 to 4 00 on the dial. Mix and match in various combinations to go from Queen to John Mayer to the Beatles. My prefered setting, shown as the final one in the video gives a nice meaty tone, without being rude, to my Yamaha Pacifica. As I tend to be more subdued in my musical tastes these days, that is as aggressive as I need for my worship song playing. The only real fault I could find with the pedal is that when power is removed with it engaged, the signal is blocked. This means it is not "true bypass" a concern for some, not so much for me . The serendipitous advantage I noted with this "problem" is that playing with a cheap guitar that has marginal pickups, the pedal significantly enhanced the sound they produced. I'm sure this has to do with buffering, capacitance, impedance and maybe even the phase of the moon, but yet my wife agreed -- the guitar sounded much better, even with the pedal settings turned down. Who knows? YMMV. After my experience with this pedal, I am anxiously awaiting the chance to buy the "American Sound" pedal from Joyo as well. I purchased this one at a discount from the vendor OutLife for evaluation and review purposes.

Keith Davis has established a distinguished reputation as a jazz pianist, composer, and educator during his more than 30 years as a professional musician. He has toured with the Glenn Miller Orchestra, the Artie Shaw Orchestra, with Matt "Guitar" Murphy Blues Brothers , and was a member of the Teddy Adams Quintet. Keith has studied with respected Atlanta jazz instructor, Ted Howe, as well as with classical pianist Steve Hall. He also attended the Berklee College of Music in Boston, where he studied with renowned saxophonist Jerry Bergonzi. Currently, Keith lives in Greenville, SC, where he serves as Adjunct Instructor of Jazz Piano at Furman University. He performs throughout the region and also teaches privately. The first release by this veteran jazz pianist and his outstanding trio is a reflection of his many years of playing and composing, while pointing out a direction for future development. The trio is composed of Ron Brendle on bass, Justin Watt on drums, and Keith on piano. Keith also composed all of the music. The works on this recording are as follows Lieb New Beginning Chrysalis Elis Taiji Camp Raja Numia Tadjimon Suzy's Waltz Tadji's Groove For piano music lovers this s a complete treat. Keith Davis is front and center and playing like a wizard. Grady Harp, March 14

I hate to say it, but save your money, and buy the slightly more expensive mute holders instead of this one. Please heed the other reviewers when they say that this holder only fits music stands where the desk part is thin and or doesn't have a lip. Another downside is that you must put this holder on the left part of the desk. Why, you ask? Because if you put it on the right side of you, when you turn a page, the upper part of this holder may will catch on the page you are turning leftward. I learned this fact the hard way when turning a page, I ripped the bottom edge of the paper on this ill-designed holder. Not sure why this is also considered a cup holder. The only cup this thing will hold is maybe a 16 oz plastic cup...that is half full. Any more weight, and the ring of the holder slants forward, spilling the drink. For my money, the "Mute Mate" brand mute holder is the best. I've been a trumpet player in pit orchestras for many years, and I swear by it. It is spring-loaded, and clamps to the pole of the stand not the desk lip , and I have never come across a stand that it hasn't fit. Sorry, Manhasset.

This is a wonderful cd from the Benedictine Sisters. I often pop this into my car when I need a calming break from the craziness of daily life, even when it's not Advent, and especially while sitting in impossible traffic. I can sometimes actually feel my blood pressure lowering to a more sane level when the sisters start to sing. These cloistered sisters have made an angelic collection of music appropriate for Advent, really preparing one for the Christmas season instead of rushing it the way that most radio stations do before the Thanksgiving turkey is even thawed. If singing means praying twice, the sisters here have probably doubled even that! Well done!

The Audio Buddy dates back over a decade, and my unit is from around then. I've used it as a microphone and a guitar preamp with several computers, via an Edirol UA-1X USB interface. I've also used it in live music settings to mic double bass and feed the output to a bass guitar amp. It's worked very well all those years and never given me any trouble at all. It is a surprisingly good preamp for the money. Noise level is very low, and sound quality is excellent. Some reviewers have noted that the phantom power is only 41V, rather than the spec industry 48V, but I haven't noticed any problems is using the Audio Buddy with my Samson CO2 microphones. You might see a loss of headroom with high-end mics in critical recordings, but for really critical work you're not going to use an inexpensive preamps like this. There shouldn't be any problem in using it with electret condenser microphones, which can run on phantom power as lot as 12V. All in all, an excellent value for a budget-minded home studio, and for live music applications as well.

I love this Lil Green Ash Vac. I use it to clean my pellet stove insert, it is so clean compared to my shop vac NO dust at all and plenty of suction. Does not clog up like the shop vac did. I bought a spare filter when I got the unit but have not needed to use it yet, so much nicer than my old one. The only draw back is it could use a longer hose but since all I use it for is my pellet stove it is just fine for that. Compared to some of the 400.- 500. units out there this a Gem for less than 90.00. I would guess this will last a good long time due to the way the filter system works. I do not write many reviews so you know this Little Green Giant has really impressed me. There is NO dust flying all over the house! I hope this has helpful. PS Tip I take the top off after each use not the filter then I gently tap the filter while it is in place and then put the top back on ready for the next use, you could dump it at that time if need be but I can go 4 times or so before dumping and washing my filter. The gentle tapping of the filter knocks down the excess ash buildup. Even tho the red gauge is only half way, I just feel this will help the vac run much longer.

Don't buy this vacuum if you don't want to be totally disgusted by your own living conditions. But if you want your floors clean - this is the vacuum for you! It is lightweight, and it does a GREAT job. My vacuum cleaner gave up the ghost so we hadn't vacuumed in a week - in a family of 6 with frequent teenage visitors - that is a very long time. I did some research, selected this one and ordered it. It arrived quickly, needed two little screws inserted to put the handle on and voila - we were off and running. My house is mostly hardwood floors with three large area rugs. This vacuum was amazing - it sucked up all the dirt and moved easily between the hardwood and the rugs. I set the setting to the middle and I will say on one of my rugs it was a little harder to push. Nothing bad - but I noticed it and had to put a bit more effort in for that rug. I feel sure if I had adjusted the setting it would have been easier. I used the hose to get the corners of the rooms and that worked great too - although, I did have to bend over so for a middle aged woman that made my back a little achy by the end. I filled the tank! Disgusting but true. The beautiful thing - the tank came off super easy push a button and then the bottom opened up easily and dropped all the nasty right into the garbage again, just the push of a button . Everything evacuated easily and quickly so nothing had to "go in" after it. i.e. no hands got dirty in the emptying of the vacuum. And then, much to my joy - the tank snapped right back into place. I didn't have to wrestle with it, no swear words were required, it just did exactly what it was supposed to do with ease. No degree required to figure it out. Very very pleased with this vacuum.

This was received as an Xmas present. Normally I swear by a different and substantially more expensive vacuum. I was a little sceptical as to whether this vac would do a good job, but since I was planning to use this in a condo and didn't want anything large or elaborate I decided to give this a try. First off, this vac is light and that's a good thing since I am in rehab after breaking an arm in three places. I can't put a lot of stress on that arm and this is really light. I can move it from one floor to another with my good arm. It is relatively compact and extremely light at eight pounds. Another plus is that it has a fairly long cord which limits how much I have to plug unplug it. Construction seems to be fairly good as well. The real test is how well it picks up gunk like sand which we are forever tracking inside. This vac sucks in a good way. It pretty much picks up everything in one or two passes and manages to get close to the baseboards. It does an exceptional job when it comes to sucking up dust bunnies and lint or really anything else you may have kicking around the house. My favorite feature is it self adjusts to the carpet pile and type of floor. Yep, I can use it in the hallways and kitchen ceramic tile and hardwood and then tackle the carpeted areas. It isn't too big and easily tucks away into the utility closet. As fir what I don't like I thought I had said goodbye to bags and belts. Honestly, I wish this had a press-the-button canister and was beltless. These are two things I have never much cared for. Well, hopefully the belt will last a long, long time. As for the bags, they are not hard to install and seem reasonably priced. Aside from my usual complaints such as 'why doesn't this vacuum by itself?', this isn't a Roomba. For this particular situation, a Roomba just doesn't make sense. However, a good serviceable vac with very good suction that can handle adjustable floor surfaces makes plenty of sense. With care, this should last a long time. I'm not sure this would have been my first choice in a vacuum, but it has turned out to be a wonderful choice and a gift I am very pleased with.

This is a great collection, for a great piece of software Sonicfire . But potential buyers should be aware that the libraries here are not from their "Multi" but the "Single" layer libraries. If you're not sure what that means, it means that Multi libraries allow you to control the volume of every instrument in a mix. The single libraries don't. How will this affect you? Well, if you're making student films, home movies, or just need background music that fits your scene, probably very little. But if you're one who really digs into software, and is trying to get every nuance out of it, to hopefully make a profession production, you may want to only look at the Multi libraries. See SmartSound's website for more information.

This Yank loves both the show and this tune! For those who don't know, Irma Thomas' "Anyone Who Knows What Love Is Will Understand "--a tune I had not heard before seeing "Black Mirror" on Netflix--is recurrent in several episodes of the UK Sci-Fi anthology. The show has now been released in the US through Seasons 1-3. The term "Black Mirror" refers to the screens of all the devices we are surrounded by everyday. Your TV, your computer, your laptop, your tablet, your smartphone, your MP3 player, your automated appliances, your car's dashboard. "Black Mirrors" are our life. The show features stories all based on how this technology is affecting us--and will affect us--in the near future. Black Mirror is an anthology show, so features a different story each episode like the old "Twilight Zone" or "Outer Limits," etc. The tune's great either way! But, if you get into Black Mirror, you WILL get into this tune! Enjoy Both!

House of Heroes have somehow remained one of the best kept secrets in rock. Like so many great bands over the past several years, they've fought and scrapped to keep going, and that grit and desire come through in their music. Smoke is a prime example four-part harmonies weaving in and out of rock that ebbs and flows, that drives, punching and kicking one moment before smoothly downshifting into haunting harmonies--this is HOH doing what it does so very well, making music that is both classic and immediate. Let Smoke drift through your speakers on repeat, and let the hooks of "Bottle Rocket," "Wake Up Screaming," and the lingering sweep of "Infinite" sink in. Fans of the punchier HOH will enjoy "The Worst Kind of gods," and "A Fire Only We Know" may be one of the top lyrical moments for a band with a history of smart, creative songwriting. These six songs only whet the appetite for more, and thanks to Indiegogo supporters, more is coming in the form of a concept album, sometime soon we hope .

I really agonized over whether I wanted to risk purchasing this online. I'd tried out several different ukes in different stores to get a feel for what I wanted, and I really loved the way the Luna sounded, but it was hard to find one to buy at a brick-and-mortar. I need not have worried, however -- this uke was in excellent condition when it arrived. Not only did I receive the promised hard case, but it also came with a gig bag, which I did not at all expect. The tuner was different than the one pictured, as well, being a Snark tuner, which is a nice brand, easy to use for string instruments, though slightly less accurate than my other electronic tuner. The uke itself really looks stunning. I love the unique design. The tone is very rich but still mellow, a little bit quieter than my brother's Mahalo he suspected the soundhole muted things a bit but still plenty loud enough, and without the harsher sound of the Mahalo. The action is just right, easy on my fingers without being so low as to cause buzzing. The concert size is perfect -- makes a lot of chords easier than the smaller soprano size. I love that it comes with Aquila strings, too. Oh, yeah, let me take just a minute and talk about the case. I have purchased a lot of hard cases in my day, and this is one of the best I've ever seen -- really beautiful! I expected it to look cheap because of the low price for the package, but this one blows most of my hard cases out of the water. The inside fabric has a wonderful nap to it, and there's reinforcement in all the right places, and feels strong enough to withstand a normal amount of bumping around while traveling. The double zipper makes locking easy. I just love the whole package. I've been playing my uke almost daily since I got it, and I'm very pleased that the strings hold their tone very well ... I barely need to tune up at the beginning of a session. I'm sure there are better professional models out there, but for a student just starting out on the instrument, I am extremely pleased.

This device exceeded my expectations. I was able to port my LG LED T. V. Digital out to my existing Bose wave radio AUX in. The device came with all the needed connections I did not need to use the RCA splitter as the device already has a stereo 3.5 mm jack, which act as an audio OUT. Below is how I connected it. 1. From the T. V. - optical cable one end went in T. V. Optical out and the other end went into the Fiio device optical in. 2. From the fiio device I used an old male male 3.5 mm stereo jack. One end went into the fiio device the other end went into my Bose wave radio aux in. 3. Plugged the ac to Dc power using included USB cable on the fiio device and turned my Bose wave on, on the T. V. I went to audio settings and ensure it's set to PCM. Voila! The sound is not surround sound but 10x louder and crisper than the onboard speakers. I have not tried powering the fiio device via my TV USB port. TBA. I have not tried this watching over the air digital content. TBA. I highly recommend this if you wish to reuse your existing audio system. I gave it 4 star because of lack of turning the system on or off. This means the fiio device is plugged all the time via USB. No sign of over heating. But like with any other electronic device, a power supply will break, and it only needs time. It would be nice if it will auto shut off if no input is detected. Anyhow, this is a minor issue as the device outputs only 3-5 volts of DC.

Purchased from Amazon and received item on 3 4 11. Did a minor test under very low power to ensure functionality and it worked. On 3 7 11 I hooked up the amp into its final configuration, powering a set of cerwin vega E712's. These speakers are 300Watt 8ohm speakers, so they should be about perfect for this amp. Sending unit is an HT Omega Striker sound card from my computer. Attempting to test amp, had each channel up 1 2 way, and on second bass note of song, fuse for the room in my house blew. Unhooked amp and took to another room with no inputs, let it sit for a minute as I reset fuse. Smelled ozone in the room, but not bad. Plugged amp into another room to see if it still powered on and to do another test. When I flipped it on, amp made buzzing arcing sound like a downed power wire , and it started to smoke. At no time during its brief operation did I exceed any of the specs or have anything hooked up incorrectly, nor was I overdriving the speakers. Amp never kicked into protection mode, even when it started sparking. I had to yank the power cord as I did not want to touch the metal casing of the amp. Will be returning this for a refund and seeking a more reputable amplifier for these speakers.

I went from a 900 sq ft apartment with all tile and one rug to a 2300 sq ft home with mostly carpet. With my first home i bought the cheapest vacuum i could find at the store, but when I bought my second home and developed adult onset allergies to my dog, I really needed a new vacuum. Pros - The suction is very strong The powerful suction can also be turned down for small rugs or long carpet It is very lite and easy to maneuver around It has a small head which makes it easy to fit around and inbetween furniture The canister is easy to remove and dump and then to place back on It is easy to clean the filters, just remove and rinse The cord is very long It has a hepa filter which has helped me a lot It is one of the more quieter vacuums Ive used I used to clean houses so Ive used a lot It is easy to switch from carpet to tile or wood as you move through the house Cons - To use the wand, you must unravel the entire cord Cleaning the roller brush is a huge pain because it can not be unscrewed to remove the hair easily, I have to cut the hair off The vacuum tips over easily. Disregard the review below--it's about bad shipping, not a bad product! There are places to complain about that elsewhere this is a review of the actual performance. As for that, Anissimov has recorded a large swath of Glazunov's orchestral music, all quite capably, too. He also recorded a nice cycle of Rachmaninov symphonies, of which No. 1 was quite remarkable, especially with its inclusion of the rarely played Caprice Boheme. As a conductor, he's perfect for this repertoire, as he conducts with a light touch, bringing out the orchestral color which the Russians excel at . He makes Glazunov seem fresh, bright, and balletic--perhaps a little unfair with the symphonies, which deserve a slightly firmer treatment I prefer Otaka's cycle of Glazunov symphonies, or Jarvi's . However, Anissimiov makes the works sing, and in his balletic scores, you can't ask for much more. This disc with Glauznov's most famous work, The Seasons, is near ideal. Good tempo, color, and drive--though the sound could be a bit more vivid. Also nice to hear the rarely played Scenes de Ballet thrown in for good measure. You won't regret this purchase and it will make you think twice about relegating Glazunov to the "one-hit wonder" category. These scores, along with his 8 symphonies and numerous colorful orchestral works reveal a prominent minor master, heir to Rimsky's legacy, if not quite as masterful a symphonist as Tchaikovsky or Rachmaninov. This is definitely not a 1-star recording or performance!

First, thank you to those reviewers that have confidence in the product. Those are the reviews that got me to order this product, and save me nearly 1000 by not hiring a plumber to do the job. After reading dozens of varying reviews, I decided to order this product. I work as an auto tech so I have an idea of how a compression fitting, albeit this being a different style that what I'm used to, works. I had to run this gas line from the basement, through the attic, to a gas stove replacing an electric unit. I would say the most difficult part was uncoiling the pipe in the attic and avoiding kinking the pipe. It won't kink easily but when you're dealing with 75 ft it can be a challenge especially in the confines of an attic. I read the instructions multiple times, and installed the fittings. I turned the gas on and had no leaks with the 2 fittings I installed first time out. There are far too many negative reviews on this product to user error. My observation is that this is a fantastic product that is held back by limitations of the installer. The home depot looks to have stopped selling the product, which I'm guessing is due to improper installations by the consumer. It is unfortunate that the product is being criticized for a poor installation, not product quality issues. Definitely will be recommending this product to anyone that needs a gas line run for large and small projects.

I bought this keyboard because it was small, compact, and lightweight, but when I received the microKEY air 61, I realized it was TOO small, especially for gigging. The keys are very tiny and though touch sensitive, the semi-weighted quality is nowhere near an actual piano. I thought I could live with semi-weighted keys, but combined with the slimness of the keys, this proved to be difficult. Bluetooth connection is not a breeze in the park, either. Sometimes it takes multiple tries to get it to connect. On top of that, latency can also be an issue. I experienced some lag with the bluetooth connection. The product also comes with a code to access the Korg ios app, but you have to pay for the app which was a bummer. Now onto the good things. This keyboard is extremely portable and definitely works with a sustain pedal. It is a perfect and simple MIDI controller. I recommend this product to people who really like something to play recreationally on the go, but not to professional players. For more experienced pianists looking for a gigging or travel instrument, I suggest going for the 88 fully-weighted hammer action keys. Though the price is a little steeper, the authentic feel is definitely worth it. I ended up returning the keyboard, but I want to clarify that this a good product for a certain type of people it just wasn't what I was looking for.

This purchase was meant to be a present for an avid reader and fan of Agatha Christie who has lost her eyesight to cancer and is no longer able to read. I wanted to be sure of the quality of the recording, I believe it's a 1945 radio broadcast, before gifting. It's definitely close to a reasonable price for two stories at 6 or 7 on disc, though if you're in the position, paying 89 cents for each of the MP3 versions is much nicer. The quality isn't excellent, there's a bit of background noise clutter but the dialogue is certainly audible. I was a little worried the accents might make it unintelligible, but it's relatively easy to understand for me. For older audiences or those who are hard of hearing it might be more difficult, especially with the static in the background. If she enjoys this then I'll probably purchase the second and third in the series.

I have heard this album a hundred or more times, since 1979 ? and, am very well versed in the world of "Hi-Fi" while living in a world full of over-compressed music that fatigues the ears and, leaves very little depth to some music that, while still being incredible as it is, it lacks headroom and the warmth of that soft piano intro of "Death On Two Legs" and, since I do not have it on vinyl right now, this is the best next thing!!! I swear everytime I play this, it sounds like the vinyl in respects to the headroom AND the crisp audio - minus the crackles and pops. As with the Compact Disc's warning in the early days of digital High Resolution Pure Audio may reveal limitations of the source tape so, don't take it personal. Blu-Ray Pure Audio Discs are the best thing to happen to audio since, well... Hi Fidelity Long Playing Records were introduced to Turntables that, not your average person can afford and, require you to remove the platter to change speeds. This album has never sounded better, from "The Prophet Song" to the intricacies and flaws of "Bohemian Rhapsody" right down to the passion of "Love of My Life" I LONG TO HEAR MORE ALBUMS ON BLU-RAY PURE AUDIO! They are even better than DVD-Audio in that, while you might get an album in 2.0 at 96khz, it is too hard to do 5.1 at 96khz, which is why most DVD-Audio is at 48khz 5.1 if it can do even that. Blu-Ray has the capacity to hold 50gig on a double layer disc. With that said, there is the possibility to do 7.1 at 196khz! The sound is beyond crystal clear, the massive headroom is there... THIS is how music SHOULD be heard It blows away 24kt Gold CD's, SACD's, DSD's, HDCD... all of it. Blu-Ray Pure Audio is a format I hope is around for a long time to come. Now... PLEASE tell me there will be plenty of new releases in 2014!

Sunshade 'n Rain, DON'T STOP THE MUSIC, Sound Column Productions SCP-02790. Lp is a Utah private press Lp, dates 1979. Track list side one-- Dancin' School Melody Don't Stop the Music I'll Be The Love Place in Time side two-- First You Cry Nancy Drew No Tomorrow Coeur D'Alene Somewhere In Life Frankly put, this is the BEST produced local kitsch pop music album I've found in the parallel universe record market in the 'Empire. The sound on this private press Lp grooves, doesn't sound dated or done on the cheap, and the overall feel puts this solidly in the late '70s pop A C by Gerry Rafferty, Styx, Christopher Cross, and the Top-40 pop before '80s synth pop bulldozed out the sound. Three tracks are disco-ed "Dancin' School," "Don't Stop the Music," and "First You Cry." There's a lot of keyboard sound, which is professionally-sounding, not the usual cheap backwards Casio feel of local music the group's vocals are very well polished, a bit processed, but though one hears a throwback to the Lettermen, it's far more contemporary and shows a great deal of attention went into recording. The balance of the other tracks fall more into the late '70s A C ballad pop of the era, but this album is far and away MILES AHEAD of the usual whitebread feel of local music that thought that the Carpenters were filthy commie rock paisley-wearing hippies who were at least suspect down in Happy Valley and Stepford, Utah. The Skousen music department folks down 'south would be rolling over in their graves if they heard this Lp. All the tracks on this Lp are originals, not bleached and sanitized covers for the local market. My copy still has the 6.98 price sticker from Deseret Book, hence the parallel universe distribution retail stocking meaning not sold in regular record stores, just like pre-'85 CCM . The second side runs out of steam a bit on the last two tracks, "Coeur D'Alene," and especially the trite "Somewhere In Life," which is over-the-top preachy. Like other period CCM more or less based on the same vanilla treatment of popular youth tastes, the songwriting on the album is weak "Nancy Drew"??? , leaving no impression on the listener cotton candy lyrics , but it works on this fluff fun pop album--Donna Summer's overt dance floor pickup aggression would be too much for the local audience this album courts. Songwriting credits show "Dancin' School" -- John Allen-Tom McFarland "Melody" -- Steve Kay "Don't Stop the Music" -- Rich Christensen-Clive Romney "I'll Be Love" -- Steve Kay "Place In Time" -- Todd Oldroyd-Phil Secrist "First You Cry" -- Clive Romney "Nancy Drew" -- Ron Simpson "No Tomorrow" -- D. Lindstrom-C. Romney-R. Simpson "Coeur D'Alene" -- Ron Simpson "Somewhere In Life" -- Ron Simpson-Arnold Stringham All selections published by Ronarte Publications ASCAP . Recorded by Jim Anglesey at Sound Column Studios in Salt Lake City and Stan Dacus at Young 'Un Sound in Nashville. Er, Nashville - disco. Um.... Sunshade 'n Rain is Dan Lindstrom, Jeff Gregerson, and Mel Teeples. This Lp is their fourth album. Two other albums that I know about by the group SUNSHADE 'N RAIN II, SCP 7347. Songs include "Listen to the Music" Doobie Bros. late '72 hit and "I Believe in Music" Gallery, also late 1972 . Lp most likely dates about 1973 into 1974. --Sunshade 'n Rain recorded a third album, NATURALLY, SS R 1003, nbsp Naturally nbsp at Sound Column Productions, c. 1975. Songs on the Lp are side one Naturally Please Turn Your Heart My World Printer I've Changed side two Where Would I Be Anna Lee I Could Never Love You More Harmony My Friend. All the songs on SS R 1003 show songwriting credit to members of the group, especially Dan Lindstrom. FIVE, yes, FIVE incredibly enthusiastic caramel-dipped onions!

The performance of Griegs Peer Gynt Suites and Sibelius Pelleas et Melisande are excellently performed here by Herbert von Karajan and the Berlin Philharmonic Orchestra BPO . The very familiar opening lines of Peer Gynt Suite 1 Morning Mood come through loud and clear as played by the BPO, and numerous familiar themes appear throughout the album. I consider the sound quality of the album to be superb, and the expressive quality of the BPO is also at its best. When all is said and done, I would highly recommend this album as an excellent depiction of the sound-pictures captured in Griegs Peer Gynt Suites and Sibelius Pelleas et Melisande.

If there was any way at all I could give this less then 1 star, I would. I am a professional performer, gigging over 200 dates per year for audiences of all sizes. Several of our gigs are very intimate and our main PA system consists of 2 QSC 10 powered speakers and a QSC Touchmix digital mixer. I was looking for something easier to carry, with less trips to the car. Last but not least, I was looking for something that was crisp at low volumes and had wider coverage. Several people I know have the competitors to this and they all lacked some functions I felt they should have. So when the Mackie Reach showed up with the feature set it has, I was blown away. I ordered this not expecting to be wow'ed, but just to get the job done. I am part of an acoustic duo, nothing fancy, very straight forward. I thought this could be an answer to all the gear hauling. Mine arrived today. The controls were straight forward enough, I took it to a small gig about 50 people we made all the proper connections, attempted a sound check and almost instantly I knew a huge mistake had been made. We struggled through the first set 1 hour doing our best to make small adjustments in between songs. It was brutal, it sounded worse then one of those all in one Karaoke machines. You know the ones with the one speaker that makes you sound like you're singing into a mud puddle? On the break I downloaded the app, thinking with the added functionality I'd be able to correct the problem. In the meantime I paired my phone with the Reach. I began to stream my album from Spotify, even switched the setting to DJ and yes, it sounded the same.. Muffled poop not the word I want to say . The second set was also a struggle, and I did everything I could to make it work, to no avail. If only 1 of us was playing, it was at least tolerable to get through a song, but barely. When both of us would play and sing it would turn into a muddled mess, of incoherent babble. One big wall of distortion and crappy sound. No matter how it was adjusted, it sounded like it was overdriven, overloaded and overworked. We ended set 2 early and setup our regular PA rig, except we only used QSC speaker and our two personal monitors. We powered up, only using half our normal rig and instantly felt all warm and fuzzy inside.. "Why the hell were we trying to get better then this?" my partner asked.. The only two things this Mackie Reach was good for. 1. Making me realize how good our current setup is. 2. Holding the elevator door open at the end of the night so I could load the rest of my gear. I will be returning this as soon as the sun rises tomorrow morning. I haven't been this disappointed in a product since I was a kid, and my pet rock died. You would be better off singing through an orange construction cone while playing a Barbie guitar then using the Mackie Reach. I couldn't even recommend this for a backyard iPod barbecue bluetooth system, you'd be better off with a small rechargeable bluetooth speaker, at least you'd be able to hear what music was playing.

First impressions don't look the greatest right now. I unboxed this item about 7 hours ago and it will be going right back in the box depending upon what tech supports says tomorrow it's now 3 00am . After calibrating the print bed, installing the software, and registering everything inclusive of both firmware and software updates , I attempted to calibrate the scan feature. First, it took multiple attempts of uninstalling and re-installing the XYZscan software before it even recognized that the thing was connected via USB on Windows10 The separate XYZware Pro software was recognizing it, updating registering it with identified serial number, and declared that it was ready to start printing . After finally getting past every scan option being grayed out and it finally letting me select scan, I chose to "calibrate now." According to the instructions and tutorials, once you press calibrate, it should move the print bed upward and rotate the scan carousel, at which time you should be prompted to insert the calibration grid. Nothing is working as it should. Once I pressed calibrate now, it moved the print bed up and immediately went into calibration mode without an opportunity to insert the calibration grid. Inserting it before I selected calibration only resulted in Service code 5001 "calibration failure" or service code 5022 "left scanning laser error". If I try to scan anything, it goes through the process of scanning with nothing more than a blank screen as the result. I'm not a fan to say the least and this doesn't even begin to address the other major issue I have.

I like these boxes. I like being able to change what they are on a moments whim. Two BIG problems. You are limited to the digital models of other great pedals. You have to use a funky proprietary cable that mates only to iPhones the old ones and the iTouch to change the box to something else. Oh, There is a third problem. You can only set it to ONE model at a time. Other than that, this is an awesome pedal. The models are pretty good. If you don't get all bent up about whether or not they sound and work just like the real pedal they are simulating. They probably don't. But they do sound pretty good. I now have five of these. That makes for a pretty good pedal board that I can change around whenever I want without tearing it apart. I think that was the plan all along. The down side is that I don't think these sold very well. The upside is that you can probably find them cheaper now. The downside is that the software controlling them will probably disappear sometime. UNLESS the maker releases a version that works on Bluetooth. In the Meantime, buy one, get the software now and download all of the patches and back them up somewhere safe. Once software is loaded, there is no reason to expect these won't work almost forever - or at least as long as any other pedal you own.

After having this system installed into a G L ASAT Classic about 5 weeks ago, I have come so far as to say I will never consider buy another electric guitar without this technology. This is a game-changer and I personally expect that within a decade people will stop wanting the noisy conventional pickup made with copper wire wrapped around magnets. I have a conventional guitar with dual passive humbuckers be Seymour Duncan--while they have a stellar sound, the technology of the humbuckers generates noise which I have to deal with with a noise gate. I don't need the noise gate to rob sustain on the Fishman Fluence SINGLE COILS of the Greg Koch Gristle Tone Set . That is unheard of. Single coils quieter than humbuckers? It just doesn't happen--until now. And like the earlier reviewer said, these pups are versatile--extremely versatile. I can do anything from the most classic "chicken pickin'" to getting Jimi Hendrix "Love or Confusion" out of the neck pup. I put together the G L ASAT and Fishman Fluence Greg Koch system for 700 combined. Within weeks I decided to sell mu 1500 2015 American Ash strat I bought new earlier this year. While the Shawbucker was great, the custom shop fat 50's single coils in the neck and mid were glaringly noisy and less capable of giving me the voicings I most wanted. Hook yourself up with this system and say good bye to limits and noise. Warning though--while these don't need any routing, they don't just go into a G L ASAT because wire lengths are the issue. Also, have a pro install them--either on a Fender or G L body because there is soldering and you can mess it up if your not careful. I burned my picking thumb by accidebtly touching the soldering element with mu thumb right above the soldering iron's handle. .

This is the first electric toothbrush I ever bought and I feel like I need to return the favor of all the people who wrote reviews and influenced my decision to buy this product. I love it and have also written reviews for the replacement Deep Sweep heads I ordered. I'd suggest going to those products on Amazon for more toothbrush-head-specific reviews. I love that there are a variety of toothbrush heads that will fit the battery case but I've been faithful to my Deep Sweep brush heads even after nuking them in the microwave. Why buy a pricey Sonicare when I can have a great investment with a functional design? I'll explain... For some reason I made the connection in my head that the toothbrush head could be disinfected by a quick rotation in the microwave because that's what I've learned to do with Clairisonic brush heads for my Mia 2. I was thoroughly disappointed when the toothbrush head no longer fit to the battery case. Why? Because the brush looked fine on the outside but I realized something on the inside must have shifted after it's minor radiation. Since I loved this toothbrush so much I couldn't imagine waiting for new brush heads to arrive. Well somehow I managed to hold the toothbrush head close enough to the battery case and was able to get the head vibrating. Wahlah! And that wasn't just luck. I begrudgingly used it like that for a while until I bought a replacement pack of nbsp 3 Braun Professional Deep Sweep EB30-3 nbsp heads. Thanks to Ama. Prime it came to me in the next few days and now it works like usual deeply cleaning my teeth with a minimal amount of effort needed. I will never use a regular brush again. Well, unless this toothbrush is low on battery. I'd say you can get about 3 or 4 brushings in before your battery case needs to be fully recharged. You'll get a heads up when the battery is dying from a little green light that blinks on the brush. It'll also blink while it's charging. So I just leave it on it's little charger all the time and it's always ready to go. The charging cord is light, long, and easy to move around. Overall this toothbrush is practical, sleek, and incredibly effective. The brush is "loud" but you're only using it for 2 minutes so I don't see it as a nuisance. As always, read the instruction manual so you get the most out of this brush. All you have to do is slowly drag the brush across your teeth and it'll do the hard work for you. I've noticed a whiteness and indescribable level of clean on my teeth since using this product. My top tips Don't microwave it and only press the power button when the brush is in your mouth.

Overall decent quality set but the 10 ml cylinder is not accurate at all. The rest of this set is a decent quality and accurate. They are sturdy and clearly marked. I tested them for accuracy by comparing their volume with a quality glass cylinder as well as by weighing the water at various volumes. Both of my 10 ml cylinders are way off. At the 10 ml mark they actually only contain 8.4 ml 8.4 grams 20 C . This is far from acceptable to me. I can't think of anything I'd measure that allows for that much variation. I could eyeball in a spoon just as accurately. Let's think of an antibiotic for example with 125 mg 5 ml to be taken 3 times a day for a week. That is a total of 2625 mg over a week, but if measured in this cylinder you would get only 2205 mg which would be the equivalent of missing almost 3.5 doses over that week. I wouldn't use these smaller cylinders for anything I need an accurate measurement for such as medicine, baking, mixing pesticides, or various other chemistry related home projects such as soapmaking, brewing, making ecig liquids etc. The other cylinders were much more accurate and serviceable. I liked the idea of having 2 of each size so that I could use one set in the kitchen for things to be ingested, as well as one in the garage for non consumable things such as pesticides and household chemicals. Which btw if you do this you should clearly label them so you don't get them confused. I've put a band of red tape with black XXX's on it for my garage set. If the small cylinders were accurate I'd love this set. As it is, consider it a 4 pc. cylinder set and value it accordingly. Disclaimer, I received a promotional discount in exchange for my honest review.

This is the third guitar rest I have purchased since I discovered the comfort stress reduction advantages of sitting with both feet flat on the floor and letting a guitar support hold the guitar in a good playing position. The single suction cup of this unit allows the guitar position to be set with neck up angle, some rotation of the guitar around the neck axis and a good bit of variation in placement on the leg I use it on my left leg exclusively and play right handed I like the flexibility in positioning a lot. The suction cup has an "application lever" that drives the suction assembly tighter when a final position is decided. It holds well for hours but will eventually release when the guitar is placed on a stand or hanger for longer periods. I suspect that may be because the units very large suction cup 2.125 inch diameter will always encounter much more guitar curvature variation that the smaller cups that are typical of such supports. That is not a reason to disqualify this unit, it just means that repeating your "perfect" positioning will take a bit of time at each use.

That may have been the intention, but in reality this recording is a showcase for the skills of the great jazz bass player Ray Brown. Believe me, it's Brown whose notes you should pay attention to on this CD of the five pianists, only Benny Green matches Brown in musical expressiveness and originality. The others play wonderfully, in their own bags, but only Green interacts with every phrase and every chord from the bass. It helps, of course, that Green is by far the most technically accomplished of the pianists included. In 1995, when this session was recorded, Green was the new kid on the block, just beginning his fairly long partnership with Ray Brown. Oscar Peterson, on the other end of things, had partnered with Brown a lot over the years their feeling of ensemble is also strong on this CD, but Peterson was playing from their past, while Green was playing toward their future. The "Some of My Best Friends ..." series also featured Brown with saxophonists, trumpeters, and singers in exactly that order of interest IMHO . It was a ploy, if the truth be known, to sell recordings by Ray Brown, since bassists may be acclaimed musicians without becoming money cows for the studios. This CD is the best of the series. Of the five pianists paired with Brown, the truth is that he'd never performed or jammed with three. Ahmad Jamal was a 'star' by 1995, and an obvious choice. Dado Moroni was a promising "European Jazz" performer trying to find a footing in the USA. Geoff Keezer was a newbie, thrilled to his fingerprints to get the call. Classicists out there! Pay attention to the lead bass line on the first track, Bags' Groove. What you'll hear is a very subtle quasi-inversion of the theme that Jamal will soon make obvious. That's the sort of interesting musical substance you'll hear throughout this CD if you know how to listen for it. Benny Green, by the way, is now performing regularly with clarinet sax-player Anat Cohen. After a long stagnation, in my opinion, Green has become an awakened giant at the keyboard, playing stuff that's fiery new.

Artist Alain Rozan the Reprobates Album Boogie Woogie Review by Matthew Warnock French born, and current New York resident, Alain Rozan teams up with the band the Reprobates for his latest release Boogie Woogie. Though there are elements of blues and boogie woogie sprinkled throughout the album, the album goes beyond a single genre as it expands into both French and American folk music, rock, pop and other genres in a blend that is both unique and highly personal. Rozan's musical background is rich and diverse, and he brings all of these different streams together into a concentrated focus that is at once diverse and held together at the same time. There are moments when Rozan and company reach back to 60s America in songs like "Too Big to Bop," which even has shades of 50's vocal groups mixed in with its early rock and surf harmony and rhythms. The guitar tone on the lead lines brings to mind the great California bands of the 60s, with their energetic phrases and Stratocaster tone that has now become a staple of the rock guitar world. Rozan also takes a dive into the American South with his track "N'Awlins Two Steps." Here, the singer and songwriter pays tribute to the great city of New Orleans, with the accordion groove provided an Acadian backdrop for the guitar riff that permeates the background through the song's verse sections. With a deep knowledge and love of classic American rock and roll, from different regions of this vast country, Rozan is able to write in a historical style, but keep things diverse at the same time as he moves between the different sub-genres that made up early rock and roll. On "Le Banc de Pierre," Rozan returns to his native French as he sings the song's narrative, mixing the lyrics with a quasi-Reggae groove that is laid down by the guitar. To differentiate the groove from a strict Jamaican feel, there is a double-timed percussion part that weaves its way through the verse sections of the song, providing a personal touch to the underlying Reggae groove of the song. Rozan also breaks things up with the instrumental song "Raisin' Heaven in Hell," where the guitar and accordion take center stage during this short but entertaining tune. By using an instrumental to break things up musically, Rozan is allowing his audience to take a bit of a side-trip into another side of his artistic output, while at the same time keeping the groove and harmonic-melodic content in line with the rest of the album. Though there are a lot of strong moments on the album, in particular the grooves, accordion and guitar lines, there are moments when the melody line becomes a bit predictable and drags on the overall success of a few of the songs on the record. "New York City" and "Real American" are both interesting songs from a rhythmic and harmonic perspective, but the melody line becomes a bit stale during the course of the song. With eleven songs on the album, having a couple of songs with quasi-stagnant melody lines isn't a huge deal, just something that Rozan might think about for his next project. Both songs have interesting lyrics, and with a few tweaks and turns with the melody they could both be raised up from good songs to great songs. Overall, Boogie Woogie is a musically diverse album that leads the listener down different musical paths while keeping them locked together with Rozan's artistic personality and musicianship. Sometimes albums with so many genres being weaved into the songs can become loose and lose its direction, but this is not one of those cases as the musicianship of the band and Rozan keep things locked together and moving forward. Review by Matthew Warnock Rating 3.5 Stars out of 5

Shark, where have you been all my life? I own 2 dogs, a Chocolate Lab Pitt mix and a Norwegian Elk Hound. The Elk Hound starts shedding December 1st and stops shedding November 30th...with a heavy period around May June. I have to vacuum every night and sometimes twice. That's a lot of fur. The Shark NV-752 is fantastic. The power head picks up a layer of fur almost like it would pick up dry breakfast cereal. Almost. Still impressive and only having to make 1 or 2 passes is a dramatic improvement over my old Dyson when picking up fur. The power head also feels like it pulls both ways. Well it definitely pulls forward and the backdraw is very easy. The canister easily detaches for hard to reach areas as well as for dirt disposal. The canister release button feels very solid and is easy to engage. The headlights so you have instant gratification checking your work are adequate brightness. I don't find myself wishing they were brighter. Power on off and carpet floor controls are conveniently located in front of the hand grip. Dis-assembly for cleaning and maintenance of all the filters is pretty straightforward but I recommend watching the video tutorial. This unit is much easier to use and clean maintain than any of my previous vacuums. This Shark is a quality product at a comparable reasonable price. Very happy with my purchase.

I bought this little beauty as a super handy, lightweight practice amp I can just throw in the car and go. What I discovered is an amp that sounds way better than it has any right to for this cheap a price I paid around 280 new on eBay . Sure, it's not as bulletproof as bigger amps so you have to treat it carefully. The stock speaker is harsh and overly bright, but it actually cuts through a band amazingly well for it's size if you just want a raunchy rock metal amp. I enjoy a much more upscale overdrive sound ala Robben Ford my other amp is a Fuchs Overdrive Supreme! . I ran the Vox headphone output into the effects return of the Fuchs one day, and I couldn't believe how nice it sounded! The first few models on the dial bring up some very sweet sounding clean tones and some delicious overdriven Fenders - the Super Reverb overdriven is a standout. The AC 30s are both awesome, both clean and overdriven. I'm not a metal fan, but some of the really heavy super distortion tones are actually a lot of fun. And all those effects, while not having a lot of flexibility, are easy to use and sound very good. I eventually stuck an Eminence Ramrod speaker in this baby, which really tames the harsher digital overtones of the amp and makes it really sweet. Another nice thing is the Ramrod is much more efficient than the stock speaker, and is almost as loud, even at 8 ohms 15 watts the stock speaker, as per the owner's manual, is 4, yes four, ohms, which pushes the amp to 30 watts . It's plenty loud with just the Ramrod for rock band practice, but for fun, I went ahead and added an external speaker jack I am a bit of a tinkerer , so now I can hook up an additional, hi-quality 8 ohm speaker cabinet to use all 30 watts, providing more clean headroom and a serious bottom end. This thing now makes an awesome practice amp and a very capable and very portable backup amp for gigs! I have used it running it into the Fuchs amp as stated above for heavier rock gigs, and it kicks butt! I am always amazed at how much fun I have using this amp.

I'm a surround sound aficionado. Tales From Topographic Oceans is one of those albums that was made for surround sound. There was always a bit too much musical information for 2 speakers, imho. The 2016 5.1 mix is outstanding, and completely does this complex album justice. Easily one of Steven Wilson's best mixes. That said, I accidentally called up the Original Mix in 192 khz stereo and my ears almost fell off! The clarity was astounding! I want to contrast this Ranco ETC-111000 with the Lux nbsp WIN100 Heating Cooling Programmable Outlet Thermostat . WIRING PROJECT This Ranco can only be installed by someone familiar with electrical wiring. It does not come with any power input cord or any plug socket to get power out. I cut an extension cord in two and wired the two cut ends through a cable clamp to the Ranco. So I had a plug to plug into the wall and a socket to plug my heater into. But if you're not familiar with the hot, neutral, and ground wires of a power cord, you should not attempt it. Also, it took me an hour to connect the cords. If you are familiar with electrical wiring, the wiring diagram is clear. I found the ETC-111000-000W on the internet that is prewired with a plug and socket. The Lux is ready to plug into the wall right out of the box. TEMPERATURE RANGE The Ranco's temperature set point range is -30F to 220F. Settable differential range 1F to 30F. The Lux temperature set point range is 45F to 90F. Differential swing default is 1F. Can be set from 1F to 9F. TIME FUNCTIONS The Ranco has no time functions. I tested for a delay in cool mode between turn off and turn on so the pressure on the AC compressor can go down. After the Ranco turned off, I got it to turn on again in 13 seconds by warming the probe in my hand. The Lux can have 4 different temperature settings each day, and the weekends can be different than the weekdays. Or, it can be set to one temperature all the time. The Lux waits 2 minutes on cool between turn off and turn on so the pressure on the AC compressor can go down. REMOTE TEMPERATURE PROBE The Ranco comes with an 8ft cable on the probe which can be lengthened to 400ft by splicing in more cable. The Lux comes with a 1 inch cable on the probe. VOLTAGE The Ranco works on 120 or 208 240 volts. The Lux works on 120 volts with a max load of 15 amps. IN CASE OF ERROR I want to illustrate the danger if a mistake is made in setting the heat cool mode or if someone plays with the settings. I put 2 100w light bulbs in the lower oven drawer of my oven. I set the Ranco to 75F. Then I made one error I set the Ranco to cool. It heated up to 175F. I wanted to see what happens at the Ranco limit of 220F, so I boosted the heat with the oven's heater. When the temperature passed 220F, the Ranco turned off and displayed "EP". As the temperature dropped, the Ranco displayed 220 and turned on again. The power strip that I plugged the two 100w lights into was warped by the heat and can't be used anymore. Ranco has addressed this problem by providing a keypad lockout switch inside the enclosure. When in locked position, the keypad is disabled. Also, Ranco warns "All ETC series controls are designed as operating controls only. If an operating control failure could result in personal injury or loss of property, a separate safety control and or alarm should be installed." I did the same test with the Lux using the Ranco probe to read the temperature because the entire Lux device was in the oven. I stopped the test at 115F. The Lux displayed "HI" but was still supplying power to the light bulbs. I have no children or anyone in my house who would touch these thermostats. If I connect them to a heater, I use the low power range and use the thermostat on the heater as a backup to prevent over heating. When I connect a thermostat, I watch it go through one on-off cycle to see if it is operating correctly.

Bought two pairs and used two times so far for shows. Out of the box, they look and feel great, and come in a nifty little container. The first time, they were used in a smaller venue and worked great, filtering out extra ambient noise while maintaining a good range of sound. The second show they were used at was at a much larger venue, however, and I noticed that it filtered a bit more of the high than low tones, and made it impossible to hear the same crispness of sound, instead compressing it to the point where only the bass qualities could be heard and making the music sound flat. At some point during the second use, the rubber had pulled off of one of the buds and gotten stuck in my ear when I tried to remove it, requiring tweezers, a flashlight, and a second person to remove. They worked great while they did, but after the rubber had pulled off it became a pain to use. The problem has only occurred with one set so far, but the way that the rubber came off, I wouldn't be surprised if it happened to the other set soon as well. It seems like it was only held in place by a bit of glue, and while it could be an easy fix with some superglue, I would have much rather avoided the entire ordeal of losing the rubber piece in my ear. Overall, 3 5 because they worked well while they did, but the overall craftsmanship could have done with a bit more.

Like many others, I had previewed this album expecting the worst. I am by no means a metal officianado, but I do like some good sleezy rock roll. Sure the styles and the videos are cringe-worthy. But the 90's brought on a wave of depressing music that just made everyone wanna mope about how rotten life is. I'd rather crank up the volume and celebrate the great things in life when I feel down. There was a great wave of 80's bands that got that.. Twisted Sister, Quiet Riot, early Motley Crue, early Poison, and certainly not the least Ratt. When I hear "Round and Round" or "Lay It Down" or "You're In Love", the adrenaline spikes, and I want to turn it up and drive faster. Sadly most of those bands took a turn for the serious, or listened when the industry people said "we need a ballad.. that'll get the fans", and eventually each band one by one got frustrated at the world and each other and imploded. Worse yet, many came back in the 90s as a mere shell of what they were, or worse yet, an unplugged style of their former selves. I heard Stephen Pearcy sing an acoustic "Round and Round" a couple years back on Sirius 80's Martha Quinn show. I knew that was the end... ever. So I put this new album on. I hadn't even realized it was the classic Ratt. I figured like most bands it was a 3rd or 4th generation cover band singer, one original guitarist, maybe one other member, and probably a d-list member of a lesser known metal band. Track 1.. Eat Me Up Alive. WTF??? WTFF? Did they accidentally put in a lost 80s album on this cd instead? Without sounding like a carbon copy of their past hits, they get the feel right of tghe Out Of The Cellar days. The riffs! The solos! The hard rock harmonies! The clashy smashy drums played with wreckless abandon! I seriously did a double take. Stephen sounds great! I expected a torn up battered voice.. but surprising he sounds like he did way back when.. torn up and battered.. but not in that way that only time does to a voice, but instead the way he is supposed to sound. "Best Of Me".. this is up there with the best of their riffy chorusy singalong, headbang-along hits. It's the sound of the whole band running on all four wheels at full throttle. The whole album follows suit and never really loses momentum. "Take a Big Bite", "Last Call", "A Little Too Much".. you know what kind of time you're gonna get when you read the titles. Only one song "Take Me Home" slows down the pace a little.. but not in that lighter-waving "Monster ballads" way. This is easily a cd I will be playing straight through start to finish rather than cutting to a few choice tracks. So I now own this and my well-worn copy of "Ratt Roll 8191". If I put them together on shuffle, other than the sound quality of the older stuff, they would blend in easily at first glance. Thank you Ratt for bringing some trashy back to this way too polished rock scene!

Has three knobs, I'll call them volume, squashing and grabbing. The attack knob grabs your signal. You can have it grab the signal early or late. Then the sensitivity compresses your sound, essentially making the quiet notes louder, and the louder notes quieter. Depending on how sensitive you make it, it makes your signal smoother and thicker with more perceived sustain. And then there's volume, you all know what that means right? For my use, rock, country, punk, blues and jazz, this is all I need. Sure it adds a little hiss, but if you do some research you'll understand why this is expected. As compressor pedals go, this one is pretty quiet, and I perceive no added distortion. Thumbs up MXR, you did a good job. Let me note, that this is a subtle effect, but that's the way compression is. If your looking for something less subtle pick a different pedal.

Update - Very Unimpressed. Now - I am not happy because this crap jammed in my extrude.. My Tevo Tornado hates this stuff I'm basing the review on he print quality and end product properties against typical expectations for PLA. Most PLA I have used is import, this is the first USA PLA and my expectations were high. Flow is gooey but surprisingly. Not impressed with the binding. Tried many temperatures and while it will bind, its not so great as other PLA filaments. In temperature controlled cabinet, layer binds were not the strongest, and ripply. The prints will split along layers with force. More heat only deforms rather than binds. I found this a softer, PLA that lower shear durability than desired. I got the Natural Clear 1.75mm and is barely opaque. Its very white. It has the texture, feel and transparency that of a gallon milk jug. Its durability is about that of the same plastic at 0C 32F - brittle. On the good side, no clogs and only one filament snap. The spool was fresh, well wound and packed. I really wished I liked this better, but alas the formula is yet to be perfected by the team at 3D Solutech. Its not a bad product Natural Transparent but for the same effect I would choose a ABS or Nylon because this particular PLA is not very tough, rigid or easy to print. Recommend calling this "Opaque White" rather than "Natural Clear".

This device is a great addition to my practice routine. The BERP is much better than simply buzzing on a mouthpiece alone for two reasons. One is that the resistance can be varied by sliding the end piece to different spots. The BERP's resistance is more stable than simply buzzing a mouthpiece while holding one's little finger over the end of the mouthpiece to add resistance. The second reason is that by fastening the BERP onto the leadpipe of the horn I can finger the valves as I buzz. I am former professional trombone player, now a skilled amatuer french horn player. I often play through an etude on the horn to be sure of the notes, then buzz through the same etude with the BERP, fingering the notes on the horn as I buzz. Then I usually play through the same etude again on the horn. My sound is always bigger than when I play the etude on the horn the second time. Probably this is because buzzing requires such a big and efficient airstream to get a good sound. One limitation is that the low range is overly difficult when buzzing on the BERP, so I find the device less useful when working on low passages, though part of this problem is surely due to my still underdeveloped embouchure for french horn notes in the low range. I also bought the trumpet version of the BERP for my thirteen-year old son. He has used it on occasion and it does seem to help improve his sound.

Bought this case for a 1925 Martin Handcraft tenor sax. This is in my opinion an excellent case for vintage horns with split bells. The construction is sturdy with wooden walls covered with 1680 gauge ballistic Nylon exterior secured with screws not rivets and well padded interior. More details under PT cases website. Our sax does not have a large horn, so there is a lot of room left in front of it in the case, but it is a good fit for the split keys. The neck of our sax has a brace which prevents it from properly fitting in the compartment, but it is no problem to lock in place. The tall bow bumper makes it fit snuggly without a neck guard. I bought this case for my daughter who plays in school band and thus needs to move around with it understanding that vintage horns are heavier , so I purchased the optional backpack strap since it was inexpensive, but after trying it, she opted for the shoulder strap which is included. The outside pockets make up for the lack of storage space inside. Overall, I am very please with the case and its price.

Biggest problem with pH Test Strips is that they arrived with no instructions. There is a color panel on the pack which one can assume one compares to the strip used to measure PH. There are also 4 colors in each column on the panel printed on the packet. I presume that each color when matched to a corresponding color on the packet should indicate the presence or absence of something. But the packet without instructions leaves one absolutely clueless about what the results indicate for the different colors. This appears to be a largely useless product without instructions about how to interpret since there is no one column of color configurations that is a perfect match for all of the colors when one uses a strip to test PH. And there is no clue as to what a match for a color on each row of colors might mean in terms of chemistry of fluid measured. I could change my evaluation of the product if provided with instructions and could then understand what I am looking at after using a strip.

I just recently bought two 57's from two different Amazon dealers at a very cheap price of 70. After testing these microphones with other Shure 57's and having a couple of expert local sound technicians spec-out the microphones, we discovered that these microphones were total counterfeits. True Shure microphones are typically very high quality microphones when they are direct from the Shure factory. If you go for the deal, check your microphone as soon as it arrives. If there are problems with your high-end being very sensitive and feeding-back and you have no low-end, it's probably a counterfeit. Shure 57's are pretty well balanced across the board with very little adjustments on your EQ. Go ahead, pay a higher price and buy them from your local music dealer or from an Internet store that you trust. There are counterfeits out there...be careful!!! I normally rate genuine Shure 57 Microphones as 5-stars.

I purchased this from Guitar Center, but wanted to share my review with the Amazon community After taking a 20 year hiatus from playing piano, I decided to take it back up at 35 years old. I didn't want to spend a fortune on a more reputable name brand like Yamaha, but after doing some extensive due diligence, I decided on the Overture 2 from Williams. Despite the slightly higher-than-average occurrence of negative reviews, I decided it was a nice mid-range digital piano, so I purchased it. First and foremost, be sure you have someone to assist you with the assembly. This is a very heavy piano, and although I was able to finally get it together by myself, it would've been much easier if I had someone to assist me. Other than that, the assembly is relatively straightforward, though the directions are not great, and the parts come all together in one bag. I shouldn't have to sit there and figure out the difference between an M4x20 screw and an M6x16 screw I figured it out, but it was an added hassle that could've been avoided with clearer instructions and better packaging . The holes drilled in the modesty panel were too big, and the screws wouldn't hold it in place. Plus, the washers that the provided were too big for the screws, so they didn't help keep the screw secured. Ultimately, I had to use large binder clips on the bottom to hold it in place... a rather ghetto option for something that costs several hundred dollars. As for the piano's performance, it's... meh. The sound is realistic enough, but the action on the keys is TERRIBLE. I know I've been away from the piano for two decades, but I remember enough that I know you shouldn't have to literally SLAM down on the key to get louder action. The pedals seem to work fine and as expected. But the poor action on the keys themselves are a major disappointment. The bottom line if you're JUST starting out with piano, it'll suffice. But once you get into actually playing regular songs, you're probably going to want to invest in a better model. Personally, I'll probably use it for a year or so, just to get myself back "in the groove" with playing, then I'll save up for a real piano. This product has BARELY earned 3 stars in my opinion. Hope this helps.

The screen is small, obviously, so resolution is not that of a larger scope. I bought it to trace the signals through an electronic ignition system, not the spark. I found that this little scope did everything I wanted. I strongly advise any purchaser to pick up a couple of extra probes because the unit has two independent input channels and one output channel. Once I determined that the magnetic pickups were getting a reasonable signal strength I could simulate the input and trouble shoot on the bench. Again this is a great little tool at a very reasonable price.

Like many I absolutely loved the previous album 'Born on flag day' Born On Flag Day nbsp and so was really looking forward to this one. It starts off reasonably well with 'Choir of Angels' being almost reminiscent of Crying Shame from their first release War Elephant', but it does go a bit maudlin on track 3 'Goodbye Dear Friend', which does exactly what it says on the tin. The same could also be levelled at Mange', but the strangest thing is a reprise of 'Christ Jesus' which was on 'War Elephant' and seems to run counter to the general up beatness that we have come to expect from John Joseph McCauley III and co. Can not think of a reason for getting the import other than the UK release unless I clicked on the wrong one so a big oops for me. That said 'When she comes home' is just brilliant and I don't think I can ever tire of hearing it. As has been commented elsewhere they are a force to be reckoned with live, even with a power failure as happened at End Of The Road Festival in 2010 their second of three sets and they joined the wonderful Phosphorescent on stage who kindly repaid the compliment to them on backing vocals. Whilst it is not a real change in direction it is a change of pace but I feel they are still cutting their teeth and I firmly feel that they have better to come hence not 5 stars but would suggest getting Born on Flag Day first and if that grabs you then you would be twattish not to indulge in this one.

I cannot say enough good things about this incubator. I bought two of these for my grandma. It is very easy to use. I set it at 37.5 temp, 46 humidity for chicken eggs the first 1 though 18 days of incubation, then 56 for the three days of lock down. Hatch rates are 75 keep in mind that we take out the ones that don't develop and some do not pip or hatch-this is the nature of chicken eggs . You should read the manual. It does have a light that shows up when the water is low. I leave this incubator at my grandmas and she checks the water. The only downfall is that the water float a little round ball sticks and one time it did not float to the top and my grandma overfilled it, causing the excess water to spill into the incubator, causing the humidity to go to 70 percent and by the time I caught it the eggs had stopped developing. It has an automatic turner. It says it will hold 20 eggs, but I can only fit 18 Rhode Island Red chicken eggs in there. I have used this incubator to hatch shipped eggs and local eggs. We are currently using it!!!!! You can really set it and forget it aside from checking the water.

I bought this item and the first time I tried it I was at a friends house and one of the mic's didn't work. I didn't have the time to investigate the problem right then since I have another mic that I use almost all of the time when I by myself practicing. By the time I wanted to take it somewhere where I really needed the dual mic's it still didn't work and my time had passed to return it. My own fault. I took it apart and discovered both of the wires was broken that connected them to the circuit board so I re soldered them and it still didn't work. I thought maybe I reversed the wires so I took the other one apart to see how the wires were connected and one of the wires broke when i took it apart. I re soldered that one and it worked but still can't get the other to work. These mic's are obviously very cheaply made. I have to admit I do like the sound. The other draw back is it only comes with one cable so you are stuck using them with only one volume controlling both mic's or buy another cable. I couldn't find the cable I needed but wound up not needing it since only one worked. Now I need to buy another dual mike system and it won't be a Voco Pro. A couple of hundred bucks down the drain. I guess you get what you pay for sometimes . I tried calling support but was put on hold for quite sometime so I hung up.

I know...I could strip the insulation off the wire and terminate it directly under the terminal screw but using a ring or fork lug keeps the wires from wiskering out from under the terminal screw. The relay is of common quality and is being used to power a 13.1 cu. ft. Refrigerator from shore power and when shore power is not available it the relay changes states and allows me to power the fridge from a DC to AC inverter without back feeding into the shore power. So I bought this amPlug so that I would not 'annoy' my fiance while she is doing her homework.
