Not only is the tone very nice, I love the delay, which is very 'analogy' and natural. The crunch from the gain control produces a very nice 'dirty' vox feel. It runs on two AAA batteries that are expected to last for 10 hours. This amPlug is ideal for people who 1 Get the desire to play in the middle of the night now you won't wake your neighbors, family or etc 2 Want to play guitar at work but don't to lug the amp it is quite light and easy to 'take and go' 3 Enjoy taking their music where ever they go. Buy this Vox amPlug or one more tailored to your needs e.g. the traditional ac30, the vox metal, vox acoustic, vox lead , and you will not be disappointed. These make perfect gifts for the musicians in your family I plan on buying my brother-in-law one so he can not 'annoy' my sis! . The only qualms I have with this product is that it is seems a bit fragile. I bought two of these to mount a pair of monophonic shotgun mics to a video camera. They are a little bulky, but lightweight and do a good job of isolating the mic bodies from any camera-generated noise, and most camera body bumps and handling noises as well. Mics mounted using this product are still sensitive to any noise caused by contact with or motion of the mic cables although this is not the mount's fault or job so care must be taken to isolate the mic cable from potential contact or noise sources as sell as the mic itself. Otherwise, your cable will act as a mechanical conduit which conducts noise directly up to the mic body in spite of being suspended on a shock mount. I'm pretty happy with this product if for no other reason than it provides a good way to mount mics to camera. The extra rubber bands O-rings are a good idea although I haven't seen any deterioration in the first set so far after using the product for several months., so I expect these won't be necessary for long while if at all.

You probably want to keep your cigars at 70 humidity, but can the hygrometer in this unit tell you if that's what you've got? No! When I first tried to use it I noticed that it was reading 4 lower than two other hygrometers I'd just calibrated. The instructions that came with this one didn't tell me how to adjust it the others had been pretty easy so I went to the manufacturer's website, only to find out that the Inkbird ITH-10 cannot be adjusted! Maybe you're thinking, "Okay, if you know this hygrometer reads 4 low you can just adjust the reading in your head accordingly each time you look at it." Well, as ridiculous as it would be to keep using a device that did that, it might work... except that Inkbird's website also says the hygrometer is only accurate to within plus or minus 5 . Five percent! So if it's reading the humidity in your humidor at if 70 , is it really 65 ? If so your cigars may dry out they could be ruined. Then again, maybe it's as high as 75 if so your cigars will probably be soggy and give you a really bad smoke. So what good is this hygrometer? None at all! I sent mine back. Skip this unit entirely.

Having used these on 4 LED costume projects, I have to say these are a great value at 10. Awesome for LED rings strips. Really fun patterns, RF remotes are nice. though the batteries eventually die after a lot of usage Only getting 4 stars because I have two gripes 1. The connectors are attached so that the male connectors have power on them. this goes against electronics wiring 101 The female connectors should always be the power'ed side. I ended up cutting all the connectors off and replacing them with female before installing them on any of my hardware. 2. The wires don't have enough strain relief. I've had solder joints fail on two of these at the controller. If you plan on adding these to any projects, make sure that you provide additional support. 3. The remotes aren't coded, so if you have more than one they all get controlled by the same remote. though I guess this can be fun

Our medical pad of choice for post breast surgery home care applications, with highly absorbent pad and gentle cushioning and easy release silicone border tape. We used a lot of these. as 10 to a box 5 days for two breasts. Initially priced in the 40's, and fairly easy to find on Amazon, as we ordered more and more over a 5 month period these pads became scarce on Amazon and price rose into the high 60's.. Maybe a good example of supply and demand? The suppliers on Amazon were all good and for most part delivered promptly however, some suppliers would fade away as they ran out of this product and did not seem to get any more for us to order. Each month, we'd move from one favored supplier to the next as the supplies dwindled and ran out from supplier to supplier. Subsequently, the prices started going up. Retail pricing for this product outside of Amazon can be in the 100's. Hopefully, the suppliers will reorder restock for others who want to use this superior product via Amazon.

Musician Tron Syversen has created a stunning series of albums that have led him to the forefront of the new age music field. His sound is characterized by his piano and keyboards backed by wordless vocalizing and occasionally other top classically-trained musicians on acoustic stringed instruments, woodwinds or horns. This acclaimed series continues with his PIANO MEDITATIONS recording. Syversen, who hails from Norway, makes music that is always beautiful and enjoyable to listen to, but also is perfect for relaxing or meditative moments. The music envelopes you in a floating bubble of gentleness that is hauntingly-lovely and stress-reducing at the same time. His piano improvisations are wondrous, and he is ably assisted by special-guest singer Helen Lorentzen, who adds just the right floating, ethereal, vocal sounds to keep the music warm, human and primal. The recording, which has four long tunes, also features some orchestral instruments played by actual musicians on the final track. Recommended.

After opening the box to set things in place, I noticed the problem this particular set doesn't come with the bolts! It's just the toggle that HOLDS the bolt. I now have to get a set of machine screws or return these. It's probably easier to return this box and complain at this point, but I'll suck it up and run to Home Depot to get the screws I need and ding them with a review so others may be helped. While I'm rating the product itself with a high score in my head, I have to drop this item to one star because you just assume the set will come with everything you need -- especially when the box shows the product with the screw in it and the previous set I bought from the store came with the screws. Who sells just the toggle doohickey itself? That's not helpful. Disappointed bear.

I have had the opportunity to hear Emrah Kotan perform live several times and he always blows me away with his rhythmic sensibility and his astounding ear for arrangement. He is an exciting musician to witness in action. All that comes through on this excellent release that travels so many different places yet remains tight and cohesive throughout. Kotan brings his apprehension of Turkish and Middle Eastern musicality and of Western jazz into a perfect balance. I love his sense of timing in moving from one idea to another, and his appreciation for space. One of my favorites is this regard is "AJ," which also incorporates so many instrumental colors and moods. I love what he does with Miles Davis's "Nardis." He is a genius at arranging. The dialogue between the musicians on "Istanblues" transports me to an absolute other place. Really, every track creates its own world -- a world I want to revisit many times. Well done . . .

I wasn't sure about spending a lot of money on a guitar since I was just starting out. I bought this to work with Rocksmith 2014 and it did the job just fine. The guitar needed some work to get it properly set up since tuning it straight out of the box sounded horrible. It turns out that the intonation needed to be set properly and also the strings that it came with are garbage. I put new strings on it and fixed the intonation, adjusted the truss rod a bit and adjusted the string height all with just the tools that they provided with the guitar and it sounded a lot better. Unfortunately there's no 22nd fret on this guitar. That probably shouldn't come as a big surprise as a lot of stratocaster type guitars only have 21 frets, but there's this one song that requires me to bend the high e string on the 22nd fret... The single coil pickups have quite a bit of hum on them so for rocksmith you kinda have to calibrate it a few times to make sure that it doesn't pick up much of that hum, or else you resort to using the 2nd and 4th switch setting so that it uses two pickups at once which cancels out the hum. I think if you wanted to try modding your guitar this would probably be a decent choice too. I haven't gotten that far yet. I've had it since February, changed strings on it twice in 4 months and so far it's proven to be a fairly nice little guitar to learn on...except for that 22nd fret oh well .

Although the appearance may not be to everyone's taste, I really like the washed-out finish with highly visible grain, and the anodized aluminum pick guard. Musically, the guitar both plays and sounds great. It shares playing characteristics with my "standard" American Standard, although the ash body and maple fingerboard give this guitar a brighter, spankier sound, in my opinion.

I really enjoy this 3D pen, and so does my younger sister. The great thing about 3D pens like this is that they can be used for simple, child-friendly creations or extremely complex creations giving them a large age-range of interest. You could even get it for someone who is interested in 3D printing as a gift as it's much cheaper than an actual 3D printer but uses some of the same principles and materials. One notable thing is that 3D pen filament comes in two types, ABS and PLA. Both of these have different melting temperatures and if you select the wrong temperature, or use the wrong type of filament for your pen, it can get jammed. I liked the way this pen can have the filament type preset changed on the screen, this means you can alternate between ABS and PLA easily without worrying about blocking or jamming. As long as you use any type of 1.75mm diameter filament and set the correct type you should be OK!

I wanted to feed my brand new PRUSA some orange PLA so after a little research I went with Hatchbox. The packaging is excellent, with the filament tightly shrink wrapped and a "do not eat" thingy inside to keep the moisture away, The outside of the box is well labeled and has a color stamp which I appreciate as it makes it easy to organize and find it on the supply shelf. Hatchbox make a big deal of their quality control on the size, and indeed it seemed to be exact as it should. Is this really a problem with some brands? I changed filaments without incident, and when the first layer went down I thought is was an "orange transparent" color, however after a couple of additional layers the vibrant orange came through. I really appreciate the product guide that comes in every box It's a high-quality print that lists all their products in all their respective colors, and provides a great primer on the different filament materials including their strength and weaknesses. Each individual product includes an Amazon bar code that you can use with your phone to 1-click order the product - VERY COOL! To me the product guide tells me that Hatchbox is dedicated to quality and making my life easier, so in the sea of filaments where it's hard to know how different they really are I think I'm now a Hatchbox customer for life.

Great reliability, no false positives, and from about 40 tests, it never missed detecting a live wire. Make sure you TEST before each usage on known live and dead wires. I remove the batteries when not in use as there is no OFF setting, but it does power off by itself after a while. As with any tester of this type, if you wave it around and move it too fast, it will flash briefly, so make sure you are moving slowly towards the wire in question to get a good reading. Used this to check 220 baseboard heater wiring when I was installing new thermostats and a new baseboard to make sure when I shut all the circuit breakers off, that there really wasn't any live wires. I'm still alive so it did its job. I have used other Fluke testers for various computer network cabling and Fluke equipment is always so reliable and useful, I didn't bother trying any other model of tester.

I picked this up because I was looking at the Zoom G5 and Amazon said this was the "newer" version of that product. It is newer and it seems to be in the same line, but it doesn't do some of the same things that the G5 does. For me, the biggest complaint was that the looper is built into each patch chain. What this means is if you change patches the loop stops. This is also true of the built in rhythms which make it useless for practicing when you want to practice changing patches. I didn't own a G5, but in looking at them online it appears that the looper and rhytmn are outside of the patch chain. With all of that said, I am sending it back because the basic loop function is what I was wanting. But the pedal itself is very nice. I like the screens that tell what the stomp boxes are. It makes it easy to remember which button to turn on and off between songs. It is also easy to program which is good because the computer software for it is only for backing up your patches and organizing them. You can't edit sounds on the computer . It is tough and doesn't feel like a toy. The bottom switches that look strange are actually really nice. Because they are made to scroll through things they make more sense than heavy metal switches. All in all a good built pedal, with decent sounds, but it just didn't meet my needs.

Two of Denmarks most promising young jazz musicians, violinist Kristian Jorgensen and guitarist Jacob Fischer celebrated a dozen years of musical friendship with this album. At the time 2000 , Jorgensen was the more widely known. Now 2015 , hes been overshadowed by Fischer. Its a musical mix of styles from the 1930s to the present. The arrangements are spare and the tunes range from a few Danish originals to American Songbook standards. Also included are a pop tune that has become a contemporary standard by Billy Joel Just The Way You Are and a modern jazz classic by Chick Corea Atmandos Rhumba . The overall mood of the album is somewhat melancholy which makes Broken Arm Blues, a Fischer original, an odd choice to conclude with. That said, its an album that has aged well and is worth repeated listening. The tunes on the album are 1. In The Wee Small Hours Of The Morning 2. A Dream Is A Wish Your Heart Makes 3. Honeysuckle Rose 4. Banks 5. Bailer 6. Tit Er Jeg Glad 7. Old Folks 8. Just The Way You Are 9. Armandos Rhumba 10. Broken Arm Blues Comment

I had a mattress topper that developed a tear and didn't want it to continue further. Spraying this allowed me to bond the two sides together for a permanent repair. It also works similarly for craft use. It works great on any of this kind of foam. A few notes 1. It seems to mildly "melt" the surface when it glues them together, and it will be a bit thicker or stiffer than the surrounding foam. 2. It is only for foam-to-foam, it isn't really sticky at all at least on anything else I tried it on . 77 is more general purpose. 3. The spray nozzle is a bit wide for small repairs so you might want to spray into something and use an applicator for a small area. 4. It sets very quickly - under a minute so you need to position the parts fairly quickly after applying and hold them together. It sets but I kept them pushed together for a few more minutes.

I appreciate good quality audio. I would never call myself an audiophile, but I appreciate good audio quality regardless. These are good. The lows are extremely clear and realistic cheap bassy headphones might appeal more to a certain crowd, and they will give you more oomph, but in return you get muddyness down there well, and in most frequencies really and often a high frequency cutoff, whereas these go deep and aren't muddy anywhere. I'm really satisfied with the way these are set up. They're also very comfortable. The pads are leather-covered and nice, though as with other leather-bound closed headphones your ears will get hot. You can make the set even more comfortable by buying replacement pads intended for the SRH-840, which fit the 440 just fine. The headphones are solid. They're big and beefy, but they fold up nicely for travel. The included cable is thick enough, and it's also detachable so it can be replaced if it wears out for any reason. I'm a little concerned by the exposed wires on the outside of it, but I've had this set for more than three years and haven't had any problems yet.

I was surprised when I first read several user reviews of Gogol Bordello's new album that criticized it heavily. After having listened to this album several times over, and being a long time listener of Gogol Bordello, I feel comfortable saying they were quite wrong. I suspect they wanted the band to just rehash the sound and beats that they had gotten used to. The thing is that Gogol Bordello has a stated purpose of constantly innovating and changing up their music in order to prove an aesthetic argument. And I think they succeed here. There is the same underlying passion and energy, but it is at times more subtle or subdued. Furthermore, they once more find ways to incorporate new folk traditions from around the world and emerge with a sound that is novel and refreshing once more. If you only like the punk sound of the Gypsy Punks album, maybe this is not for you. If you enjoy interesting and innovative music, than I would suggest this album. One user was trying to declare Gogol Bordello's music dead with this album. Fortunately, they seem to be in good aesthetic health.

Most entry-level crash cymbals that I've used tend to have a shrill metallic decay, but so far my experience with Meinl's HCS line has been exceptional! I personally own this 14" HCS crash cymbal and the HCS bell which is far superior to any other sub- 100 bell that I've played.... but this review is for the 14" crash cymbal . This cymbal provides the expected decay from one of its size, but does not have any unpleasant, ear-piercing overtones as I feel most entry-level cymbals made of brass provide. I won't mention any other name brands for comparison, but this HCS cymbal far exceeds my expectations than the P-brands and off branded brass alloy cymbals I have played in the past. This is an excellent crash cymbal for a student drummer or on an experienced drummer's practice kit.

I bought this ocarina after failing to get any sound out of a flute. I had never been able to play an instrument. Now I feel I can! This ocarina, at 5 1 2 inches is right for my medium small hands. I was able to play the lower notes the right hand ones on the first day. I knew the high notes would be more difficult, but I think with practice I'll manage to play those too. I found the Lord of The Rings booklet useless, because it only has drawings an outline of the ocarina with the holes to plug in for each note. It is impossible to sight-read the drawings while playing a song. I bought instead "The Art of The Ocarina." It explains the fingering for 17 notes, teaches how to read music notation, and has lots of familiar songs and exercises. I had never been able to sight-read music before, but with this book I can learn. When I am not using it, I keep the ocarina in the carton box that came with the package, so it doesn't break it did not come with a case . My only complaint is that I don't look like the female elf playing the ocarina in the STL videos, and I don't have a white horse. But I can dream, and play this wonderful instrument.

It hasn't been a good decade for Korn. Slumping record sales combined with line-up changes and creative frustration have threatened to force the group into irrelevance. In 2005, the band saw the exit of guitarist Brian "Head" Welch and released " See You on the Other Side ", a blatantly over-produced and experimental album that attempted to reinvent the band's signature sound by blanketing it in dense electronics and slick pop production. A mere two years later, and with the arrival of " Untitled " the band had bid farewell to drummer David Silveria, thus slimming down to a three-piece. With three different drummers in tow, "Untitled," lacked consistency which compromised its creativity. In turn, it flopped harder than any Korn album before and for a while, it looked the band would never recover. With album number nine, the Bakersfield heroes attempt to set things right once and for all. By recruiting drummer Ray Luzier Army of Anyone full-time and reuniting with producer Ross Robinson -- the man at the helm of their ground-breaking nbsp debut nbsp and its follow-up, " Life Is Peachy " -- "Korn III Remember Who You Are" is an honest to goodness attempt at a return to form for a band facing a mid-career crisis. Gone are the pop-songwriters and the studio trickery which masked the group's short-comings and in their place is a down and dirty, back to basics sound that attempts to re-capture the intensity and immediacy that accompanied their best work. Unfortunately, this technique also serves to show the holes in Korn's creative process, revealing a band that sounds incomplete. Part of what made the first two Korn albums so great was the call-and-response riffing of James "Munky" Shaffer and Welch, and since the band refuses to hire a full-time second guitarist and instead, choose to cram their touring guitarist Shane Gibson behind the equipment during live shows , Munky's riffs are forced to pick up much of the slack in the band's sound, but without accompaniment, fall flat. The biggest crime on "Remember Who You Are" is the lack of a single memorable riff in the vein of "Clown" or "Good God." Nowhere is this more evident than on the album's first single, "Oildale Leave Me Alone ". Instead, it's left up to the rhythm section to compensate, and to that end, the album succeeds. Ray Luzier is a big part of the album's sound, and his chemistry with Fieldy on bass is evident throughout. Between the two, some of the band's grooviest and catchiest material in years comes to fruition on songs such as "Pop a Pill" and "Fear is a Place to Live". The biggest enemy of the album, however, is frontman Jonathan Davis. While Davis turns in a decent enough vocal performance, the subject matter of his lyrics is cringe-inducing at best. While he made valiant enough efforts at expanding his scope on the band's latter-day albums, he regresses to the whiny, mopey dude in a track-suit that he grew out of many, many years ago. When you're pushing forty and you've got a successful marriage, career and children, it's hard to take it seriously when you cry at the end of a song. This sort of thing worked in 1994, but even its most hardcore fanbase has grown out of Korn's self-pitying and for a while, it seemed like Davis had too. The lyrics and their delivery sound forced and artificial, verging on self-parody at times and threaten to tear down whatever integrity the album may have. On the whole, "Korn III Remember Who You Are" isn't exactly the return to form that you may be hoping for, but for a band that has slimmed down considerably, they make do with what they have, with the addition of Luzier being perhaps the best thing to happen to the band in years. Old-school fans might find comfort in hearing a band attempt to re-align itself with its roots, but those who have grown above and beyond will find it to be merely child's play. It makes for decent enough background music, but when examined too closely, there are too many surface cracks and flaws to ignore. Is this Korn's " St. Anger " and is there a much better album to follow? Or have they stalled creatively and changed so much that they will no longer be able to function as a cohesive band? Only time will tell.

I own the PC3x and it is the best keyboard I have ever owned. I am always on the look out for new gear and recently tried the Korg Kronos at guitar center looking to add to my collection. I was shocked that even though The PC3x has been out a few years it still seems light years ahead of the Korg in sound quality. Other reviews have commented on the difficulty in programming the unit, Like any modern synth there is a learning curve however I do not find programming the Kurz any harder or easier than my other synth. Actually the support from other users and the video training lesson on Kurz's website make it easier than most! The great thing about the PC3x is the preset sounds are amazing and really don't need much tinkering. Intuitively the parameters I would think of adjusting on any preset have already been assigned to the one of the midi faders. I have spent very little time modding the presets. why? they sound awesome to begin with and I rather spend the time making music which is what this keyboard is about. Yes the keyboard is heavy, in a good way. It is a real instrument it doesn't have that cheap plastic feel other top of the line models from other manufactures have. That is one of the reasons I didn't add the Korg Kronos to my collection. The Kronos has a lot of amazing features especially if you create a lot of dance music but for me personally I couldn't get past the cheap feel of the instrument and the thinness of a lot of the sounds. For me when I am making electronic dance type music I use software synths which are light years ahead than any stand alone keyboard which brings me to the Kurz as a controller Action feel of a keyboard is personal preference. To me this is very important and I feel the Korg keyboard has a natural solid feel which helps me create the feel I need to play the acoustic piano sound. Pros -very solid feeling professional keyboard with build quality second to none -amazing presets especially organs,and acoustic instruments -Great controller keyboard - the versatile presets make this a great goto keyboard for live use - sound sound sound, In the end for me the most important characteristic of a keyboard is the sound quality. I yet to find a better overall sounding board. -best costumer service - Amazing collection of strings and orchestral sounds Cons -The LCD screen could be touch or bigger -The ribbon controller has to be bought separate no other keyboard even has a ribbon controller -could use more electronic dance sounds however this is not a big deal since I use soft synths to get these sounds Overall this is the perfect keyboard controller for those who want a professional instrument that IMO has the most full and lush sounds and the keyboard action has a solid professional feel. For me the decision to buy this keyboard was easy because in the end the only thing that matters is the quality of the sounds and the playability this is where the Kurzweil shines.

My kids have enjoyed watching movies in our basement on an old Sony 35" CRT that I hooked up to an old stereo system with an RCA cable. A couple of weeks ago, I got a great deal on a new Samsung 51" plasma screen to replace the old Sony 35" CRT. To my chagrin, the new Samsung only has HDMI and optical audio outputs. There was no way I was going to shell out a few hundred bucks to buy an updated A V receiver just so the kids could watch Dora the Explorer and play Wii. The Gefen converter arrived via standard shipping just 2 business days after I ordered it it does not come with an optical cable so I had to order that separately . The converter is quite small and is easy to hide behind your screen. The hookup was a cinch. At first, there was no sound all that was required was a push of the toggle switch and ... presto! Great stereo sound was mine again. If your TV allows you to disable Dolby digital 5.1, you can save money by getting the cheaper model. Otherwise, this baby is what you want. A few other reviewers complained of loud "pops" when changing between standard def and hi def channels I have had no problems so far. I wish it was cheaper but it was certainly a better solution for me than buying a new A V receiver. Pros arrived quickly, easy to install, takes up very little space, works well. Cons pricey but still worth it .

Wow! These are tasty and have a great texture. They are thick with a subtle salty taste. They have an interesting history - Stacy used to give them free to her customers as they waited in line at her bread cart in Boston. They almost have a bread stick texture. Regarding nutrition, there are healthier chips out there with more fiber, less salt, less sodium and less fat. However, these are better than potato chips. They are baked, not fried. These have no cholesterol which is positive. Sodium is 11 of daily value, fiber is 2 , total fat is 8 . And you can have 9 chips for 140 calories. 9 chips are filling as these are thick, so that's positive. 5 chips are plenty if you want a bit of crunch. They remind me a tad of thick chinese fortune cookies but aren't sweet, are thick and salty instead. Only 1g of sugar which is excellent. For a fairly non-guilty chip, these are worth trying. They are tasty! As a musical instrument maker I have several tuning aids which have served me well over the years but I was looking for a device that would recognise more concisely certain notes that other tuners fail to be happy about. Also I wanted a tuner that would measure harmonic positions within a complex sound. This is at times a tall order but I think the Auto Strobe 490 is achieving this. My first tuner was a Yamaha PT4 which cost close to 1000 back in 1976.. it was very good but the Peterson Auto strobe is a great improvement... long may it last. I am a woodwind instrument maker but I also have other types of instruments to tune , Accordion type reeds and some stringed instruments. One feature of this type of tuner is the way it shows the decay of a note. The extreme accuracy may appear to be overkill for musical instrument work but there are times when measuring a pitch to one thousandth of a semitone is usefull.

I have avoided bagless vacuum cleaners for many years, as tests show they don't clean as well as bagged vacuums and often can be messy to empty. That said, bagless vacs are very popular so I was delighted to have the opportunity to test one. The Hoover Air Pro borrows a lot of design cues from the Dyson uprights that made this category popular, right down to the ring of cones and the "cyclone" style air path. The Air Pro assembles easily without tools. All models of the Air Pro come with a combination crevice and brush tool that stores on the side of the vacuum. This one also comes with a "turbo" brush that uses some of the airflow to spin a brush. Inexplicably, there is no place on the vacuum to store this accessory, so you have to find another place for it. You can pull the wand out of the vacuum body or just release the handle and put the accessories on that. A dial on the side switches between floor and hose. The power switch, and a switch for the motorized brush roll, are on the main body of the vacuum, so you have to reach down from the handle to push them. When I first used it, I was perplexed at how to release the latch that holds the body upright - usually there is a foot-operated lever for this. Here, you hold down the brush base with your foot and pull back on the handle, unsnapping it. It's a bit disconcerting at first, but it works. I do wonder about how long this mechanism will last, though. The joint swivels allowing you to turn easily and slide the head under furniture such as beds and sofas. The Air Pro is lightweight and there's a convenient carrying handle on the main body. The cord wraps around stays on the side, one of which swivels down to release the cord. As for emptying the dirt cup, while the cup fills quickly, it is also easy to empty without much mess - just hold it over a wastebasket and push a button - the bottom swivels down and the dirt mostly falls out. I found that I had to bang it a few times to dislodge some clumps that got stuck. In use, the Air Pro is quieter than other upright vacs I have used, and the suction is decent - not as much as my regular Hoover upright, but good enough for most use. I tried it on an upstairs hall carpet that had cat fur, dirt and pieces of cat food, and it was encouraging to see the swirl of crud in the clear dirt cup. I don't think it cleaned as well as the heavier upright, but for a quick or frequent cleanup it is pretty good. The motorized brush worked well and was easy to clean. Edit October 24, 2013 The Air Pro continues to work well, but I stick by my assertion that the suction is weak compared to more traditional vacuums. Certainly it is weaker than both my Hoover U900 Wind Tunnel bagged upright and also my Miele 300 canister. But it is adequate. One thing I noticed recently is that if the brush roll gets stuck, the roll motor disengages - you can push the brush button again to restart it. I also like that the dirt cup disassembles completely for cleaning, though it took me a few minutes to figure out all of the steps needed to do this.

OVERVIEW I was given this product to test and review as part of Hisgadget's Super User program. PROS Accurate -- With 2 lasers instead of one, this is even more accurate than the first Dr. Meter I have. Again, I didn't perform scientific experiments to measure the precise accuracy of this thermometer, but I'm confident in the results I did see throughout my apartment. F 'n C -- Both units of temperature measurement are here Lights 'n Lasers -- Backlit LCD screen makes it easy to read. Red dot laser beam makes it easy to see where you're pointing Simple -- Point shoot. That's all it takes to measure the temperature of nearly any surface Battery Included -- It take a 9V so good that it's included. CONS None really BOTTOM LINE At 40, it seems overly pricy. You probably would be fine with sticking with the single-laser model, unless you're looking for absolute precision measurements. But if you are going for precision, there are probably higher-end models you'd consider.

I don't gig. It is unlikely that I will ever play in front of a crowd. But if you're like me, play as a hobby and like to fart around with various sounds and genres, this amp is a blast. It is very likely that I will play a Johnny Cash song on Tuesday and a Metallica song on Wednesday. Pretty much just make sure your guitar is in tune and dial up the sound. No pedals, no hours of minor tweaks, just punch it up and learn the song. Virtually every genre is represented here. I also have to mention that although I like metal, I rarely play it. I prefer traditional R'n'R sounds and the Spider IV recreates tons of my favorites in addition to the high gain ultra noisey sounds. Even with the best modeling in the world, my Les Paul does not sound like a Strat being played through 65' Fender. It sounds digital. No getting around it. Something else that weighed into my purchase was durability. Although I only play for fun, I still play 2-3 hours a day. My 9 year old also is learning to play, so it gets a lot of use and moves from game room to garage to bedroom daily. The one thing that was common in virtually all of the reviews of Line 6 amps was that they were long lasting and durable. I have a family member that is a professional musician and one of his band members gig with the larger 120 just becuase they can take such a beating. Ultimately I decided that for the price 300 at the time , I couldn't beat the Line 6. There is just no comparing a tube amp with effect pedals against a solid state modeling amp. They sound better, more prefessional. They are also designed to be played loud to get the most out of the tubes. Although the Line 6 sounds tinny and at max volume, for in home play, it sounds terrific. There are mountains of reviews that compare the Line 6 to Vox, Marshall or Fender. They are ALL better than the Line6, every one of them. But be prepared to pay 10 times the price for effects pedals, head units, cabinets, cables, etc. to get those flawless professional sounds. Good amp, great value.

If this EP is any indication of the album to come, I am very excited. "Good Times" is a solid tune that is reminiscent of his earlier work and a great combination of his more recent work, but has a similar feel as Lennon's "Instant Karma". "Loving You" is the clear stand out track of the EP- it starts off with a classical piece that maybe original it may not, I'm not that familiar with the classical genre, but regardless it is a pretty piece and then it cuts-off and a hard driving piano rhythm comes on with a pure pop sound, like an "Unfamiliar Faces" Matt, but with the addition of strings throughout the song, the chorus is catchy and everything you want in a pop song. Sacred Hills could just have easily been on his last album "Mobile Chateau", It is good, but I'm afraid it is going to be one that is going to take multiple listening on a playlist before I truly appreciate it. "Never Change" is definitely a folk song, it is probably the prettiest song on the album, it reminds me of an earlier song of his "Bound", but it has a more personal sound to it- acoustic guitar, bass, lead vocal with no effect, and a subtle female backing on the chorus,so simply done and so perfectly beautiful. Overall this EP is just like Matt Costa himself it doesn't fit into any one genre, but somehow it all just goes together and you find yourself loving every bit of it.

I bought and returned this because it had the richlite fretboard. In case you did not hear, a couple of years ago , Gibson was raided most unjustly by the government for supposedly using illegal wood. Most of their supply was confiscated. Gibson had to try to stay in business so they began using man made materials. Not richlite is used by Martin and other big makers. But for 4 grand I we as players expect tradition . That means ebony. We expect the guitar to have a WOOD fretboard. Not some bizarre , chemical filled pressed recycled paper mystery substance. I was annoyed to find this out AFTER getting the guitar. Beware that the new Les Paul Customs seem to all have non ebony fretboards now. Just so you guys know and can keep an eye for that. Otherwise. well the guitar still sounded good. It was nice and clear. The tone was slightly effected by the lack of the ebony fretboard. I would say it made the tone a little thinner. Slight though ok.. Its not a massive difference. If you can deal with the lack of an ebony fretboard on a 4 thousand plus dollar guitar then this is ok. But I like most feel that the price should have been lower. Somewhere in the 3200 - 3400 range because of the lack of the more expensive ebony. Long term collectability might be harmed by Gibsons choice of a non wood fretboard. If you can pick one up cheap, go for it. But don't pay retail for it . In my opinion, long term value of the richlite models will be very low compared to the vintage and classic wood versions. ALso what if.. IF.. and maybe when Gibson manages to make them again with ebony. Poof! there goes your money!. for now, seek older , vintage models pre 2011 for sure.

A good student saxophone is hard to judge for anyone entering the music scene that doesn't know much about saxophones. These ratings are skewed by lack of experience and knowledge so go to a saxophone forum where professional saxophonist constantly read and post comments and ask the professionals what their opinion is of these low end cost saxophones. You don't even need to create an account, just do a search because there tons of negative posts on these instruments. I promise you 9 out of 10 will say they play OK for a few months then they are only good for being pretty wall decorations. Kelly Price by herself is amazing, but when you couple her with another musical powerhouse like Ruben Studdard you get lyrical magic. Back 2 Love is that kind of song that couples will find themselves grooving to day and night, and reminding them what they have at home---and why it's important to return there often. "I've been working hard all week," sings Ruben, "and I can't wait to put my arms around you. It's so hard to be without you." Kelly's next line mirrors his feeling as she says "I feel a little lovesick, and you are the remedy" No matter what responsibilities you feel you are taking care of outside the home, it's important not to neglect the one that has committed themselves to you. "Time to get Back 2 Love" they sing. "Time to get back to trust." When you can have that kind of realness in a relationship then you are destined to succeed. "Time just seems to fly when I'm with you," the song says. "You are my guilty pleasure. Make this moment last forever." Who wouldn't want to hear these words from someone they have entrusted their heart to? Definitely a love song to be remembered, Price and Studdard tell it like it is with BACK 2 LOVE.

Released in 1991, "Echoes From Coquina" was pianist composer celebrated piano teacher and music educator Carol Comunes second album, following her 1990 debut, "Intracoastal." The album consists of seven piano solos that are a reflection on the serene lifestyle Comune experienced while on a trip to Floridas Gulf Coast. Six of the pieces are original compositions and one is a beautiful performance of Claude Debussys Clair de Lune. The music often suggests the quiet ebb and flow of a peaceful ocean and sometimes sparkles like light dancing on the water. Comunes background in classical music is apparent in her composing and performances, but she also brings a contemporary mindset to the music that is ageless, melodic and very accessible. "Echoes From Coquina" begins with Awakening, a slow and very graceful piece that gradually builds as awareness does when someone wakes up from a deep and very comfortable sleep. Gentle and very soothing, there is no rush to start the day! Song of the Palm Trees hints at the stately nature of these trees as well as the movement of a breeze through the fronds and perhaps the activity of the wildlife that lives in the trees. Very free and relaxed, this piece is truly a pleasure to listen to! Shell - Dance of the Coquinas refers to the seashells that dance at the waters edge at low tide. These delicate sea-creatures are often colorful and resemble butterflies, inspiring this light yet swirling piece. Rainbows are magical everywhere, but over the ocean, they can enchant even the most jaded beach walker. Rainbows gently expresses that breathless awe. If you have ever watched a sunset by the ocean, you know the profound peacefulness of the changing sky and the intensity of the colors reflected on the water. Sundown paints a vivid picture of this experience. Debussys Clair de Lune Moonlight has always been a favorite classic both to play and to listen to, and Comunes performance is lyrical and expressive. Quiet Nights, Distant Lightning beautifully depicts the flashes of lightning on a dark sky. Exciting and unpredictable, sometimes they are bright flashes and sometimes flickers in the distance. There is no thunder, so this really is a dance of flashes of light against the dark sky. Even though it was released twenty-six years ago, "Echoes From Coquina" is fresh and timeless. If you love peaceful music with a nature theme, be sure to check it out! Recommended!

Package arrived fast, and contained the right number and size of screws. But, I was surprised to see that the outer 'coating' is actually yellow zinc plating. My own fault, because it says 'yellow zinc' on the description page . I've been using this type of screw on indoor projects for years... but I didn't think this was an exterior screw. So I did some internet searching and found this ... They say it is twice as corrosion resistant as standard zinc plating. But then they say 'we cannot guarantee these screws against corrosion.' I guess it is going to depend on your application. Myself, I'm building a fence along the north shore of a marine island, where the winter winds blow salt spray up on to the property... So they're probably not going to work here. Maybe if you're building a deck in a dry climate, these would be appropriate. All in all, they delivered what they said -- but a better picture of the product would show what it actually looks like. And then people like me would know what we're getting.

Moving Mountains is amazing live. I saw them play a frenzied, urgent set of rock and roll songs that were filled with energy but still had room to breathe. Maybe it's the tuning of the guitars, maybe there's a certain sound' or maybe it's just me, but a handful of bands just exude this attitude and sound through their music that instantly attracts me and hooks me - Moving Mountains is one of those bands. I'd never owned a Moving Mountains record, though I had heard a few songs off of their Deep Elm full length Pneumonia , back when they sounded more like a post-rock punk-rock imitation of Appleseed Cast certainly not a bad thing . I was stoked to buy Waves' but I was really nervous to listen to it. A lot of bands, especially indie bands' is this still a relevant term? , sound great live but the sound or the energy or both don't always translate to their studio albums. While the abrasiveness of Moving Mountains is dialed down on Waves' when compared to the live show, it is replaced with beautifully placed strings violins and such and a bit of a more refined sound. The energy is still there, that sound' is still there and above all the immediacy is still there. These guys are talented and their music shines because of it. After seeing them live I have to admit that I am not disappointed by Waves' at all. In fact, I think I'm even more impressed by the band. Once Rendering and My Life is Like a Chase Dream And I'm Still Having Chase Dreams do a good job of showcasing the extremes the band takes their sound to. Once Rendering is quiet, moody, full of acoustic guitar and violin while managing to keep from being boring or dull - it's got something brooding under the surface, something that never comes up for air. My Life is Like a Chase Dream And I'm Still Having Chase Dreams is full of that relentless energy and urgency, the kind of indie rock and roll that caught my attention in their live show, and never lets up. The rest of the album lives in between these two extremes, some songs dipping their toes in both pools while others remain on the quiet side with violins and moments of serenity or the energetic with driving guitars, steady drumming and the occasional Cave Inesque yell . Waves' is a great record. I like to call it a thinking record,' which is a very rare and beautiful find. It's the kind of autumn record that would boost creativity and make the breeze feel better than usual. Like I keep reiterating, it has that sound' that just makes me feel a little more alive - it's the only way I can accurately describe it. Call it urgency, call it post-rock, call it indie, call it sincerity - I can't pinpoint it but Waves' is drowning in it. If comparisons had to be made and deep down we all love comparisons I would take Cave In Perfect Pitch Black and Jupiter and Appleseed Cast Low Level Owl and Mare Vitalis , mix the two, and add in a dash of autumn and night skies. But the key ingredient would be that undeterminable factor that some bands just have, that one unnamable thing that makes a band unique - Moving Mountains new record Waves' is dripping with whatever that is.

"All the Waters" is a difficult, rewarding extreme metal album. It's tempting to think of it as sampling the same zeitgeist as last year's Sunn O album "Monoliths and Dimensions" - like that album, "All the Waters" features a cast of dozens of musicians, giving it a "chamber metal" texture. But where "Monoliths" explored idioms from outside of the broad category of metal, with a particular emphasis on blues and jazz, "Waters" instead chooses to play with an expanded metal vocabulary. The looped sample, computer manipulations, and processed synthy drums powerfully evoke and expand on Ministry's industrial metal work of the "Land of Rape and Honey" "Psalm 69" era, and the distant, screeched, vocal performances and slightly clippy sound nod to black metal influences. And floating above the rocky base the genre's usual downtuned, droning guitars, there's the play of the sounds of an ethereal vocal chorus, didgeridoos, low brass, and electronic sounds, all crashing together with tectonic force. This is the state of the art in metal slashism, a black doom industrial chamber metal record, and will reward patient relistening with fresh frissons of dread and sonic vertigo. Highly recommended, it's the best album of 2010 so far. Track picks "Empty Hearth", "Even the Saints Knew Their Hour of Faith and Loss"

It takes a while to get the hang of this flute but I think it's totally worth it to enjoy something so simple, affordable and authentic. I ordered this last flute year just out of curiosity I am no musician, I just wanted a simple instrument I could relax with and that was also portable and this was affordable and authentic looking. It took more than a month to arrive !! and I was so impatient to play it. It is shipped by regular mail from Hong Kong and the tracking is quite rudimentary. Note you can track it at the USPS website . To my surprise, like some reviewers mentioned, I couldn't get a single sound to come out of it! At first I thought it was broken. Soon I was humbled into realizing that playing a flute was not as easy as I thought. It took me about a month to be able to play a single steady note. Now I can play the basic notes and have become good at putting on the membrane. So, wanting one to keep in my car, I ordered a second one. This one just took 2 weeks to arrive It is also slightly longer and thicker than the first one same scale though . This one also included the erjao glue as well as the membrane paper my previous one did not include the glue like some reviewers mentioned . There was also a link to a website showing very clearly how to put on the membrane on the flute which was not there before. If you can put the membrane on properly the sound is just amazing. I get carried away just doodling nonsense on my flute. The build quality is quite good, the white ends are plastic so they do feel a little cheap. It comes in a nice velvety tubular sack, my first sack was green, the second one is a nice royal red. The flute easily separates into two for portability. Pros 1 - I like the sound when the membrane is done right sounds almost like a sweet bagpipe . 2 - Feels authentic 3 - The membrane makes the sound different each time, makes me feel that the flute is unique and personalized. 4 - Nice sack included and link to instructions for putting on the membrane. 5 - Glue is now included a small piece so you may need to order a new one soon . Cons -This con is really for my first order last year, which took forever to arrive, and did not include the glue, and I had to wait another long period to receive that. Hopefully they seem to have sorted out the glue and speed issues so your experience should be better.

CakeWalk Sonar is my all time favorite software. As a software engineer I have admired this company for so long. In any other program I can probably find a bug within the first half hour of using it. Cakewalk, on the contrary can be used right out of he box, release after release. It is exceptionally tested and debugged before it reaches de user. Dimension Pro, however is not in the same boat, yet. It needs to mature out of some irritating features. For example a series of notes for the String patch plays like a nice Glissando in all my synths. In Dimension, it sounds like a stair case with sharp attacks. The Ahh patch individually sounds nice and actually human, in the whole sequence, though, it sounds out of pitch. Also, when a Patch is loaded, Cakewalk does not capture the patch information in the assigned track. But the most irritating omission is the lack of examples or exercises in the user's guide, a multilingual manual with about 100 pages per language. Not enough space to scratch the surface. For the time being, I find that the TTS-1 synth that comes with Sonar Producer 6.00 is a more robust product. But If I had the opportunity to return it, probably I would not, just because knowing Cakewalk, I am confident that eventually they will refine it to the level of the superb SONAR.

I will be honest. If the use of the vacuum cleaners in our home relied entirely upon me, our house would look very different than it does. My lovely wife has always kept a very clean house. She vacuums EVERY SINGLE DAY or night . She is a very hard working and diligent person. So I will tell you what she has shared with me about this Shark upright vacuum. First, she says that the Shark vacuums are outstanding products. Essentially, we started with the cheapest Shark to give it a try and we have added one which was priced in the middle, which she said is a great vacuum. This "Rotator" was a Christmas Present from our "canine kids" to their "mom". She said this Shark "Rotator" is a preferred feature for her which makes the machine even easier to use than the other Shark machines. She also said that while the Shark "Lift Away" features are certainly very handy on the stairs, the cheaper "lift away" is very similar in use to the more expensive "lift away" and they all work fine. I wanted to prevent my wife from having to haul these vacuum cleaners up and down flights of stairs in our home, so now she has a machine which can be left on each of the 3 floors of living space. We have a couple of Simplicity Vacuum cleaners which are very nice vacuum cleaners and MUCH more expensive than the Shark's. I think the Simplicity machines are around 800 per vacuum cleaner. She claims the Shark machines clean as well as the Simplicity and since the Shark machines are lighter, they are a little easier to use. She grabs the Shark machines to use prior to any other. When I asked her which vacuum cleaners we have owned that she liked the least and that she felt were a waste of money, she instantly replies the Dyson machines. We had service problems and no one would work on them. The part about the Dyson machines which really irritated me was their requirement that for some of the machines a few years ago, you had to buy the entire component part to get the "consumable" item. For example, one of the drive belts failed and we had to pay for a new motor which came with the belt. So instead of a few dollar part being replaced, suddenly the repairs were costing what we paid for the entry level Shark machines. I can tell you I spent more time messing around with Dyson machines and either trying to find parts or repair them than all other vacuum cleaners we have owned over the years, COMBINED. Shark has been a great product and we have both been very happy with them. When something breaks or fails, she defers to me to either repair it or find someone to repair it. Other than taking the Shark machine's out of the boxes when we bought them, I can say I haven't touched them since, which to me, is a good thing.

I have been using the Hanna pH Temperature tester for two months now. It gives relatively accurate readings, and is easy to use, and calibrate using the calibration solutions...sold separately . I keep mine submerged in a balanced salt solution. It is critical to keep the electrode hydrated at all times. If it should dry out, the life of the electrode will lose its accuracy, and may have to be replaced. I use mine to gauge the acidity of cheeses that I am making from milk products. The reason I only gave it a 4 5 is because you must recalibrate it after4 each usage. You must also purchase the standardizing solutions separately....an expensive proposition in the long run. But, I would purchase this model again.

The only concern I had was that after setting the box aside after receiving it, when I picked up the box, the rod actually slipped out of one corner of the box. Had it done that in shipping it might have been damaged. Extra care should be given by the shipper in taping and reinforcing the ends of the box because slender metal rods like this are prone to puncture the end of the packing. This concern about the packing wears the only negative I had and kept the rating from being 5 stars.

I bought my first Shop-Vac about 5 years ago and was very disappointed it came with such a small hose. The suction was very good, but the hose was constantly getting clogged. I use it primarily in my shop, and so pick up lots of little wood splinters. These would get hung-up in the hose catching up smaller items that would normally pass, and I found myself cleaning it out almost every use. I found this kit, and the ratings and reviews looked good, so decided to give it a shot. I've only had it for about 2 months now, but I can't believe I didn't buy this years ago. My biggest concern was that the suction might be somewhat reduced due to the bigger diameter, but so far I haven't really noticed a difference. Even if it has, the fact that I've never had a clog yet easily makes up for it. The larger floor floor attachment also drastically cut down the time it took to get all the sawdust off the ground. However, if I had to give a down-side, it might be that the larger hose is a bit more difficult to manage. It doesn't seem to be able to maneuver into some of the places as easily as the smaller hose could. I also liked the smaller crevice attachment for those very hard-to-reach places. I suppose there's a coupler I could get though, so I could use my old attachments. I was able to find a great use for the old hose. My miter saw is permanently attached to my workbench, and hooking up the hose to it every time was kind of a chore. I took the old hose and permanently attached it, then routed the other end where I could easily get to it. Now I just need to unscrew the large hose and attach the old one when using the saw. Eventually I plan to make a homemade cyclone system, which I can use the hose to permanently attach to it.

I liked my first Parker a double-humbucker NiteFly RF522 well enough to go for this one in HSS configuration as well. I can only say good things about the quality of these two guitars, they are very well put together. Compared to the NiteFly, this one looks a bit more "normal" with a less severly shaped upper horn, but it makes the guitar much more comfortable to play with, especially when you are sitting. I much prefer the very smooth satin emerald finish of the MaxxFly to the ok-but-boring porous matte black finish of the NiteFly, but the best feature of both these guitars is that slim, natural finish neck with the smooth ebony fretboard and the stainless steel frets, together with the tonal variety of the SD and Piezo pickups. I realize that this is just the entry level in Parker guitars I mean, genuine U.S. made Parkers , but this is a great guitar for a budget-conscious, G.A.S. afflicted collector like myself who does not care too much for flame or quilt tops, bindings and other fancy appointments in a guitar. For about a Grand and change at Amazon, no contest , you not only get a lot of guitar, you get a guitar that is truly special.

I got this because it looked like a good compromise between compactness and versatility. The wireless feature is very computer-friendly and works well, although it would've been nicer if it could hook up through Bluetooth or similar, instead of having to rely on a USB-dongle that's only 2.0 USB at best. Couldn't that have been updated to 3.0? Downloading all the software was a bit of a pain, but I already had Ableton Live 9 Lite on my PC, so that saved some work. Setting the keytar up to talk to Live was easy enough, since the instructions were fairly straightforward. The keytar has no difficulties talking to to Live, although I could only get the keyboard and octave change buttons to really do anything. The pads were almost entirely unresponsive, when I tried out a few sets of drums. I had to really hammer on them to get anything audible. The remaining knobs, buttons and slides seemed to do nothing at all, despite having installed the Vortex key mapping for Live. I couldn't find the Sonivox Vortex-I DVi software anywhere. When I registered the keytar with Alesis, their software download page didn't list it, but the user guide that came with the package insisted it was essential to operations. I followed the link to Sonivox's website, but all they provided was a link back to Alesis. Back to square one! I registered to post on the Alesis forums, and got a response from an employee that this software had been discontinued, and that I should instead download XPand!2 and install it see below . The plugin, XPand!2, wouldn't talk to Live, or anything else, for that matter. As far as I could tell, it did nothing at all but take up 1.5 Gb of space. The Vyzex software, that was supposed to allow me to quickly and easily swap patches in and out, had instructions that were more confusing than helpful. For example, there was no explanation of how to store patches in the software to begin with. I was under the impression that it came with a small selection of patches already installed, but I was wrong. The Ignite software, made by AIR Music Tech, refused to start on my desktop computer. I contacted support, and we found out that the software won't run as long as my virtual 7.1 headphones are operational. I'd have to go in and change them back to 2-speaker headphones, which bypasses the virtual sound card, so I can then start Ignite. Huh? I don't have to do that with Ableton Live 9 Lite, or with any other DAW or music writing program I already have installed. Is this program broken or what? Major fail! The keytar itself feels cheaply made. The case and keys are made of flimsy-feeling plastic, and the bottoms of the keys, which are quite easily accessible, still have molding flash attached that could easily cut my fingers open if I wasn't careful. The whole thing felt like it was going to fall apart at any moment. The terrible strap provided looked very unreliable. I'd rather use a locking strap with it anyway, just to be safe. The keys are synth-weight to the touch, but require heavy pressure in order to make audible sounds, and the "aftertouch" feature could only be activated by striking the keys very hard. The pads needed to be pressed heavily in order to activate, and considering where they're placed, would make them almost impossible to use when holding the keytar like a guitar not enough leverage . All in all, the minuses outweigh the pluses too much, to allow me to keep the Alesis Vortex Wireless. I'm returning it for a refund, so I can use the money to buy a MIDI controller from a different manufacturer. I seriously doubt I'll consider any more Alesis products, after this bad experience, and will likely avoid any software made by AIR Music Tech as well.

Lonestar Venice Mandolin I think it depends when the mandolin was made. In the early 90's, the tuners were low quality but from 1998 until now, the Lonestar Company, acquired by LPD International in 1998, installed new quality tuners made from a high quality alloy. Mine rarely goes out of tune. Also, I am very hard on mandolins when playing and this mandolin really takes a beating from me--haven't even broken a string in 2 years. It has a wonderful sound, not too bright, not too loud, just sounds like a beautiful mandolin. Mine was set up by a professional, Mr. Rizzotti, who owns All Things Plucked Plus Music Store on Ebay--that makes all the difference. The bridge was adjusted, Mr. Rizzotti put in a bottom peg so I could hold it with a strap, and the action is fast. Personally, the set up is everything for any mandolin. When you order one of these, make sure you get a free set-up from the seller or take it to a music store that is known for working on mandos. In my area, there are none, so I bought it from someone who knows mandos. It's a great mandolin at a great price. I have no complaints about mine. It's beautiful to look at and the sound and fast action on it is great. The finish is amazing I have not had to polish it once, it seems to retain it's shine. I cannot say enough good things, and I am complimented when I play music with others. I play many genres and it really holds it's own. I understand these mandos are hand-made in Mexico from what I read on the web. Remember though, treat your instruments with care and respect, never let anyone else touch it, and you'll avoid many repairs.

Rilo Kiley is all about Jenny Lewis, the fashionable, cool chick who frounts this band. Both Lewis and Blake Sennet, Rilo Kiley's guitar player, were former actors. Lewis appeared in movies such as Troop Beverly Hills and Pleasantville. Sennet was on the classic Nickelodean tv show Salute Your Shorts, which is one of my ALL TIME favorites. When they and a few other great musicians came together to form Rilo Kiley in 1999 they became, in my opinion, one of the greatest bands of our times. The Execution Of All things is their first record on Saddle Creek Records in Omaha, home to greats such as Bright Eyes, Cursive and The Faint. This is definatly an Omaha record, as compared to their last album Take Offs and Landings, which I like, but this one is definatly saddle creek material. The first song on the record is one of the best. All The Good That Wont Come out has a really catchy melody. Lewis's voice sounds almost enchanting in it. With Arms Outstretched is also a great one. It's featured on the Saddle Creek 50 compelation album which has songs from all the artist on saddle creek. Towards the end there is a chior of voices that includes Conor Oberst bright eyes and then... CLAPING! I love claping in songs. I give the execution of all things five stars because its just a good rock album. And ever since the spice girls went under bands with girl singers have been overlooked. Jenny Lewis is no spice girl although she does have an air of "girl power" . Shes a rock star. NOt in a sence of Mick Jagger, but an approachable girl with a great sence of fashion and an awesome band. The album is genius.

I'm just as fond of tradition as the next guy but sometimes new-tech just makes too much sense to pass up. The ToneShaper for the Stratocaster kicks Leo Fender's timeless design into the 21st century. A guy can spend a lot of time and bucks searching for "The Tone." Changing pickups is a large part of that, but sorting through wiring diagrams and soldering pots and switches is kind of daunting for a lot of us. The ToneShaper changes all of that. No soldering! The ToneShaper consists of the usual pots and 5-way switch but they're mounted on a circuit board that slips into a Strat's wiring cavity. All connections are solder-less as all the wiring is completed by inserting the ends into clips on the circuit board. Thirty minutes is the average installation time, even for guys who hate reading instructions like me. Furthermore, by the selection on on-board dip-switches, one can instantly configure how each pickup and pot and the 5-way switch interact with each other. Well, kind of instantly. You have to remove the strings and R R the pick guard to access the switches, but still ... after a few times, that can go pretty quickly. A small price to pay for the added flexibility. Last but not least ... no more 60 cycle hum! Forget all the cavity shielding! The ToneShaper eliminates the need for all that. No matter where you set your 5-way, no hum. Cool, huh? This is all nice but ... what about "The Tone?" Well, I love the Stratocaster "chime" with a clean amp. The cleaner, the better. After installing the ToneShaper, that's what immediately blew me away clean, clean, clean! I set it up so that I don't lose the upper frequencies as I reduce the volume, one thing I don't like about the traditional Strat setup. To sum it up, the primary advantages of the ToneShaper are 1 Ease of pickup removal and installation no soldering 2 Multiple switch pickup configurations with a flip of the dip-switches 3 No 60 cycle hum 4 The cleanest output ever 5 Great customer service the owner returned my call in person to answer a question I had about the installation Down sides 1 Funky website half the links were useless 2 Having to remove and replace the pick guard to change configurations

I'll let the audiophiles argue over the finer points of sound quality, this review relates to my needs and my experience as a guy that just wants good musical reproduction while at work. As a web developer what I needed was a well-built set of headphones that deliver deep, accurate audio across multiple genres without completely blocking environmental noises I work with an online news team and have to be able to hear when something is starting to go wrong . Bingo. I couldn't be happier. Total comfort thanks to all of the points of articulation. I get a snug fit over my big ears and can adjust the angle of the pads so as not to get pressure on the bony part of my mandible right in front of the ears. This is perhaps my biggest joy as I historically get headaches when wearing headphones. With these I can listen for hours before I get any kind of discomfort, and with a slight readjustment and refit I'm good for another few hours. When I'm listening to music, the audio drowns out incidental conversation without completely blocking it. When I turn the audio off, I can hear people fine. I broke these in with Arctic Monkeys, Moody Blues, ELO, Motrhead, Gorillaz and the Marriage of Figaro as performed by the London Opera Philharmonic. Not disappointed on any given song or performance. My purchase included a separate Bluetooth speaker and a "it'll do" pleather drawstring bag. The speaker was a nice add-in, gives a lot of sound out of a small box, and it's now in the kitchen so I can play directly from my iPhone without makeshift speakers out of the fruit bowl or a plastic cup. While not necessarily a part of the review, the inclusion of a small-but-solid speaker makes this an extraordinarily good deal. I don't think there's anything remotely comparable out there for 150.00.

First and foremost, this unit makes really great tones. I own an old Line 6 unit and this, and this is every bit as good in terms of modeling IMHO . Lots of amps to choose from, lots of cabinets to choose from, and even the pedals are modeled against commercially available units. The flexibility is fantastic, and it really does turn your amp into not much more than an amp - all of the tones come from the unit. The noise gate is really effective. I have a lot of line noise in my practice room, and single-coil guitars routinely buzz when used there - but this noise gate does a really good job at canceling that without harming the tones at all. Good tuner, that will show flat notes if you want to tune up or down. Easy to use, too, and it bypasses the output while its on so you tune silently. Very nice. It appears to be very rugged, too. Heavy case, etc. Caveat I've only used it for a week, so it's not as if I've had it on the road for a tour or anything. Durability is really something that can only be measured with time, but first impressions are strong. So, why not a 5? Three reasons Documentation stinks. 30 pages of detail, but it really doesn't tell you how to do basic things like "if you want to have two tones right next to each other, so that you can toggle between them during a song, here's what you have to do..." You can figure it out, with time and patience, and at some point the whole logic of the thing becomes clear and you really can dig deep. It's complicated only because it's so rich with choices, but the method of changing things is very consistent, very "learn-able," and ultimately quite easy... once you figure it out. Better documentation, including perhaps a few YouTube or Amazon videos would go far. The wah-pedal is a nuisance. If you really use it, it turns on and off whenever you step down on it. It's also a bit small. If a wah or volume, or whammy - it can do all three pedal is your prime reason for buying this, it might not be the best choice. For me, it's not a big deal. The main reason No power switch. Unbelievably, there is no on off switch for this unit. The most basic thing in the world, and they don't provide it. To turn it on, you plug it in... To turn it off, you unplug it. Completely impractical, and over time it will cause wear. So, use a power strip that has a power button. That's an easy solution, but it doesn't excuse DigiTech from their obligation. This unit is a value, and it puts out great sound. But even a value unit should have a power switch. If you are looking for an easy to use once you learn it system with a LOT of modeling capability, this is a great unit.

These cuvettes are as advertised, and just in case anybody wonders, they work with the Hanna HI 967XX meters I have the HI 96742 . I mention this because Hanna sells these cuvettes under two different model numbers. I did not have the scratched glass problem described by some other reviewers. Each piece was wrapped separately in bubble wrap. I'm giving the product only two stars for two reasons. One, the product gets only two stars is because it is stupidly expensive. Over 4 a piece for a tiny glass bottle with no cap is insane Well it's not really insane let's call a spade a spade It's rip-off by Hanna. You unsuspectingly buy a fairly expensive meter, then, AFTER it comes in the mail, you learn that you can't use it at all until you spend another 150 for reagents and specialized measuring devices. The second reason the product gets only two stars is related to the first. Not only do the cuvettes cost 4 a piece, but they won't even work without the specialized plastic caps that cost an additional 4 a piece. They should come in a set and be priced more in accordance with what they are. A single cuvette with cap is worth about 50 at most. I'm pretty disappointed with Hanna's "marketing" tactics.

The problems with this are in the design. If you look directly below the platform you will see a post that comes down to the arm that supports it. First problem is that the arm is just not long enough for the platform flat part with holes in it to clear the mic stand, so it is hard to adjust properly. Second problem, is the threaded bolt that screws in from the bottom of the post does not pull the top part down far enough to tighten snugly against the arm, and so it WOBBLES. I think the right washer would probably fit in there and fix the problem and I will have to go to the local hardware store to find one, but this could have been easily avoided had it been designed more thoughtfully. Or maybe it's a parts problem. If the washer fixes the wobble it's still got the problem with the arm too short, and I can think of no way to stretch it. The solution will probably be a separate mic stand dedicated to just the music holder. And that kind of defeats the purpose then, doesn't it? Now I have 2 stands again when I'd hoped I could use one for both mic and music. Bad design - looks good, but notice the picture does its best to conceal the arm length problem. Looks like there's clearance, doesn't it? Well they know what they're doing. Should have returned it but like so many things, I order them knowing I will need them eventually and trust they will work properly. By the time I try them out the return period has long gone.

I actually found that blending this with another humbucker can create some amazingly acoustic tones with great depth. I bought this to replace a humbucker in my BC Rich, I'm so glad that I did. When I started out playing guitar, I strummed with my fingers. That didn't work out so well, what with the blood and all. Then I tried the standard Fender torties, medium gauge. I've got a pretty fierce attack, though, and they wore down too fast, sometimes even tore. I switched to heavies, then extra-heavies, but torties are slippery, and the extra thickness made them fly out of my hand too often sometimes straight into the audience . And they still wore down. Then a friend of mine Mike? Mike Troutman? Are you out there? lent me a Dunlop nylon standard .73mm. There was enough texture at the grip that I could hang onto the pick, but it was smooth enough on the playing surface that it didn't sound scratchy. It was too light for my taste, but I was hooked. I ran out and bought a fistful of the .88s. I've used them ever since. That's been almost 15 years now. They're perfect. Not too stiff, not too bendy, just right. And they last forever or at least until you lose them , because they don't wear down like plastic picks do. If your style runs to frenzied strumming the way mine does, you just might fall in love with this pick. Every time I meet someone who plays like me, they've got a pocket full of these things. No kidding. Same gauge and everything. Okay, so it's a little weird that I'm rhapsodizing like this about a guitar pick, but seriously -- this is the perfect pick. If the three bears played guitar, Goldilocks would've dug Mama Bear's Dunlop Standard Nylon .88mm. Believe it.

Ambiance is a now sadly retired group of four women that formed out of the Sweet Adelines Barbershop tradition. In 1986, with their original bass and having been together for just six months, they won a regional competition. With a new bass, they went on to win the 1986 international quartet championship just a few months later and with the songs on this album, you can see why. The first song on the album is an absolutely amazing rendition of George Gershwin's "Rhapsody in Blue" and from the very first note, with the group's soprano soaring to incredible heights on the lick that is normally played by a clarinet, you know you're in for a treat. The musicianship, the vocal quality, and the blend from the group is just phenomenal. My favorites on this album are the aforementioned "Rhapsody in Blue," "When Will I Be Loved," and "Canticle of the Sun," but every song is worth a listen. I particularly loved the ending on "When Will I Be Loved," as the notes shift and turn until, magically, they resolve into a ringing chord that you would swear is being sung by a whole choir. Highly recommended.
