I had to post on here because there were only 3 reviews, 1 was spot on, 1 was in French, and 1 seemed to be written by someone who was looking for a bargain ukulele. This is NOT a beginner or bargain level ukulele. It is an INTERMEDIATE level ukulele made by the company KANILEA -- one of the four prominent ukulele makers in Hawaii known as the four K's since they all begin with K . The Islander brand is their lower-priced line that is produced in Asia, but is still made to their standards and superior to other companies that mass produce their instruments think Kala, Lanikai, Cordoba, etc, which are all good ukes and superior to the myriad of bargain ukes you'll find out there . I researched for months before making my purchase and went to several music shops, playing probably at least 50 different ukes in all price ranges. This instrument is absolutely BEAUTIFUL. Its acacia wood is stunning and the sound is brighter, louder and fuller than others in this price range. The soprano's fretboard is longer than most and has the room of a concert for people with large fingers. It comes with the premium Aquila strings, and actually has bridge pins which is virtually unheard of in a uke under 200 dollars. Most players pick up an inexpensive uke and then upgrade to an intermediate one once they realize how addictive ukulele playing is. If you would like to bypass this first step and go straight to an intermediate level uke that you won't need to upgrade until you're ready to invest 500 or more, and support a Hawaiian American ukulele company, then the Islander line is really hard to beat without spending much, much more.

This is an excellent studio album of the late great Lynyrd Skynyrd Guitarist, Steve Gaines. Plus, you get 7 more studio back-up musicians. You get a song set of 9 great songs. This album was recorded 2 years before Steve Gaines joined the Lynyrd Skynyrd Band. It was not released until 1988. Steve, along with his sister Cassie, Ronnie Van Zant, and Road Manager Dean Kilpatrick were all killed in the October 20, 1977 plane crash. Thank God, Steve left this album cd for us. He served as a excellent guitar player for Lynyrd Skynyrd, and even sung the lead vocals on one song, on Skynyrd's album "Street Survivors". Plus, he co-wrote some songs along with Ronnie Van Zant included in that album. This album cd "One in The Sun" by Steve Gaines consists of the following songs 1. Give it to Get It written by Steve Gaines 2. It's Alright A Curtis Mayfield song 3. Blackjack Davy A Tal Mahal song 4. On the Road a John Moss song 5. One in the Sun written by Steve Gaines 6. Talkin' About Love written by Steve Gaines John Moss 7. Nothin' is New written by Steve Gaines 8. Take my Time written by Steve Gaines 9. Summertime's Here written by Steve Gaines This is an excellent album cd showcasing Steve's very talented singing, guitar playing, and his songwriting. I consider it a real treat, and "must-have" for hard core Lynyrd Skynyrd fans, and I definitely am one. Thanks, and May God Bless!!!

This shouldn't have to be said, but it's not the Grateful Dead, so any direct comparisons by others are fairly pointless. If you want the Grateful Dead, listen to the Grateful Dead the music is just as amazing now as it was when it was recorded. This compilation is a really nice tribute to what was created, how many were influenced in so many ways, and to help it live on. My admiration to the musicians it must have been somewhat daunting to put their versions out there to a legion of hardcore fans who might judge them. I don't personally love everyone's interpretation of some of the songs, but I respect them for doing them in their style. The songs revive what 'modern' music has been missing for so long. Thanks to The National! ...who, BTW, recorded the song that got me through college. I also admire everyone doing it for the cause. I'm knocking two stars though because I have listened on different very good audio systems and it sounds so muted, like it's missing the full audio spectrum or whomever engineered it put too much of their own preference into the low end. At first I was completely ecstatic about it but the more I listened to it, it sounds like hearing the music over the phone or a copy of a copy of a copy. The guitar on Loser is there but doesn't come through. I listened to the original Terrapin Station next to the new one and BTW, my hat's off to anyone who has the cohones to record that one and, although a lot of it was very true to the original, the range just wasn't there and it wasn't the musicians' fault. Is anyone else noticing this? The only other thing, 2nd star, is a lot of the music is missing the crisp, happy, melodic, guitar that was Jerry's signature. I call it "don't murder me." I don't expect anyone else to be Jerry but it is and was an importance essence of the music. This is something that other musicians, like Trey Anastasio 'get.'

Finally! A rational, reasonable, affordable alternative for taming the aluminum wire menace. The only 100 solution is to rewire your house with copper. Meanwhile, the rest of us live in the real world. The COPALUM crimp system has been around for years, but is still very expensive and it can be a challenge to find a company-trained and -certified electrician to do the job. Enter AlumiConn basically a little set-screw bus bar encased in a plastic box with the wire holes packed with antioxidant sealing grease. The connections are super secure, sealed, and the aluminum and copper never touch. If you do some research you'll find that the UL-approved installation requires an inch-pound torque screwdriver. I did a destructive test and found that the 10 aluminum and 12 copper wires were nicely squished under the set screw when following the company's instructions without the expensive screwdriver. After decades of tightening fasteners and terminals, I have confidence in these things. I've cleared out one dubious pigtail done by the previous owner and I'm looking forward to installing AlumiConns on every outlet and switch in our house. Pros Quality construction and materials, not nearly as bulky as you'd expect, in-line wire arrangement makes box stuffing easier Cons Only 3 terminals, big enough to require larger remodel boxes if you're working with devices like dimmers

Herb's 16th 1. Coney Island 9 10 Upbeat, sparkly and fun. Extra point for the trombone toots. 2. I Have Dreamed 7 10 Nice main melody but a little too razzle-dazzle on the chorus. 3. Senor Mouse 6 10 Fun tune, but a bit too busy for my taste. 4. Mickey 9 10 I like Herb's pensive mood. Interesting string section on this one. Almost sounds synth, but maybe its filtered through a processer. Bob Edmondson gets a nice mopey trombone solo. Plus one for the accordion. 5. Sweet Georgia Brown 4 10 Nothing too great about this one. And those drums are way too cymbal heavy. 6. Ratatouille 10 10 Ah yes. Nothing like those French melodies. Melancholy verse with a misty, wistful chorus. My favorite tune on the album. Herb's trumpet sounds great with some nice interplay with the trombone. 7. Catfish 4 10 Its got some groove to it, but the totality of it sounds uninspired. More of those cymbal-happy drums. 8. This Masquerade 9 10 Strong minor-key melody with a down and dirty groove and cool slippery trumpet read. Kind of reminiscent of his circa SRO style. 9. Carmine 9 10 The mellow, minimal structure provides a good framework for some serious trumpet acrobatics. And that high F? Holy banana! I didn't know that was possible. 10. The Crave 5 10 Underdeveloped, briefly rescued by that salsa rhythm. 11. Vento Bravo 10 10 Peppery melody with cool percussion, enhanced by a very effective piano part. 12. I Belong 7 10 Lyrics are a bit sappy, but who am I to quibble with the great Hal David? a nice instrumental portion and vocal refrain. I think I hear Lani Hall in the mix. 89 120 3.71 5 A little bit of a let down after the heights achieved by his previous album the year before. Nevertheless, it has a couple of really good tracks, and is a must-own for any Herb completist. After Coney Island, Herb would retire the Tijuana Brass-TJB moniker, briefly resurrecting it for one album in 1984.

I own a small business and own 5 3D Printers. I blow through ABS like nobodies business, and have tried a lot of different sources. I've mostly been sticking to ordering from Makerbot.com just because they're filament is consistently better than anything else I've tried. You pay a lot more and it takes FOREVER for them to ship out, but I guess it's the whole 'you get what you pay for' deal, and I accepted that. But I kept trying other sources just because I was hopeful I'd find some decent filament for a lower price. When Amazon started to consistently carry Sainsmart under Prime, I gave it a whirl. I'd tried Octave mostly through amazone before, because it was the only one I saw regularly that was covered under Prime. Octave is CRAP. I had sooo much trouble with it, so for a long time I didn't bother trying to find abs through amazon. I cam back and saw Sainsmart here and decided to try it - it's wonderful. Colors are a bit more limited and less vibrant, but the quality is great. It extrudes smooth, rarely clogs the nozzles, and there's very little carbon buildup on the outside of the nozzle too. The prints come out great with no weird little discolorations, or white cracked specks like I was getting with Octave. Nothing stringy or gooey or weird. Just smooth clean extrusion. I'd say the quality is just as good as Makerbot's filament, but with Amazon prime, there's free shipping, and it shows up in two days, instead of two weeks. I consider this a huge score. I definitely hope Amazon continues to consistently stock Sainsmart. It's good stuff.

When I first stumbled across Mountain Faith on the Daystar TV show, Great American Gospel, I was instantly captivated by the tight bluegrass picking! I immediately bought their first cd, Save Me, which became my favorite musical purchase of last year. It bumped such performers as The Isaacs, Sierra Hull, and even Allison Krauss Union Station down a notch on my playlist and became the most-played on my daily commute. I was very excited to know they would have a sophomore release with Battlefield, and just had my first listen, hoping it would at least be as good as it's predecessor. Wow! Did I underestimate my expectations! Battlefield will go straight to the top of my rotation as my NEW favorite! Everything about this talented family-based bluegrass gospel group from NC is just so appealing! Musically, they are second to none, exhibiting terrific skill on each instrument. They do not settle into a comfortable format, either, really stretching their talents to incorporate a wide range from slick bluegrass flatpicking to swing, with even quartet hymn-singing thrown in for good measure. But the best part of this whole album is the crystalline vocals of Summer McMahan, especially on the title track and the poignant "I Will Praise Him". Summer won me over with Save Me, as I heard the same qualities in her voice as we heard in a younger Allison Krauss, like back in those Forget About It days. I don't know how she did it in such a short time, but Summer's voice has matured and is just so captivating, she is quickly becoming my favorite female vocalist! Another beautiful moment on Battlefield is the final instrumental reprise of "I Will Praise Him" with it's haunting viola, steel guitar work and surprising subdued synthdrum and shaker rhythm. Mountain Faith is not afraid to stretch the boundaries, and they are winning fans and souls on this Battlefield for our Lord!

This is a review of the Nino Percussion NINO570 Small Wood Tone Block with Natural Finish. Mallet measures 6 long handle and then the ball on the end is approx. 1 round. The percussion T portion has a 5 handle leading up to the top and the top extends another 1 with a width of 5 5 8. Summary - My daughter is taking snare lessons in school and wood tone blocks have become a needed item for her in order for her to continue with the various lessons. I was frustrated as I needed only a wood block with a low and high tone for her to do what she needed. Almost all the listings I found were at a very high cost with more blocks and larger setup than I needed. Or if not overkill in nature, they would be only a toddler aged toy. This was perfect and is both small and easy to use. I am going with four of 5 stars as the mallet I received was warped-bent. Prior to purchasing the Sonicare I had a handful of Braun Oral-B power toothbrushes. I liked them enough, but after giving the Sonicare a try I know I will not be going back to Oral-B anytime soon. The Elite e9500 includes a nice travel case, a regular and compact brush head, a timer, 2-speeds, and a battery charge status display. My favorite feature is the customizable brushing timer. I set my for a 2 30 brush. It pulses every 30 seconds to let me know to move onto another section of my mouth, and when the "dentist recommend" 2 minutes are up I use the remaining 30 seconds to lower the speed and massage my gums. The only negative for this model Sonicare is that some kind of slimy gunk builds up in the head of the brush. The first time it fell out I was both grossed out and fascinated by it I need to get out more... I know . However, if you simply unscrew the top and rinse it out with water then give it a quick bang against the sink that problem goes away. All in all... It may be expensive, but keeping your teeth healthy is important.

La Roux, the synth-pop duo out of the UK compile and mix a CD full of vintage and modern dance tracks. Synth pop, italo disco, freestyle, and current electro all rolled into one. This is very much like mixes from the DJ Kicks series DJ Kicks , DJ Kicks , DJ Kicks , where artists who often do not normally DJ, choose a list of their favorite classic and new songs. Just like the DJ Kicks series, La Roux does a cover of a classic. Here, it's the Rolling Stones' Under My Thumb, which she turns into a playful, bouncy synth pop tune, in typical La Roux fashion. This track is currently exclusive to this mix. Just don't expect a slick DJ mix, as it's basically a simple transition between the tracks. There are a few weak tracks, but overall, the tracks are solid from end to end. There was one previous release in the Sidetracked series, nbsp Sidetracked , which featured more 90's tracks than 80's, and also came with a 2nd, unmixed disc. They seemed to abandon the two disc format, but let's hope this mix series continues regardless.

I own a few laptops for doing audio production and deejaying on-location. The UCA-222 interface acts as an external sound card without dealing with 1 8" connectors and the typically noisy on-board sound cards in laptops. Once I connect the UCA-222 via USB, the computer recognizes it after a few seconds. IMPORTANT! You MUST reconfigure any audio players or software to be routed through the UCA-222!!! Once you do, the difference is incredible. I own three UCA-222's and they are reliable and have never failed me. I've never used the optical connection. However, the input outputs...even though they are unbalanced RCA connectors....are dependable. NO noise floor....ultra quiet! It has an on-board headphone connector and volume control, but I never use it. I connect the laptop to the UCA-222 via USB. Then connect RCA cables from it to the mixer. Clean, line level audio.

This is the best digital caliper at this price point. I am a mechanical engineer who spent my last two semesters of college in the machine shop working with metal on a daily basis. As a machinist, measurements have to be extremely precise, so all the members of my team, including myself purchased calipers. Everyone bought a different set, not on purpose, that's just how it happened. When my set wasn't around I'd use theirs. I used nearly all the brands from the harbor freight special to Mitutoyo, including some of the more popular models on here like Neiko. These are by far my favorite caliper, and my reasoning is quality for the price. I didn't want to spend a lot on calipers so I stayed with a budget model, and I'm so glad I did. This caliper outperforms every caliper in its price range on all fronts. It belongs on a similar stages as iGaging, which is twice the price or more. This are highly accurate caliper with consistency not found in any other caliper at this price. I used the Neiko caliper and other cheap models regularly and found that each measurement was slightly off. Not the case with this set. Every reading was exactly the same. The resolution is excellent. Going down to one half of a mil is awesome, especially for the price you pay for these. The caliper is built solidly and has an incredibly smooth slide. I received many compliments on how expensive my calipers felt from fellow machine shop workers. T Sadly, I misplaced my calipers when I moved and am in need of a new set. Luckily I have this set to fall back on and luckily it's so cheap that I can afford to replace them easily. The only other caliper I'd consider replacing it with would be iGaging's absolute orgin model, which maintains it's calibration of the zero. Quality and precision wise, these calipers are similar, but the absolute zero option is really nice. iGaging's model is 3 times the price, so I'll stick with the cheaper model and deal with zeroing manually.

I purchased this as a replacement to a standard Fender single coil on my American Strat which came in an HSH configuration. I also replaced the neck Humbucker with a Hot Gold double, and the bridge with the overwound Hot Gold double. My intent was to get the guitar roughly equivalent to the output of my Les Paul Standard which came with Burstbuckers but retain some of the key versatility of the sounds available through the strat. It came out pretty close, but more importantly were a few nice surprises that came with this configuaration. Apparently the design of the Lace pickups are such that the magnetic field is better confined than on a standard Fender pickup. This gave me a lot more sustain, but more important was the clarity. Even my bandmates commented on how clear the sound was even when overdriven. So even though it didn't match up exactly to the output of the Les Paul, it really didn't matter because the clarity is such that it cuts through the mix without the volume...and that's what I'd prefer anyway. You're not going to get the mid-scooped growl you would get from a Les Paul with Burstbuckers, but then I wasn't looking for a replacement to the Les Paul. Each guitar now has it's own space for different types of songs, but I don't need to make any adjustments when I swap guitars.

I have been playing flute for five years, and piccolo on and off for about three. Being a high school student, I've been forced to use only the terrible piccolos provided to me by the school, and I finally decided to get my own. This is not the most amazing piccolo in the world. If you want an exceptional one for concert band symphony playing- go buy a wood piccolo. For marching band, it is very good if it's for marching band, I suggest buying the lacquer, because it is probably best in weather conditions. But talk to your band director first to see if he she wants all the piccolos to be silver to match the flutes . It is satisfactory for beginners and intermediates. For the price especially, it is good for any young musician to experiment on before deciding to go buy a more expensive instrument. The sound is good, as long as the instrumentalist knows how to play properly. The piccolo itself looks very nice. It has an easy grip and the keys don't stick like on other piccolos I've played. Complaints The cleaning cloth doesn't fit in the piccolo, so I can't clean the inside it might be wise to purchase another cloth just for the inside , but it is good for basic cleaning. The cork isn't set quite right, but it's easy to adjust other ways for tuning purposes. I haven't taken the piccolo to be regulated yet, so I don't know if this is fixable, but it's minor. The case feels durable but the clasps are a little annoying to open. I just bought a new case. Edit Pointed out to me by another musician- the finger rest is very high up, so it really doesn't help the grip of the piccolo and it's actually rather annoying, but it's tolerable. Overall Good for beginners and intermediate players. There are a few minor issues with the piccolo but nothing that really affects performance. But considering the price and the ease with which you can fix most problems, it's probably worth it.

The organ at St. Denis basilica just north of Paris is often regarded as one of the best, if not the best, organ of its type in France. The basilica has a history that likely goes back as far as the seventh century. The current gothic architectural components, commissioned by the famous Suger and serving as the prototypical gothic design that was later exported all across France, is largely a 12th century work. Although there are records of an organ as far back as the 16th century, today's Cavaill-Coll instrument is dated to the 19th century. Pierre Pincemaille, the current organist of the basilica, is widely regarded as one of the premier organists in France. He is a recipient of numerous organ awards, including competition events, over the past twenty years. If you have ever had the fortune to witness and experience in person this man's exceptional playing, you know that Pincemaille is part of a generation of players totally devoted to his craft. He plays with skill and conviction, and his ability to "bring to life" any organ he sits at is a marvel to behold. The CD here has a good sample of pieces from a variety of composers that showcase Pincemaille's variety at the keyboard. The recording is excellent, and you will hear the sound much as it would be heard within the basilica itself. Highly recommended for organists, lovers of organ music, and those who enjoy hearing the unique sound of a massive instrument such as that found at St. Denis, don't miss out on this unusual and exceptional recording.

We do not really think of Simon Garfunkel as being a folk group, mainly because when "The Sounds of Silence" became a big hit and a signature song of the 1960s it had been electrified. But when you listen to their first album, "Wednesday Morning, 3 A.M.," it becomes clear from the opening track, "You Can Tell the World," that they were a folk duo. Of course there is the obligatory Dylan song, "The Times They Are-A Changin'," Sixties songs like "Last Night I had the Strangest Dream," and traditional songs like "Go Tell It On the Mountain" to add to the folk song bona fides of the album. In retrospect what is interesting is Paul Simon's attempt to write folk songs, a category into which "The Sounds of Silence" does not fall. Of those original songs, "He Was My Brother" is the one most in keeping with the spirit of the Sixties, while "Bleecker Street" evinces the delicate music he could write. But Simon is still learning his craft, as evidenced by "Sparrow," which tends to be a bit pretentious. Then there is Simon Garfunkle's rendition of the traditional song "Benedictus," which gets a bit electrified as well. There is a real sense that the duo are still learning their craft and just beginning to find the sound that would define them. In addition to be digitally remastered, this version of "Wednesday Morning, 3 A.M. includes a trio of bonus tracks, which consist of bare boned demo version of Simon's "Bleecker Street" and alternate takes of his "He Was My Brother" and the cover of "The Sun is Burning." But even with those additions this remains an interesting but not great debut album by a duo that would continue to improve with each and every album they put out until they reached the pinnacle with "Bridge Over Troubled Water" and then called it quits when they were literally on top of the music world.

Assuming your shells and lugs are true, it's all about the hoops and heads. And here, outside of obviously too loose or tight to choke point, it's all pretty much subjective. There's a combined sound and feel you're looking for and sometimes it's a long road that gets travelled to find it. Hopefully, you get lucky and the trip doesn't get too long and or expensive. Reading reviews can be helpful but, again, I can only tell you what worked for me. Drum Tama Starphonic 1mm steel shell, 13 x 7, used, great drum, by the way. worth every penny. Head at purchase Remo Powerstroke, clear - worn out, flat and generally lame IMO Go to snare head tried Evans Genera Dry HD - at any tightness it choked the poor old Tama to death! Second try Evans EC Reverse Dot - Bingo! Great rebound, sensitivity and shotgun pop, too! It should be noted I tried this EC on both a Pearl, Limited Edition, 13 x 7 chrome over steel drum and a Tama SLP G-Maple, 13-ply, 13 x 7 with truly lack luster results. However, the Genera worked great on these two. Go figure. Best of luck.

These worked great for my application. A Gate to my backyard. They are surprisingly sturdy. I had to whack the crap out of them to get them to go into the ground all the way, but it was WAYYYY less effort than digging a hole that would produce the same sturdiness. Saved me a lot of time. The clamping mechanism could be better, but for the price compared to the others I looked at on Amazon, I don't think you can beat the value of this one. A few extra screws, and if needed, just drill a hole in opposite side and you will have a very sturdy base. NOTE I did have to purchase a full sized sledge to knock this sucker in. How I rate something When I review an item, I try to always do so in context of the product and its description. For example, if I bought a vase that said shatter proof, and then drop it on the ground and it shatters, that is bad. But if I purchased a crystal wine glass, and knocked it over on the counter and it broke, I would not say "these glasses are not very sturdy". I believe, when possible, in reviewing items based on what is available in the market. Sometimes the market simply does not have better options. So if the product is the best in its class, I think it deserves 5 stars even if you can "poke holes" in their design or materials. I want to help you pick the best item available COMPARED to all other items that fill the same user requirements. The "Girl Group Sound" which involves female-dominated or led groups and solo vocalist from their early to mid-1960s heyday has become so popular amongst collectors that CDs abound covering almost all of them, from the famous to the not-so-famous, as well as in series such as Where The Girls Are and Early Girls from Ace of London. Sadly, to date anyway, there has been no such compilation offering the music of Rebecca Becky Page, sister Joanna, song-writing mom Rickie and her sister, Susie Kuykendoll, an aggregation that functioned in the late 1950s early to mid-1960s under so many different names that few realized then - and now - they were one and the same. So it's difficult to figure out just how many records they actually cut. Their one claim to national fame in terms of a charted song was the April-May 1964 62 Billboard Pop Hot 100 Donnie which came out as Era 3125 b w Chu Sen Ling, and the hit side may be found in Volume 5 of the Ace series Early Girls. The rest, some of which are scattered here and there throughout Amazon as 45-rpm discs under varying names as listed in the Comments below, have never to my knowledge, appeared in a quality CD with the exceptions of White Levi's and Chu Sen Ling which are part of the 1996 Phantom Sound Vision CD, Surf Bunnies Hot Rod Honeys - but it's now out of circulation. Their one charted song may not seem like much of an accomplishment on the surface, but consider that it came right in the midst of the opening stages of the British Invasion when the likes of The Beatles, Rolling Stones, et al weren't leaving a whole lot of room on the charts for ANY North American artist. And when you hear them you realize they were every bit as talented as many that had far greater commercial success, but who also had the benefit of recording for labels with much more funding for promotion and distribution. Troy Records, for example, was a small label run by Rickie's husband George Motola. Yes, some of their sides did come out on major labels like Epic and the Motown subsidiary V.I.P., but by then the British Invasion was simply too much with which to contend. The sides listed in the Comments plus whatever other material of theirs can be found would make a great compilation for those of us who would be delighted to add it to our collection.

Very nice fit for the 2000 Fender American Deluxe Power Telecaster I picked up secondhand about a week ago. The Tele came with a molded fender case, but the tiny storage space it has barely held a snark tuner, a tiny Phillips screwdriver, a spare 9v battery and two spare sets of strings - the flap was bulging up a bit just from those few items. I play out, so I need a capo, string winder, strap, nail file and clipper, clean cloth to wipe down my guitar, etc within easy reach. I have three Strats that came with the Fender branded SKB rectangle cases, which seem to be essentially identical to this one...I tried the fit of my Tele in one of them before ordering this guy and it fit perfectly. The guitar has a tiny bit of extra room, as Strats are slightly larger, but a Tele doesn't move at all when the lid is closed. The storage space inside is currently accommodating a thick Levy's leather strap with locks on it, just folded over, 5 sets of Daddario exp strings, 2 capos, 2 spare batteries, a winder cutter, nail care items, a few little plastic containers of different gauge picks and a microfiber cloth below the headstock, the neck support has it raised enough that there is plenty of room . I would certainly trust this case in my car or a friends suv, at home, out at gigs, no problem at all. If I were to fly with a guitar that I'd be very upset to have broken I'd use the next model up from SKB, with the wheels and four latches. I have one of them as of now and I don't see any way a Tele Strat could be damaged in it, even if one were to try. Also keep in mind that all SKB cases include a lifetime warranty for the original purchaser - one of the latches on one of my Strat cases broke and they sent me parts to repair it at their own cost. They did offer the option of sending it in to them for repair but it was an easy repair that I was comfortable doing. You can't go wrong with the quality and customer service that SKB offers.

I run a small company and we do things a lot of other small companies don't do. I needed a scale that could measure out to 800lb. Obviously, this scale is only rated to 440lb, and I figured I would install a double-pulley system to multiply the weight to get up to 900lb capacity. My scale was delivered today and I was off to the shop to measure the strength of a steel cable , without the pulley system. I was aware I was not going to perform an 800lb pull test and I knew I should stop at around 400lb. I hung the scale on my crane hook and tared it to 0.0. Then attached the test cable between the scale and the crane's base. As I lifted the crane-arm, the display went up, nice and steady. Once I hit 340lb, the display said OL overload in most applications . Because I can't get absolute precision positioning on a hydraulic crane, I will assume the scale maxed out at 350lb, instead of the 440lb the scale is rated to. I performed this test 3 times today and the results were the same. I still give this scale 3-stars because it does seem to work nicely, very smooth, compact, and CHEAP. My other option is to go with an industrial 1000lb scale which would cost me around 900 at Grainger. I would like it to measure up to the weight it is rated to, but I hardly have requirements to weigh anything or tension too often, but I need it for a current project and I can't wait for a replacement. The "fix" is nothing I can't work through, and since I need to install a pulley system to multiply the weight, instead of a 2 1 pulley system, I'll just install a 3 1 pulley system - not a huge deal. I bought these because I needed a pair of earbuds to see live music at a bar-- but they kinda let me down in that regard. The product does not completely seal inside my left ear, and so some sound still comes through. Also, The amount of sound that is reduced is not enough. Your ears will still ring if you're in an enclosed space where you're drowning in the sound of bass guitars, drums, and heavy metal shrills. That said, these are good to dull out day to day noise. I live in a city where I commute on the train and wore them from home to the office. I was able to listen out for my stop on the train, and hear things like cars honking, but it took the "sharp" metal noises that you hear when you're riding the train every day. I also sit under a HVAC unit all day which drums on for about 8 months throughout the year, so it's nice to pop these in and still be able to hear someone call my name from across the office. Personally, for me iit's easier to concentrate on what I'm doing without all the white noise. I had a coupon and only paid 10 for these, which is appropriate. If I had to spend 20 - 25 on them, I'd probably just buy some quality ear buds and use them to dampen sound instead.

I've tried a lot of strings in 30 years of playing and keep coming back to the D'Addario XL. Used them exclusively for the last 2 years and see no reason to change brands. I've never broken a D'Addario XL string and I use a lot of extreme bends. Of course if you're into thrashing your strings, all bets are off no matter what brand you choose. These nickel wounds are fairly bright out of the box but mellow nicely after a couple hours playing. The XL set will handle various altered tunings. Open D, open G, etc no problem. One caveat with the XL and very light gauge strings in general -- make sure your neck is good and straight or you will get fret buzz on hard attacks, especially if you tune them down. With a regular cleaning these strings will brighten right back up to "nearly new" sound, which for my taste is just right. They will retain good intonation for a long time with a little care. Not too fat, not too skinny, the D'addario XL are good all around strings for articulate playing. Highly recommended.

Remember, this is the probably the cheapest mandolin on the internet. And ALL instruments like this need significant set up before they can be enjoyably played. With those two caveats, this is one of the best music purchases I've ever made. I really think this package is the way to go. You will want quality strings, which you get here. The case is really well padded. The zipper sticks in one spot but otherwise very solid. And the strap is pretty much perfect, a nice piece of leather that goes on well and easily adjusts. The mandolin itself, well I read a lot of reviews before I bought this, but was still pleasantly surprised. Construction and finish had no flaws that I could find. You could have told me this was a 300 instrument and I'd believe you. Once set up, volume is good, holds tune well, I have no fret buzz and sweet action. It seriously plays as well as anything I've tried maybe a half dozen mandos scattered over many different places . Key is a good set up. I'm not a luthier and don't really service my guitars, but I took the time to read what to do and follow the included instructions. Honestly, the whole thing took me about two hours. First I inspected the finish and construction, then carefully examined the fretboard. All good. Then I tightened all the little phillips screws in case any were loose. I took the strings off, put lemon oil on the fretboard, lubricated the tuners very important , blew some sawdust out of the f-holes. Then I spent a lot of time on the bridge fitting it. What is fitting it? Match up the bridge approximately where it's going to be. Look at the bridge from the base of the instrument, looking for gaps. Then sand or file until the gaps all disappear and the bridge is perfectly fit. Before putting the strings on I lemon oiled the bridge both it and the fretboard were really dry . After reading at least a dozen people say the nut was too high, I was going to lower the nut by removing it and filing it down. But mine was really stuck in there and I didn't want to damage my new instrument. I'm glad I didn't file it down, because when the strings came up to tension the action was fine. I also spent a lot of time getting the intonation right. It was my first time dialing an instrument in and the bridge kept wanting to drift toward one of the f-holes. I also kept checking the intonations with every string I put on. When I was done, I couldn't believe how good it sounded. It does not sound cheap! Can't yet speak to the longevity, but to learn on I am over the moon with how great this has been. 100 recommended.

This guy is kind of a cult favorite among serious music fans. Not many know about him, but he's been discovered by Pearl Jam, KEXP, and others. Look him up on You Tube. I am transitioning to using stab in connectors and this 8 port made for a neat and easy connection in a 3 gang box with 5 neutral wires connected together 5 wires in one connector and 5 ground wires with 2 branching off total of 7 in one connector plus the hot wires. So this was my original criticism Have you ever covered a school textbook in clear contact paper? That's what my mom used to do way back when! Or perhaps a cabinet you wanted to change the appearance of? Or shelving you wanted to make clean so you covered it with contact? Well, believe it or not this fairly attractive dispenser is only a plastic box - and not a particularly strong feeling plastic box - covered in dotted contact paper! To be fair, we didn't realize it at first. Then we looked closely and saw the air bubbles!! It wasn't even covered properly! We immediately returned the item as defective for not properly covering the box . But seriously, like at some point I wouldn't have scratched the surface when grabbing a paper towel? and then be left with a tear in the contact paper. Right. This is a cheaply made product and I recommend you stay away. At first we thought a few extra bucks for the satin stainless steel dispenser was too much, but not after seeing this - and taking note of the price we paid for it! Go with something far more durable metal. But, after working with Tripp's Customer Service, I purchased 2 of their paper towel holders - this and the granite one. My opinion now is a simple one This is a paper towel holder worth getting. The dot design is colorful and blends nicely into our kitchen, as it would in several other rooms that are done in neutral tones. A few general comments about ALL the TrippNT Paper Towel Holders of this shape design we own two! 1. Mounting The holder is easily mounted on almost any surface Tripp is good enough to include screws and anchors which are great for plaster or sheetrock walls . We mounted one with heavy-duty Velcro. We weren't sure exactly where we wanted the holder so we decided to try it in one place with Velcro. Found that the Velcro holds the fully loaded holder - so we didn't need to make holes anyway! Viola! 2. Filling Dispensing With its open top it could not be easier to load the holder with paper towels. I realize that an open top is not everyone's cup of tea, but, all I can say is - Get over it! Seriously, if you go through paper towels in your house or garage as much as we do, having the open top is of absolutely no consequence. It's not like the paper is sitting there for weeks on end and will get dirty. As for dispensing, the opening is sized perfectly to release a single towel. The only time a few towels came out at the same time was when the paper towels themselves were crimped together by the paper towel manufacturer - it had nothing to do with the holder. Customer Service Because of my earlier negative experience I had contacted TrippNT and found that their Customer Service Department was responsive and genuinely concerned about my satisfaction. And not just concerned, but concerned enough that THE Customer Service representative who handled my concerns was THE Customer Service representative who I communicated with throughout until the issue was resolved. I knew her name, her email, her job title and her telephone number. THAT is the definition of Customer Service sorry, Amazon, that beats even you! Because I had the pleasure of dealing with a single person I did not have to re-tell my story each time I contacted Tripp. In fairness though, I only contacted Tripp twice and then only because I had additional questions. The representative actually resolved my issue with her first email to me. Can someone PLEASE tell Verizon about this type of Customer Service??? Sorry, that's another story.... In conclusion - buy it. TrippNT makes so many different designs and colors, this is well worth the cost and I didn't explain how these paper towels are so much more cost-effective when compared to the round rolls most of us purchase. We are not at all sorry we moved on to the folded paper towels!!! This purchase has me Not-So-Cranky-24 7.

I will start off by saying that my expectations were not very high. I have been playing guitar off and on for the last 10 years. I am not the type to spend a bunch of money on something if I can find a good value deal. I took a chance on this guitar because it was on sale and I had some Amazon credit kicking around. My other acoustic guitar is a Fullerton Dreadnought that I bought from music123 when they were clearing them out a number of years ago. If I had known how much I would like the tone on such a bargain basement priced instrument, I would have purchased a couple of them. Anyway, the bridge started to lift and I needed something quick and cheap to be able to plug in for a live gig. That is where this Starcaster with pickup came in. The Good The general tone of this guitar is fairly clean and I haven't experienced the tuning issues that others have. That said, the tone is also fairly shallow with minimal bottom end and no discernible richness in the low-mids. The Bad Fit and finish on these guitars is poor to say the least. The fret edges are sharp and the neck coloring rubs off a bit on your fingers. The intonation of the guitar is very poor. If you are going to play down the neck do not expect things to sound quite right. The action is also very high. I like to play Elixir nano-web Mediums because of the balance and richness they generally add, but with the action so high on the guitar it was unbearable. It's not even worth the effort and money to take it to my luthier and get things cleaned up. Conclusion I will keep it as a knock about guitar and let my kids play around with it, but it will not be joining me for any more live gigs if I can help it. I will keep playing my repaired Fullerton and maybe pick up something else as a backup when the right deal shows up.

I received this as a gift a few months ago. I loaded the software, plugged the turntable into my computer had to assemble some parts, but it is pretty straight-forward and started playing my music. The sound is great and the recording very easy. The software - audacity - is fairly simple to use, but you have to research the instructions on the internet because they do not come with the software disk. They are readily available though. The turntable is pretty solid. It has what look like shock absorbing feet as you see on high quality electronic components. The turntable is aluminum not plastic with a rubber mat that sits on it to protect the LP. It has a much-needed dust cover which some do not come with. There are three buttons up down, start and stop. The start button lifts the arm, starts the table turning and gently sets the needle on the record. Stop does the reverse. Up down, simply raises or gently lowers the needle. There is never a need to touch the arm of the turntable, which is a major benefit to your records by minimizing the likelihood of your causing scratches by setting down or lifting the needle manually. The arm does return automatically at the end. Great product and would highly recommend.

We got this out as soon as the box showed up. My kids ok, me too! thought it was neat to watch the lights change color and spin around on the walls and ceiling. After a few minutes, I snuck away to find a thumb drive and music. I found one song on an old USB style mp3 player and it kept us entertained for a bit longer, until I told them we could pack it up and take it to our cousins house. There, 7 kids between 2 and 8 were entertained for 45 minutes with them still asking for more. The music stopped and switched to my husband speaking which was recorded on the same mp3 player , and the kids were still entranced. I did get several more songs loaded and they enjoyed that even more. The light part is about the size of a soccer ball. It is a domed top with a black plastic bottom. There is a silver section that has a spot for an SD card and a USB device, with buttons underneath for Previous Track Volume Down, Play Pause, and Next Track Volume Up. When plugged in, it lights up right away with little dots of color all around the room, and rings of color directly over the light maker. The lights flash from one to another and spin around. It can make you a little dizzy, in a fun way. It also starts the music up automatically in a couple seconds, with the lights changing according to the music. The remote requires 2 AAA batteries not included , and controls the music only. There are several buttons for Play Pause, Mode I assume this switches from SD card to USB , Previous Track, Next Track, Volume up, and Volume Down. Unfortunately, the second day I went to unplug my mp3 player and the whole section that allowed the light to play music fell too far into the machine to reach it. I am disappointed that it no longer plays music for me, but the light part is still really fun, and I can play the music on another speaker, probably with better sound. I have used a bluetooth speaker next to the light, or under the light, and both ways, the light still changes with the music. I wasn't able to retest this because the music part broke, but I think the Previous Track button doesnt restart the song, but truly goes to the previous track. Sound is fine. Good enough for my at home dance party purposes. The light part is still really fun. The kids don't care if their music is coming out of the light itself or out of a different speaker, even if it's in a different part of the room. DISCLAIMER I was fortunate enough to receive a this at a discount in exchange for my feedback. These are 100 my own thoughts and opinions.

Let me start out by saying that I'm an electric player who has always hated playing acoustics, even while loving the sound. I've always felt that they were too big, too awkward, and too hard to play with their heavy strings and high action, and boxy body that digs into my rib cage. This guitar has solved all of those problems for me. I had a chance to play a friend's Mini Martin, and I loved the size, comfort, and playability, but the tone just wasn't there in a 3 4 scale. The GS Mini sounds like a great full-sized guitar, and is only a little bit larger, still very comfortable. The neck is fantastic and the action feels more like an electric--I can even bend, within reason. Both unplugged and amplified tones are ridiculously good. The world of acoustic playing is now open to me, and I'm loving it. I can't recommend this guitar highly enough--just buy one and thank me later.

If I were writing this review solely on the performance of the Yamaha Stagepas 400i I would give it 5 stars. The sound is fantastic. I bought this PA to be used in my magic show, along with a wireless microphone system and a powered subwoofer. I needed something that would play to small to medium sized venues. It needed to be able to amplify my voice, pre-recorded music, and sound effects. The unit performs flawlessly. I paired the Yamaha 400i with a Behringer B 1200D powered subwoofer. I have used this combo successfully at outdoor events for 150 to 200 people. The built in crossover preserves the limited amplifier capabilities of the 400i and allows the B 1200D to do the heavy lifting. Without the addition of the powered sub this system would NOT have been powerful enough to amplify both voice and dynamic music, perhaps at indoor events or for smaller crowds. Here's the rub. Yamaha claims the 400i is a 400 watt system. If you read the fine print you discover that's 400 watts peak power. At less than 1 distortion it only has 125 watts per channel and that's into a 4 ohm load!! Yamaha claims that they increased the power of the 400i over its predecessor the stagepas 300 when all they really did was lower the impedance of the speakers from 6 ohms to 4 ohms. Again this system sounds fantastic. When coupled with a powered sub, It is killer. For those who are trying to choose between the 400i and the 600i I would suggest that the 200.00 difference between the two will go a long way towards a good sub. I guess I'm just disappointed in Yamaha. I would have expected them to have been more honest in their power ratings. Maybe I'm expecting too much.

OK, I'm not a drummer, I'm a bass player. But some of Gibraltar's stuff are useful to us folks who don't necessarily BANG on things hehe . I recently purchased a Tascam DP-008EX 8-Track Digital Recorder, and a Tech 21 SansAmp Bass Driver DI sorry, NOT from Amazon . I needed something that would put these devices within arm's reach. The SansAmp is SUPPOSED to be a "stomp box", but there's a definite need to tweak it from time to time. I didn't want to be bending over every time it needed adjustment very undignified, especially at a GIG. An item like this is REALLY hard to find. I mean, if ya wanna Google it, just WHAT do ya enter? I found a cheap sheet metal thing online, but NOBODY who'd bought it liked it, and it looked like you could cut yourself on the edges no covering whatsoever. Ya might even put yer eye out! A drummer friend of mine turned my onto this item. Good thing, because if you do look through Gibraltar's entire website, you'll never find it IT'S NOT THERE. It IS in their GIBRALTAR'S CATALOG, which I suggest you DOWNLOAD PDF and keep on-hand. Anyway, it's quite a useful item. For MY needs, it'll hold BOTH my Tascam and my SansAmp at the same time! And unlike the "cheepie" other tray I mentioned, it will fit on either the UPPER or LOWER section of a microphone stand. The folks who reviewed the "other" one said it would not open wide enough to clamp onto the lower section, which meant if ya loaded it up with anything too heavy, it would become be top heavy, and prone to tip over. You WON'T have that problem with the Gibraltar. For years now, I've had a Gibraltar "mic-clamping" beverage holder, so I didn't need to be "sold" on Gibraltar's quality, and I could see from the illustration that the clamp was identical to my cupholder, so it was a "no-brainer" once I saw the thing. The one limitation is that IT WON'T TILT only lays flat. So I'm not sure how good a music stand it would make, but I think a "clamp-on" one of THOSE might be easier to find. It's well-made, STABLE, and I love it! I had a need where NOTHING but this type of item would satisfy. If you have a similar need, THIS is the device for YOU. larrythereviewer .

I purchased my Dot Studio new from a Guitar Center. Once in every other blue moon, a guitarist picks up a guitar and something magical happens the guitar just feels "right" in his hands and body. That is the only way I can describe my experience with my Dot. A couple of things need to be understood here. 1 I am not an Epiphone kind of guy. I have always gravitated to Fenders or custom luthier made instruments I built a few and fix a pile of stringed instruments . 2 I am definitely not a "Made in China" kind of guy either. Yes, the Epi Dot Studio is made,.... there. 3 The Dot Studio finish worn brown, worn cherry and worn brown burst suck. Not that the finish is applied poorly, it's just too Early American furniture looking for me. Having said that, I can bypass all the negativity about this guitar based on "how" I buy guitars. I buy them because they feel and play well. Call me strange, but I still like people with character rather than beauty, prestige, or money. I like my guitars the same way. The Dot Studio has excellent sound and playability "character". This guitar answers it all. It is simple to use with just a single volume and single tone control and a single switch to excite the hot bridge pickup, warmer neck pickup, or combination of the two. You can dial in a ton of tone for just about any kind of music you play. The price allows the collector or rather, hoarder to buy just one more. It also gives the beginner a quality instrument. This is so important. I cannot tell you how many people I've spoke to that quit learning because they bought a cheap, hard to play instrument. For the working musician, a few minor upgrades will make this your number 1 Blues machine. Oh, one more problem with this guitar. It won't leave my hands.

While the product delivered is quite good quality, the image paired with it online is not accurate. The picture for this product shows two bends in the tool, one that angles upward and another that angles downward to the point, and the forceps delivered are straight with a single curve to the point. My high school teacher had a pair of Carolina forceps with the two angles like the image presented, and I wanted to buy a pair for myself for college labs. They were very easy to use and, because of the extra angle, were able to reach into small areas during dissections without having to bend my wrist in odd positions. They were especially good for delicate brain dissections because of their fine point. Don't get me wrong, these are very good forceps. They have a good, fine point, and are sturdy. My only complaints are that they do not match the image presented, and are not as easy on my wrist as the pair I was expecting.

I have just come from my first live performance of this piece, which I consider the definItive version of the PASSION ACCORDING TO SAINT MARK. Better than Bach's, sorry. I have THE first version, which I have not only memorized but has inspired quite a few pages of poetry. But, as I sat there, I realized to my surprise, Golijov has made some substantial changes to it since it was first recorded. Listening to a piece, no matter how many times, does not give you a its true measure as a live performance can. Some highlights 1. Golijov does away with your standard operatic practice. All the principals, regardless of gender, sing every solo role. It gave me shivers to watch Jessica Rivera and Luciana Souza sing Jesus. 2. Golijov utilizes the full panoply of Brazilian percussion instruments. There is even a solo for drums and cellos! 3. The chorus--and there were four different groups--, 250 hormonal adolescents on stage--does NOT stand still. They shout, cry clap and stop as in flamenco zapateado, in a most chilling entrance to the crucifixion. 4. There is a full Casta Diva, or Rusalka's Song to the Moon, in Brazilian Portuguese. But there are also more than mere nods to jazz, Cuban Brazilian Santeria, and plainchant. 5. Perez Prado, Verdi, Tito Puente, Bach, Ella Fitzgerald. Desacralization of the Classical Music urban legend. 6. Languages Spanish, Portuguese, Latin, Aramaic. Final comment It brought me back to my multiple roots. It restored my faith in what I cannot rationally believe in. I even realized death is the culmination, not the end of life. But you have to watch it, live or on DVD.

David Paton, best known as a member of the group Pilot, was without peer as a pop music craftsman. It used to be called pop-rock. This CD is apparently comprised mostly of rerecorded tunes from an out-of-print Paton CD, with a couple of songs added. The Search is marketed by a small label and is not cheap. But for those who enjoy prime pop, it is worth the money and the wait. It's a great antidote to the vulgar and musically incoherent popular music of today. Also, Paton and Ian Bairnson still record as Pilot, under whose name they recently released an excellent CD, "Blue Yonder." I'm amazed that they still do such impeccable work after so many years. They bring back the excitement of hearing a song with a fantastic hook for the first time.

First off, if you're looking for a different sound with this harmonica, don't be fooled. It uses the same reeds as the other MS harps, although the stock reed thickness is thicker in this one compared to the lesser-priced harmonicas like the blues harp MS. Replacement reeds, however, are the same for all Hohner MS harps the thicker version is standard . That said, holding the thing is a dream. The case extends to the edge of the comb on both sides and is both thicker and molded instead of "folded" so there are less air leaks. This makes it able to be played with less force and in turn bends are easier. It feels like it is part of your hand. The all metal construction adds a little weight but it feels right--very comfortable in fact. Despite the toy-like impression the photos give, the brushed look of the comb mixed with the chrome shell gives a very impressive look to the instrument. The stock reeds are chromed, and this does not add much to the sound but it looks nice and the front is rounded and very smooth feeling on the mouth. Sorry to burst the bubble here but this is in no way a four, let alone five star soundtrack. All the reviews I've read have talked about this score being one of the best soundtracks in a long time...', a great display of John Williams-esque orchestration and bombast...'. I'm sorry but not in my view. I would say this score is adequate and I can't help but feel that there has been a motive on the parts of critics to encourage more orchestral works into the video game territory, hence the high ratings. Some cues are indeed reminiscent of John Williams Rescuing The G3 Officer, Panzer Attack and there are some notable themes weaving their way throughout but the album does not at all develop them well Merker's Salt Mine is just the same melody played over and over again - unforgivable and the harmonies and orchestrations are often minimal to an extent that I feel John Williams would be quite ashamed of. There are so many tutti passages which in my view indicates either a lack of being able to write ongoing multiple lines of counterpoint or downright laziness and there are quite a few passages of drama where there are just strings and brass playing that any composer worth his salt would realise needed some woodwind to just make the effects fuller and more interesting. Unforgivably, there are also a lot of downright cheesy Om pah pah' moments - just witness the really quite dreadful opening of Rjuken Sabotage - which might be rousing for engrossed gamers but certainly detracts from an enjoyable separate listening experience. I'm aware I've focused on the negative points - there are moments of really good music The Jet Aircraft Facility is particularly notable - but that's because I forked out 16 for an album that had been given a good review from Movie Music UK a site whose reviews I have a lot of faith in and others and which is really now a non entity in my soundtrack collection. I remember the music accompanying games such as Goldeneye and Turok being far more effective and they didn't even have an orchestra...

I'm a professional classical violinist, and I busk regularly around Chicago. I mostly play with music minus one backing tracks of string quartets and piano, but I also play duets with another talented violinist. I've bought a couple of Behringer MPA-40bt pros, and used them a few years for busking. While I enjoyed the sound of the Behringers, they are mono, and they cannot get loud enough before ugly distortion sets in. Distortion is also a problem as they are running out of juice. Plus, their 12v batteries barely hold a charge after the first year or so, and all replacement batteries I've tried don't play nearly as long as the original. So I decided to try this Roland BA330. I've taken it out busking a few times now I've already made back what I've spent on this amp , and let me tell you, it is everything that Behringer isn't! Ultra clean sound with more bass, and very usable effects and mixing capabilities. And the volume, wow. I originally placed this amp behind me with the master, channel, and di box volumes at half of max, and I cannot stand the sound pressure level. My ears start ringing! There is absolutely no distortion at these levels, but the raw volume is too much for me. I now place this amp to the side of me while busking, which is plenty loud for me and much louder for the crowd in front of it. People downtown state that they can hear me a block away, and that is through rush hour traffic. How much louder do you expect a battery powered amp to get??? I do have it up at ear level on a speaker stand. For some reason I have to turn the aux in channel level almost all the way up to match my violin's channel volume, but I am playing through a Baggs DI box preamp, so that makes sense. My backing tracks have a little reverb on them, and it sounds great to be able add some to my live instrument to match that wonderful sound, while still sounding fully acoustic. I'm using Powermax rechargeable 2700 mah batteries I purchased from Amazon, and they last over 2 sessions over 8 hours, and I run it loud . Amazing. I took other reviewers advice and bought an On Stage reversible speaker stand, the Roland is a perfect fit for the large end, whereas to was a loose fit on my Pyle speaker stand even with an adapter. I also bought a custom cover for it from Connie's Covers on ebay for 45, which fits and protects this amp perfectly. I'm confident I now have the best classical busking rig possible. Great product, Roland.

I have been SO interested in these 3D printing pens for almost a year now, so I couldn't pass this up. This is a nice quality 3D pen- it has survived near-daily use by myself AND my two children. I would recommend adult supervision if a child is using it, solely because the tip gets very hot in order to melt the filament. This comes with instructions for use that are clear and concise, so you're not overwhelmed with confusing directions. There are two buttons on the side- one to feed the filament in, and one that backs the filament up to stop. There is also a sliding button on the opposite side that you can use to adjust the thickness of the filament coming out. It came with a few different colors of filament so that you can play and create your art immediately. The filament feeds into a small hole on the top of the pen near where the power cord is, so it keeps the filament out of the way and feeding smoothly. Once it's plugged in and on, it only takes maybe a minute or two to fully heat up and be ready to go. I love this pen so much! My kids and I have had so much fun creating drawings that they can actually pick up and play with! I was so impressed with this that I ordered a whole set of new filament with tons of different colors, and plan to buy more filament once we run through all of what we have. This is SUCH a fun product, and I would definitely recommend it to my friends! I did receive this at a discount to test and give my unbiased opinions- hopefully my review is helpful!

This tuner tunes very reliably, even in pretty noisy conditions. I bought this for my wife as it's easy to read and use in light and dark. The metronome function is silent, so, I consider it nearly useless. If I can't hear it, it's difficult to know if I'm actually playing a note exactly on time or not. Doesn't matter to me though, as this was bought as a tuner, and not for the metronome function. The screen is easy to read, and easy to position. Two things I don't like about this tuner aside from the metronome The whole things feels kinda delicate, with the whole swivel joint, extension arm, and odd shape, I don't know how long it will last. Being thrown into random guitar case pockets, pant pockets, accidentally being sat on, doesn't seem like it'll hold up against pressure. The other thing is that the clip doesn't open up as wide as I thought it would, nor does it clamp as tightly as I thought it would. It's not as easy to put the tuner on my headstock as I thought it'd be. I usually keep my capo on my guitar's headstock too, so it's a little tricky managing both up there. When returning my capo to the headstock, I easily move the tuner out of place. The two swivel joints make it easy to re-position the screen though. I like the paint job, it's classy. I'm not even a fan of red, but this looks good. The tuner is certainly consistent, far more than most other tuners I've used. It's accuracy is enough to have my Taylor guitar sound great on stage. It saves me a lot of time. With the more accurate tuners I use, from a plugin on my laptop to a Korg guitar tuner, it usually takes me longer with a lot of interpreting to get my guitar to sound just as in tune. In a little while, I'll definitely be buying one for myself. I recommend this Snark SN-2 over the other models they offer because it's chromatic and because it has a microphone. I bought it based on reviews I read about how I can use this for my guitars acoustic and electric , and for other things like violin, trumpets, saxophone, whatever. The reviews didn't let me down.

As a seasoned musician and experienced pianist, I am very selective about my keyboards. Of course I want to experiment and experience all the big name brands to compare differences and strengths, but this past year, after trying out a Korg model and playing around with a Roland, even giving the new "professional" Casio a chance, I came back to Yamaha. This is a great model with excellent voices and, of course, a fantastic feel. The keys are sensitive and responsive in dynamic and the samples are way better on this model than the ones before. It is heavy, a little under 40 lbs, but with the carrying case it's not so bad to lug to gigs. Along with everyone else, I do think they cheaped out by not included the music stand with the keyboard, but so far, that's my only complaint - oh, and there's no record feature for layering. Other than that, it's a great, professional, wonderful-sounding instrument for any musician. Highly recommended.

Fit perfectly into the body of the control and has a nice rotatable knob to set the max temp. That is far better than the old one which required some strange and not intuitive adjusting. It could be my imagination, but it seems there is a more gradual action in selecting the temperature. The original unit was sooo sensitive and had to be adjusted by the millimeter. Action is smooth and it doesn't leak, the reason I needed it. Previously I bought a little kit with springs and seals, but this is a lot better. I think it is too expensive and I had to take a star away for that, perhaps unfairly. The manufacturer's web site is very good at helping you get the right part, so good that I reinstated the star I was going to remove. There are lots of videos regarding the installation. Turn off the water and put a washrag over the drain. I can assure you that anything you drop will bounce directly into an open drain. The work demons never sleep. The hard part can be loosening the allen screw that holds the handle on. If you are to have any chance of success you must have allen wrenches and pick the right size. Also, there is a brass ring collar that you have to unscrew and remove. Be very gentle using big arc joint pliers or a big pipe wrench as the ring will deform. I used a tool that has a strap that grabs the part evenly and gently. One of the thousand tools a homeowner will need at some point... Using big pliers or vice grips, pull on the blue part straight out. It might take some effort and wiggling around. Clean out the innards of the control with a rag, push the new cartridge in making sure the arrow on front is up. screw on the brass retaining ring. Turn the water supply on, slip the handle on without screwing it on. Turn it to hot. If it is too hot, especially if there are unaware people using the shower, slip off the handle and set the front dial to colder. Mine was set to barely warm by default, to save on lawsuits most likely and I had to set it to hotter. Then slip on the decorative tube and screw on the handle. It is normally not necessary to unscrew the escutcheon decorative plate. I had to because the brass collar thing was so deeply inset that I needed to be able to get to it.

Highly recommended, especially at the new lower price under nine hundred bucks. This is an updated version of a great printer. It is very capable and printed dual-extrusion prints out of the box. No significant complaints at all, and only minor niggles that could more honestly be described as differences in preference. One thing to note FlashForge does sell their own filament. Usually I would assume that you don't buy the manufacturer's consumables if you can avoid it because they are overpriced and of merely adequate quality. However, the ABS filament that came with the printer prints just fine, and at least at the moment on Amazon, the 1kg spools of FF filament is CHEAP, as in, just over half the price of other brands. I see absolutely no reason not to buy the FF filament. Just note, that with any filament, you must measure the ACTUAL size of the filament carefully and use that to tune your slicer, and then calibrate the extrusion using the calibration cube, in order to get good results. Also, your results will heavily depend on the slicer you use, and your willingness to tune the printer. NO 3d printers available today are plug-and-play, but this printer has the capability to give top-quality prints.

Another Johnny Cash live album? Well, by 1976, Johnnys albums were stalling in the charts and the label was flip-flopping him between new producers with no great results. After a scrapped gospel album, they turned back to the format that rebirthed his career Unfortunately at least for Columbia , Strawberry Cake is no Folsom Prison or San Quentin. For the Cash fan, though, its an enjoyable album, different again from any of his others. Folsom is the wild one, San Quentin an introduction to all new material in the raucous prison environment, Madison Square Gardens the performance of a gentleman, The Johnny Cash Show his gospel message to a TV audience, and Pa Osteraker a stripped down document of his singer-songwriter period, with just the boys backing him. On Strawberry Cake, Johnny is back on the road with the whole family band. Hes still the placid gentleman often heard in the 70s, but he seems to be moving into a greatest hits mode, a shift away from the early 70s Cash who sang political tunes and the narratives of David Allan Coe and Kris Kristofferson. The abundant dialogue on the album, however, shows why Johnny was just as popular as he was, even able to break the British market which is generally resistant to country music. The sound on this album is great, too. Carl Perkins is gone from the band, so his edgier leads are gone, but new guitarist Jerry Hensley is fantastic with Bob Wooten. The two of them weave guitar lines back and forth in a wonderful way, almost like those early Stones albums where you cant tell where Keith Richards ends and Brian Jones begins. So what do we have here? Certainly some classics, all played masterfully. Big River is paced perfectly too often its rushed in its live performance and there is a balance between the minimal lead riff, and some flashier licks during the verses. Doin My Time is a magical rendition, sounding Sun Records-like without any drums, but modern with the dual lead guitars. I Still Miss Someone falters a bit with schmaltzy piano. They recover quickly with an a cappella duet between Johnny and June Carter Cash on Another Man Done Gone interrupted only briefly by some tasteful blues guitar. I Got Stripes is perhaps a bit chipper for a prison song, but still works. And Rock Island Line is played masterfully, even improved by Johnnys joke about how audience member Lonnie Donegan of UK skiffle fame stole the song from him in 1958! There are a couple of old-time numbers. The Carter Family, in an increasingly rare performance for the time, do a great medley of Church in the Wildwood and Lonesome Valley. With only 12-string guitar and Johnny singing bass as backing, its a fantastic performance. Plus it gets interrupted by a bomb scare evacuation! Closing gospel number, The Fourth Man, is a hokier affair, but enjoyable. The three newer songs are all enjoyable but don't cover new ground for Cash. Strawberry Cake is an unreleased waltz documenting the tale of a Californian down on his luck in New York who wanders into the Plaza only to be tempted by the sight of strawberry cake which he steals to amusing results. Fun but forgettable. Navajo is a beautiful tribute to the story of this native tribe, but lacks the punch of his Bitter Tears material. And, introduced as his newest song, is Destination Victoria Station, the somewhat generic train song released on his special album for the Southern US restaurant chain of the same name. The fact that the new songs are all unreleased obscurities only highlights how he was lacking a decent new single to promote. All in all, though, its a great live album showcasing Johnnys laid back mid-seventies style. Tragic if only in how it showcases his ability to draw a live audience while not being able to sell new records. 4 5 Read this and reviews of other Johnny Cash albums, including discussion of rarities and outtakes, on my blog at raisemyglasstothebside at wordpress dot com.

Works fairly well. It will put out the rated voltage and current and is built good enough. It is a little hard to adjust the voltage or current with precision because the knobs are course. I will probably change out the adjustment pots for 10 turn pots. The pots are unmarked, so I will have to take them out and measure them prior to ordering, unless someone knows what resistance they are. It would be nice if the meters had one more digit. If you need to adjust accurately, you need to use an external meter. One benefit of the analog design, is that you can set supply to exactly what you want, while the digital supplies, have digital steps, Turn on off is smooth and controlled with almost zero overshoot, I would have no issues powering on with circuitry connected. Power supply ripple is meets spec and is about 10mV. Power supply seems bigger than it needs to be. There is allot of empty space in the case. There is that crazy china tool smell. If you don't know what I am talking about go to your local Harbor Freight Tool and you will smell the same smell when you walk in the door. I have it in my garage so it is not an issue.

This is a very well-built bag and should have no issues with offering protection for the drums to be stored within. The zippers are pretty heavy gauge, making them hold strong and be less prone to jamming. Also, the shoulder pad strap latches are of solid metal construction. I purchased the 10x12, 11x13, and 12x14 models, and here are a few features that these bags came with that weren't listed in the product description. -A luggage-type identification tag is included that you can attach -In addition to the rubber-padded shoulder strap, there are three carrying handles around the bag, making it easier to find a grip when fumbling around with it. -On the bottom there are four rubber feet to add to the durability of the base and to reduce skidding.

Pros Automatic beatmatching, most everything can be controlled from controller, software is easy to use, and very straightfoward, amazing price for what you get. Cons often times things like the knobs on the crossfader and pitch control can feel like cheap plastic, the advantage of having the ability to use all of your mp3s is nice but I have a non dj sound card in my computer so compared to turntables the sound quality of this thing sucks big time. This is the best pedal tuner I've found so far. I'm selling my old standby of six years, a Boss TU-2, for the Digitech. I'm writing this as a comparison against the Boss, since this is what I'm familiar with, and since the Boss TU-2 is the "standard" floor tuner I've seen people use. Function It grabs on to the note faster than the Boss and it doesn't "hunt", and this is what most of us care about. For this alone, it's an improvement over the TU-2. For my guitar player SG drop tuning light strings constant retuning , he loves it. For my bass, I find hitting a 12th fret harmonic for the low E or low drop-D makes the string more stable and finds the note faster. Occasionally it finds a false harmonic, but mostly it doesn't and it certainly tunes faster than the TU-2. A minor point The DigiTech is true bypass and the Boss isn't, but honestly I can't tell the difference. Features If you flat-tune Eb, etc. and use alternate tunings Drop D, DADGAD, EBEGBE, Open D, etc. , this is the tuner you want. You do not want the Boss TU-2. Here's why. When you set the DigiTech to Eb simple, a few button presses, like the Boss , it recalibrates itself, so your string tuning reads out as DADGAD, not C -G -C -F -G -C . The Boss has a flat tuning option, but it only reads out as 123456 and so it assumes you're tuned EADGBE. So if you're a guitar player tuned to an alternate tuning, you have to put the Boss back into Chromatic mode and try to remember C -G -C -F -G -C . The DigiTech is better, because it will read DADGAD. If you're a bass player in Eb, and you drop-D, the DigiTech will read out DADG. The Boss reads 1234 and assumes you're tuned EADG which you're not , so you have to put it into Chromatic mode and tune to C -G -C -F . Reliability The Boss has been around a long time, everyone uses one, and it has proven reliability. The DigiTech may or may not be, time will tell. Summary The DigiTech tunes faster than the Boss and it's true bypass. The Digitech is much better for flat tunings combined with non-standard tunings. Other than that, they're both about equivalent. Hope that helps.
