There are a few things about this album and I want to get one out of the way first. This is a different side of Hawes' playing. He is more subdued and interactive on this album than he is on others, and if you are deeper into his earlier work you will be taken slightly aback. Give the album a chance because after a few listens it will grow on you. Or, at least it did me and I dislike change. The other thing you need to know that this and a companion album titled nbsp I'm All Smiles nbsp were both recorded for Contemporary at Mitchell's Studio Club in Los Angeles on April 30 and May 1, 1966. Discographies I have cross checked do not show which tracks were recorded on which sets, or even days. I will say that the mood shifts from one album to the other and I am not sure if it is because of selective editing or if they were, indeed, different days or sets . What I do know is there is a telepathic bond among Hawes, bassist Red Mitchell and drummer Donald Bailey. It goes deeper than mere interplay - there are new nuances between and among them that I hear each time I play this and the other album, which lately has been often. Sadly there are no sound samples or track listing on this page at the time of this review . The tracks are 1. The Seance 2. Oleo 3. Easy Street 4. Suddenly I Thought Of You 5. For Heaven's Sake 6. My Romance There are tracks from each album that will surface if you diligently search them out on the web, but there is nothing like listening to both this and the other album back-to- back. I will forewarn you that if you are a Hawes fan who loves his earlier work this is going to take you aback the first time you hear it. If you take the time to listen without judgement and when you can devote your ears and mind to it you may grow to appreciate it as I have. Inside is another view of Hawes' genius.

"Tallest man on Earth?" I think not. Upon seeing the name and cover art of this CD, I was intrigued. A music CD by the tallest man on earth? So the late Robert Pershing Wadlow, Guinness Book's tallest person in recorded history for whom there is irrefutable evidence had, unbeknownst to me, a music CD? And he had played guitar no less? I assumed his gigantism would have made it difficult, if not impossible, to use a musical instrument that requires such precise movements. At 8'11'', surely Robert would have needed a scaled-up guitar to match his gigantic frame, and even then the excessive bone growth in his fingers seems like it would make it impossible to play at a high level. As a long-time Guinness Book of World Records aficionado, I was thrilled to see an amazing physical specimen like Robert perform such feats of talent. What's next, Jyoti Amge, the world's shortest woman, playing basketball? Naturally, I picked up the CD right away. It wasn't until I read the liner notes that I realized how misleading the packaging is. The music was recorded not by the great Robert Pershing Wadlow, but by a Guinness unknown, a British musician by the name of Kristian Matsson. But it gets worse. To add insult to injury, after doing some online research, I found that Kristian stands a mere 5'7''! "Tallest man" indeed. Shame on Krstian for trying to cash in on the name of the great Robert Pershing Wadlow. I hate to see such disgraceful marketing practices being used. However, the music was actually rather good, so I still give it 4 out of 5 stars.

I first heard these guys while searching on Youtube. I was going to see Alter Bridge, and I saw that Emphatic was on the bill as the opening act. I came across the song "Stronger" and immediately liked what my ears were hearing. This brings us to the debut album from Emphatic, entitled "Damage." 1. Do I - I Love this song. To me, as a new up-and-coming band appealing to the masses of rock 'n roll this is the type of song you want to lead with. Pretty heavy, solid vocals and a catchy chorus. I was also pumped when they played his live. For me, this is the strongest song on the album which is both good and bad. 5 5 2. Get Paid - A good follow-up song here as it remains pretty heavy and seemingly just a solid rocker. The one weakness I see which begins here and follows repeatedly throughout the album is the weakness of the lyrics. A good, solid rocking tne overall just not the best lyrically. 4 5 3. Put Down The Drink - A solid outing. This song is caught in the middle somewhere as I wouldn't quite consider it a ballad, but it has elements of such a thing. Again, some aspects of the lyrics as a whole can come across slightly cheesy, but overall, another good rocker here with a slightly slower feel. 4 5 4. Bounce - Let me just say, I like the energy of this track I do. I also understand that this song is probably the one single that the record company made the band write and release in order to get any play and or any extensive support on a tour. Overall, a solid strsight rocker which is very heavy. However, lyrically some of the cheesiest I've heard, and one you can tell is simply there for radio play. I feel that this song is a step down from what I can see this band is capable of doing. 3 5 5. A Place To Fall - Luckily the band seems to no pun intended here bounce back with a very, very solid song. Again, I personally wouldn't consider this song a ballad by the standards, but it is a very good track. Lyrically they are stronger and the words really seem to mean something. Overall the band seems to be very well put together here and shows us a glimpse of what may be coming in the future. 5 5 6. Beg - Another mediocre song follows however. This for me is quite a shame and this is where I begin to see the ups and downs of this record as a whole. Albeit this song isn't quite as bad lyrically as "Bounce" but it seems to me the band is trying to hard to come across as Motley-Crue type rockers who just on't care about the law, etc. Maybe it's just the record company again but I feel they can do better then this. 3.5 5 7. Tonight - This is the ballad of the album. Where the other two were riding the border, here is where the band brought out their softer side. For me though, something comes up short. It's not a bad song per se, it just comes across as slightly a generic sounding ballad. One that again, every band NEEDS on their album before the record company will back it 100 . Ballads bring in the most well-rounded radio play and that is what record companies want. Overall, good but very average. 3 5 8. Pride - I want to like this song I really do. There are elements to the song that I do like. It seems to be a very positive and inspiring type song, but again the lyrics are the downfall. With a very solid musical arraingment particularly good drum playing the lyrics just don't make this song believable. Overall, a good attempt but falls short. 3.5 5 9. Original Sin - I like this song. This is a song where again, the band shows signs of what they can do. Lyrically even this song isn't tat strong, but it is a very straight foward rocker and gets you hooked. A song that will get you pumped. Good track by the guys here. 4.5 5 10. Don't Forget About Me - The album closes here with another ballad. This song however has some good lyrics and one you can tell comes from prsonal experience by one or all of the bandmates. Good guitar playing here and the vocals just seem to gel, harmonizing with everything. The album ends on a very high note here. 4.5 5 Overall, you get a debut album by Emphatic which I consider slightly above average. I would give this album a total of 3.5 5 stars. To me they need to work on a couple things. One, the lyrics. Cheesy on some okay most of the album. Two, being who they are. Don't let the record company scare you. I urge anyone to give these guys a chance. They put on a very good live show and have massive potential. One final thing, I would have replaced one song probably Beg or Pride with "Stronger." That's a killer track that needs to be released on an album in the future.

-Great value for the price, this controller's got pads that are less sensitive than they should be, but a simple modification involving tape can fix this. -The touch pad is a lot of fun for effect sliders, also pressing on it in the first place generates a mappable CC note so you can activate and slide an effect around with one finger. The touch scale is easy to play. -Didn't have any trouble installing it and mapping to VirtualDJ. -The build quality is plastic. Plastic everywhere. This thing is made out of two things plastic and rubber, and you notice it. Thing is, PCB's don't break that easy, and this plastic seems to be less brittle but still more rigid than others. Plastic ain't a bad thing. -This controller could more or less likely be dropped repeatedly and it might only crack the case. No knobs or anything sticking off it, which means there really isn't any danger in just throwing it in a backpack, it'll be fine if you're not too rough. -I'd worry about is the midi connection breaking inside the controller from misplaced weight on the midi connection on it -Touchpad hardware seems to be somewhat delicate, surface is matte and will wear down to a gloss over time with serious use.

Fans of more thoughtful electronica rejoice, your album of the summer, if not year, is available for your listening pleasure. For most people the anticipated electronic release of the year was the Daft Punk album. An artist like Henry Saiz doesn't get the same radio play and that is a shame since his music can elevate you to such magical places. Most people will agree a good Daft Punk track can get your head nodding and feet thumping. A good Henry Saiz track can get your heart thumping and send your mind soaring to new heights. And luckily for purchasers of this album, there are a ton. Stand out tracks for me are Moonlight Wolf, Sleepwalk and Afraid. But more than just the standout tracks, the tracks have different tempos, create different feelings, not letting the listener predict the vibe of the next track. Saiz creates some haunting melodies that are eerily beautiful. A really beautiful album that is not only a refreshing change of pace from the louder blaring commercial electronica, but relishes the melodies created with the tiniest of sounds.

I am perr-itty dissapointed in Decca for this nice little boxed set. Why? Well, firstly the Symphonies are broken up. For example 5 is with 2 and 7 with 1. Then on one of the discs you get Leonore Overture 3, Symphony 4 and then the first movement of the 9th!...then you have put on the next disc for the rest of the 9th! Oh Man! Secondly, the sound quality is not the demonstration quality one expects from Decca during those amazing analogue years. The sound sounds muddied up. The volume levels are dim, and the clarity and brilliance don't shine through on the recordings. Thirdly, Solti doesn't deliver as you know he should. They are standard readings with a bit of extra insight, but nothing of that typical "Solti" drive. It seems that Beethoven isn't Solti's composer par excellence! He doesn't understand him as say, Klemperer or Karajan do. Solti and the CSO didn't really know each other so well back in 1972 74, and you can tell. I'd imagine that their later digital cycle would be more authoratative than this first set. I still like my Klemperer and my Karajan any sets!

I purchased this, with an optional probe, to control an electric smoker. I assumed my mistake that the unit could operate up to the temperature of the optional probe, which is 249 degrees. That assumption was incorrect, the unit is only capable of operating up to 210 degrees. As for controlling the heating element in the smoker, the unit is useless, the temperature in short order rises to well over 210, tripping an alarm which cannot be silenced without unplugging the unit. Not sure why so many complaints about the wiring diagram on top of the box. Pretty clear, I thought. UPDATE Having used the unit for an overnight cook, I have to update my comments. In it's current form, it is less than ideal for a smoker, due to the temperature limitation. That said, this little device is fantastic. Works perfectly and should have all sorts of applications. If Inkbird comes out with a unit that functions up to the 275 degree range, I will be the first in line to buy it.

D'Addario nickel wound light gauge electric guitar strings come in a anti-corrosion package. I can attest to the effectiveness of this packaging as I have had these strings in their original unopened package for months. There is no corrosion at all. The strings hold up well and will hold up even better if you clean your strings of sweat after playing . Yes, the sound is bright. D'Addario tells you this ON THE PACKAGE. Some people down-rated the strings for this! If you don't like bright sounding guitar strings, the D'Addario website can direct you to their more mellow sounding strings such as the XL Chromes Flat Wound . There's also a chart on the back of the package that shows you where these strings are on a 'brightness' spectrum. The 3rd string is unwound-typical of electric guitar strings. Ends of strings are color coded so you know what string your putting on your guitar 1st string, 2nd string, etc . Highly recommended.

This axe is killer. I can't believe you can get a guitar of this quality- for this price. Obviously, it's not going to give you what a Made in the USA Custom Tele will give you. But his is a really fun guitar to play, and I think it sounds fantastic. I love the sound of the bridge Pup, and I would almost call it a "warm growl," if such a thing exists. This thing will dig and claw- and just give you some real nice growl when you run it through your amp- with some overdrive or distortion. It will go everywhere I want it to go, anyway. Maybe not for the Metal enthusiast. But everyone else should be able to crank it to nice places. The neck Pup will give you nice clean tones, and polishes up nicely. The neck feels nice, and the guitar is beautiful. I have the White Blonde version. I have 1 major beef with this axe. The ash-tray bridge has the 3 brass barrel configuration. And for the life of me, I can't get the thing to intonate properly. I'm close. But no matter what I do- no cigar. It chords nicely, but depending on how you choose to go with those barrels- certain chords are just a little tiny bit off. But it's enough to throw off the AWESOME sound you'd otherwise be getting. I really want to throw another ash-tray on there- but I need to find a string-thru- with 6 separate saddles. That way, I can fine-tune, and pin-point my exact intonation preferences. Other than that issue- I really LOVE this guitar, and I would highly recommend this axe. I have about 15 or so guitars, and I'd say this in my top 3 or 4 faves- at the moment. Had it for about 6 months now. And been playing for 12 years I guess. I'm no pro at all- but I love this guitar. Impressed with what Squier has been doing of late. Go check it out!!

This is the best compilation of music that I have ever encountered. Many of the songs are below the radar of Top 40 in the 60's -- or at least from my perspective as a child during that period. However, I am still in the dark about what exactly made the Mods versus Rockers, but that is because of my own shortcomings. To me, the music presented creates a real blur as to the difference in music choices between the opposing and sometimes clashing groups. I do realize that the groups had different styles i.e. fashions , hangouts and dance moves, but the music choices of the groups is not so clear. Most important is that this music compilation is stellar, regardless of which group preferred what. I am confused and disappointed that the present review is the first 04 12 15 . What's going on? The CD is a huge bargain I am tempted to buy several several to spread the word, but sometimes it seems best to hold our favorites close to the vest and keep them as our very own. In short, this collection is essential.

Jessie Jones debut album, after years fronting Feeding People and after a sojourning period, is a broad mix of genres. If the categorical hashtag is psych-pop, Im more aligned with the latter than the former, but labels are usually simplistic things with little meaning, and this is a cd thats best defined individually. The cd kicks off with Sugar Coated, a song that belongs on heavy rotation, summertime pop radio right now and its the type of track that can take an artist to the next level. Its definitely the hook to get you to explore the rest of this diverse cd. Sugar Coated contains catchy refrains, empowering lyrics, and just enough experimentation to raise it above generic pop. A second single Lady La De Da starts with a jazzy lament reminiscent of late 80s Siouxsie Sioux then pauses momentarily as if to take a collective deep breath before kicking into a surf-guitar finale with Ms. Jones repeatedly wailing the chorus. These are two quite different songs, linked only by Jones voice, and therein lay the appeal of this record. Some favorites are Nightingale and Twelve Hour Man. The former is a lovely song, floating above strings with Jones plaintive, aching voice reminding me of a cross between Maria McKee and Billie Holiday. The latter is a jangly pop beauty with horns and psychedelic organ flavors creating another very radio-friendly song. Prisoners Cinema is a bit too self-indulgent and trippy for me, and the closer Mental Illness has the feel of a demo, an understated acoustic guitar providing the only accompaniment before fading out in a brief ninety seconds. These two are the only missteps on this cd, and that yields a pretty solid batting average. Jessie Jones writes thoughtful, introspective songs with a heady combination of strength, vulnerability, joy, and spirituality. The songs are a buffet of variety and, like every buffet, there are some treats you're likely to go to for seconds and others you may not find to your liking. On the whole, however, this is a banquet I truly savored.

Perhaps the most surprising thing about Apocalyptica's Shadowmaker is that it made me nostalgic. It brought me back to my pre-hiphop youth when I listened to bands like Whitesnake. The problem here is that the days of my pre-hiphop youth are long gone, and if you asked yourself, "Who's Whitesnake?" all I can say is, "Exactly." This is a band I love and am committed to, and I worry that adding a full-time vocalist may not be the thing they needed. There is no Ruska, or Cortege, or even Beautiful, on this album, and I worry that the direction they are going in now is taking them away from their strengths. Still, I'll give more time and more listenings. Apocalyptica albums have to grow on me, and perhaps in a month or so I'll write another review completely refuting this one.

I've been a fan of Osamu's ever since I bought a vinyl copy of "Benzaiten" well over two decades ago. Unlike Kitaro, who though often lovely concentrates mostly on electronic instrumentation, and who tends to repeat himself too often, Osamu mixes in many traditional Japanese acoustic intruments and tries to give each of his projects a different quality and mood from the previous ones. Though his style is instantly recognizable, this is his most heavily electronic, bass-laden, beat-driven CD, and I love it. The female Japanese vocals give it extra interest, and there are so many layers of sound that I hear new things each time I listen to this CD. The only reason I don't give it five stars is that Osamu has still not topped his wonderful 1987 album PASSAGES, which has been, unfortunately, long out-of-print. I keep hoping CBS will re-release it someday. Until that unlikely event, I must content myself with the few Osamu CD's currently available, and this one is the best of the lot. If you like this music, search out "California Roll," also out-of-print I have seen a copy or two in cut-out bins, but not recently . This guy deserves to be much better known than he is. Update the out-of-print CD of PASSAGES is available from time to time I finally got one from a third party Amazon seller. It lived up to my memories. I highly recommend PASSAGES as being worth keeping an eye out for! In the meantime, BEYOND THE CIRCLE is an excellent addition to this artist's outstanding catalog.

I record in my home studio so my installation is stable and fixed. I was intrigued by the design of this product. Two screens separated by about a half inch of airspace. I was impressed by its construction. The screens are securely mounted in a molded plastic assembly that looks and feels robust. The gooseneck is smaller in diameter than others I've seen in this price class. The larger size being 3 8ths inch vs. 5 16th inch for this one. The smaller diameter gooseneck isn't as stiff as its larger cousins so installation may be slightly more tricky and the filter may float about annoyingly when the mike or its mount are moved. If this filter is destined for a spring-loaded mike boom, its weight may be a problem. The double screen assembly is noticeably heavier than a single screen type. The gooseneck is welded to the clamp assembly so the clamp cannot be removed. It costs more to weld the gooseneck to the clamp but the increase in quality actually reduces versatility. If you use a spring-loaded full articulation mike boom, this filter may be too heavy depending on the type of mike used. I use such a mike boom with a relatively heavy mike and the weight of the total package was a problem for me. I removed the double screen assembly from its gooseneck it unscrews easily . Since standard mike stand threads are used, it was easy to attach the double screen assembly to another gooseneck I had from which the clamp assembly had been easily unscrewed. I used cable ties to secure this double pop filter to the shock mount for the mike. It worked perfectly and the filter stays in front of the mike in all positions. The double screen pop filter is highly effective. If you like to "close talk" the mike, this filter makes it possible without all the potent plosives. While its weight may pose some installation challenges, this double screen pop filter is highly effective and does its job better than any single screen pop filter.

After the daring but critically unsuccessful 'Kilroy was here' came six years of separation for the Styx boys, releasing various solo albums in that time, only to reconvene in 1990 without Tommy Shaw. The product is 'Edge of the Century', which, instead, featured the credible Glen Burtnik. 'Edge of the Century' is a very careful album, no daring concepts or experimental songs. Instead, it is a slab of predictable rockers and ballads, with very little variety. 'Edge' has a flavour of hair-band music, which was all the rage at the time. So this should be a weak Styx album, and, admittedly, it is disappointing upon the first listen. However, while 'Edge of the century' is generic and artistically dull, it succeeds with quality songwriting. DeYoung's ballads soar with uplifting melodies and passionate vocal harmonies, while Burtnik's rockers are fun and catchy. The only deviation from predictability, 'All in a day's work', is a real gem. It mixes acoustic rootsy musings from Glen Burtnik with a melodious, classically-tinged chorus. So, a fairly uneventful album, but 'Edge of the Century' has enough genuine substance to become a keeper in a discerning music lovers collections, rating 1 2

I cannot believe the difference these stabilizers make. I chose the BAL arms over their very similar competition because of the way they lock. Instead of a T-handle or other hand activated lock they use a bolt head the same size as the jack screws. This way you can use the extension arm for the jacks and limit how much you have to get on the ground to lock and unlock them. I purchased 2 sets, but have only installed one due to interference from the generator exhaust and propane lines. I still need to resolve that, but it's not BAL's fault. The ones I did install make a night and day difference for side to side motion when moving around in the RV. That used to be very noticeable. Now you can hardly feel side to side when someone moved in the camper. The problem is you can feel the front to back movement now. I suspect that nearly all movement and sway will be eliminated once I install the other set. I also tempted to get a set for the rear supports too now. They are very easy to install once you locate good places on the frame to mount them. I didn't use the included bolt for the bottom of the legs, it was too long. I purchased one that fit better from a local hardware store for less than a dollar and it looks much cleaner that way.

The device has a design flaw which results in the leads on the relays burning off in time. If you are buying a replacement, this is probably why. The connections are left far too long from the factory and can flash over to other parts of the board, and a few of the traces are light on solder. Pull back the four metal tabs holding the IC board in, lift the board out. Dab a soldering iron on any funky looking joints and trim back terminals to the board with a pair of end cutters. If you have a hot glue gun, slather the whole back of the Ic board with about 1 8" of glue. With any luck it will be the last replacement you ever have to buy.

I always keep a bottle of this in the fridge. It's very convenient and makes a great marinade. I find that it is best tasting when it is fairly fresh, so I skip the huge bottles and buy the 8 ounce bottles. It makes an amazing frozen cherry limeade in the blender. I've also used this juice to can the cherry limeade concentrate when fresh cherries were in stock-that recipe is on several canning sites. It's not just like a fresh lime, but in my area the fresh limes aren't always that great themselves and they are often about 1 each. The secret to this product, to me, is using it when it's freshly opened and not letting it sit unused too long. Then it is FINE. I use it in salsa, pico de gallo- just SEVERAL Mexican recipes, and various marinades for all sorts of things, as well as to just squirt on food as it cooks sometimes. This is a great price- less than my local stores as of the time I am writing this - and it's nice it's been in stock here- I've recently had trouble finding Real Lemon on store shelves in my area.

Hello Echo is an indie rock band that i fit somewhere between modest mouse and Elliot smith or radical face. They sound like themselves, which in turn sounds really good. I thoroughly enjoyed this band. In a sea of small label groups that reflect each other, i found this album refreshing. My daughter was gifted a fun colored recorder from the 1 section of a store thanks grandma prior to officially learning how to play. That thing was pure torture to listen to. The notes that come from the Kingso Soprano Recorder actually were real notes that changed as you covered up the holes in different patters. And, you could recognize a song after a little bit of practice. I was hesitant to purchase a teal colored recorder because I have only seen the standard issued black recorders sound okay if user has skill and colored ones were more like toys. TJ Casey has a lovely homespun wisdom that he imparts with his wholesome and entertaining songs. "Higher" is philosophical and beautiful piece of writing....with very very nice vocals - I loved Casey's vocals in "Its In My Blood".....this song is soulful and melodious. "I'm Just A Poor Cowboy" is a delightful,classic road song...brought a smile to my face - "My Heart And Soul" has attractive guitar and vocals....this is a very hummable tune. "He's Broken Every Promise One by One" is a moving song of heart-break...lovely! "Without You" is a folksy and charming, romantic song with a simple and moving melody. "Freedom Is A horse" is a song made for the love of nature....being one with nature....great spoken words and a haunting flute in the background...absolutely loved it! - The titular track is a jolly and sarcastic song...very entertaining lyrics in this one! One thing for sure, all the 14 songs in this album took me far away from the hustle bustle of big city life -

First, disregard the boilerplate description saying it's 2 inches wide which'd properly apply to a guitar strap maybe this one is 3 4" wide, except for the wider part where the headstock cord attaches. My tenor ukulele's amp cord jack does double-duty as a strap button, but it's larger than this strap's multiple, precut slot hole connectors. So I sized up which slot hole to use for proper playing position and then cut that slot hole a bit larger, using an X-Acto knife kit's circle hole cutter tool. Any razor-sharp craft knife, even a box-cutter, would work, if you're very careful not to make the button hole too big. When I got the right fit for my strap button yours might be the right size fit without enlargement , I then cut off the excess strap that would have dangled below the button rounding the cut precisely, for looks . I then folded the shoelace-like headstock cord in half, held the two ends together, and tied a single knot with both ends. Slide the other, unknotted end of that loop under the strings on your headstock, just above the nut, then loop it around and cinch up the knotted end through the other-end loop. Then push the knot through the large slot at the end of the strap after untwisting, so the strap is flat from button end to headstock end . Trial and error in this step should get you there eventually. Oh, when sizing up which of the strap's button holes to modify if necessary , be sure to consider the cord as part of the ultimate overall length. Great strap for a tenor uke, if it has a strap button. All you'd need, I think. This kind of strap is preferable, IMO, to the hook kind that wraps under the uke body and attaches to the bottom of the soundhole, because those kind of no-button-needed wrap straps deaden the instrument's resonance somewhat.

I saw this guitar on Musicians Friends website and immediately fell in love with it. However, due to it's shoddy construction it was a love that was not meant to be! It all began with a cherry finish serial 12121500317. I loved how the USA Mini humbuckers made the tone of this guitar really sing and the playability, action and setup was fine. Finish-wise, I noticed the areas around the lower sound hole that were roughly cut during the manufacturing process and could have been sanded smooth. This also caused the paint to not adhere properly causing dots of the wood to show through. I was a bit miffed by this yet considered it to be a minor issue. The strings left a greenish-black residue on my fingers which was a rather odd. The strings also felt rather scratchy against the frets. Looking at it through a magnifying glass I noticed that the tops of the frets were somewhat mysteriously abraded! Again, this was strange because every new guitar I have ever played the frets were shiny and smooth. Here we have a brand new guitar and it's already telling on Epiphone's quality department! Although these craftsmanship issues were a disappointment what I noticed a week later was the icing on the cake! A week later I just happened to be playing this guitar next to my living room window and just happen to notice the second issue that was reflected in the sunlight located on the fret board under the high E string running between the second and seventh frets. There is a decorative binding stripe under this string and the low E string that runs the length of the fret board. Between the second and seventh frets mentioned this stripe looks as though it was embossed into the wood with a screwdriver creating a noticeable deep groove. Everywhere else it was nice and flush. What puzzled me was why was it that all the block inlays were beautifully installed flush on the fretboard whereas the decorative stripe wasn't given the same care!?? This was the last straw!! My interest in this guitar was extinguished! I just figured I had received a poorly made copy and returned it to Musicians Friend for and exchange. They sent me serial 12121500618, also a cherry finish. This copy had all the same defects!!! This time the paint around the sound holes was even worse then before and the fretboard binding stripe issue was not only under the high E string but under the low E string as well...almost in the same location as the first copy they sent me!!!!! What's going on here Epiphone??? Some might scoff at these issues and accept them but I flat out refuse to! Why? Because this guitar was Chinese made and I currently own 3 Chinese made guitars one is also an Epiphone and all of them were beautifully made! The quality of these guitars was fantastic. Oh well, consider this a lost sale because today I returned it for a full refund! Epiphone's motto is "Passion is our performance" but I'd like to ask them just where the hell does their current quality values stand! Yet apart from this dilemma I'd like to thank the customer service department at Musicians Friend for their help in returning these shoddy made guitars. After dealing with this dilemma times 2 my interest in any future purchase of another Epiphone guitar has pretty much been squashed! One more thing...I took pictures to back this up. Message me if you'd like to see them for yourself.

The first time I listened to it, I said "what the hell is this?" I listened to it again and said "there's something about this album." The next morning, I woke up and couldn't wait to get in the car for the drive to work. I felt like I needed to listen to it. Then I started to discover specific songs that were just awesome. Then I started to feel a connection with most of the songs. Now, I love this album. I haven't really listened to anything else since St. Patty's Day. It just works. It connects in a deep way. I love We Were Dead Before the Ship Even sank. I still listen to it a lot 8 years later. This album has some stuff that seems to elaborate on the themes of that album. Some songs here still have that salty wet sound. But Strangers also explores a bunch of other "genres." It's a musical adventure. It's a familiar voice, a familiar style, a familiar texture. But somehow it feels strange. In a good way. Strangers, The Ground Walks, Sugar Boats, and Be Brave were the first songs to pull me in. It may be different for you. Definitely check out "We Were Dead" if you haven't. It's the bridge between "Good News" and "Strangers." Great album.

Good music for dance practice or for teaching dance. The songs, though plain, have a distinct beat that any beginner could follow. Too bad most mp3 download services do not include the CD cover text or CD liner notes. For specialty CD's such as this one, such notes would be helpful. So, I'll write the CD liner text here This album contains eight basic merengue rhythm tracks at various slower tempos designed for dance practice. These are not typical Top 40 songs. Instead, they are instrumental tracks of the basic rhythm style of merengue. The last three tracks contain a voiceover counting the basic merengue footwork timing. This CD is designed for dance practice and dance teaching. Typical nightclub merengue music has a tempo of 120 to 150 beats per minute. These tracks range from 90 to 120 beats per minute.

I was so excited about this pedal. I watched tons of youtube videos, read reviews, and generally anticipated its arrival. It gives the amp a voxy sound except that when you try to use crunch past 12 oclock on the sound knob, the pedal creates a terrible buzz that makes your guitar sound like it has the cheapest single coil pickups except that no repositioning could get rid of it. Granted, the tones it did produce were quite nice, warm, singy, and fat. I thought that the noise was from the guitar or amp. I tried a different Boss dist pedal and it was fine. The pedal was the problem. Maybe the noise is meant to simulate the amp being turned up really loud. I don't know. I wouldn't buy this with the hopes of it being your new drive pedal. I know in live situations pedal noise is tolerable, but this noise would make it hard to have the pedal on without playing with the additional noise. It probably works best as a clean boost or low drive pedal. I got the boss Dyna Drive used for 30 dollars, and it has made me pretty happy. This pedal I fear will be relegated to the drawer or sold off when the time comes. So sad.

Ry Cooder's last record, "Pull Up Some Dust..." showed how politics had energized this brilliant, but meandering musician to produce some of his best work. Election Special continues this trend, in a more concise, and even more intense way. Ry is lathered up over the insanity of U.S. politics, policy, and economics, and it is driving him into a wonderful phase of manic creativity. Almost like Picasso's blue period. But on steroids. Demonic guitar work, sparkling mandolin, spot-on vocals, and harmonies, with son, Joachim, as his sole supporter on drums. Lyrics stinging with venom and truth and despair. Poor Ry must wonder if anyone is listening, and does anyone believe or care anymore? Is it just a "kiss away"? Some of this sounds like the Stones, with Ry in the studio, circa 1970s!!!

--EDIT-- I had previously given this item 2 stars, but I'm going to bump it to 4. VicTsing saw my previous review and sent me a second interface. It's a drag that I had to get a second one, but it shows initiative on their part, so they get a bonus point for that. This unit does not exhibit the "note-off" behavior that the first one did making the device nearly unusable . It does work for actually playing the keyboard. However, it does exhibit some strange behavior when sending CC messages, which I've seen other reviewers describe as well. When using a CC knob on my controller, the interface will send rapid-fire note data to the program, sounding a bit like an old-time rotary doorbell. It's also a bit dodgy how much of the CC information is making it through. Trying to use a CC knob to control volume or panning is unreliable. THAT SAID, I'm going to give this 4 stars because for the insanely low price, it does work well for connecting a keyboard and playing it. And if you happen to get a bum unit, VicTsing is very easygoing about sending a replacement. I can't give it 5 stars because of the CC issue, and because I -did- have to get a second one sent. If you just need to connect a non-USB MIDI keyboard to a computer, this will work. I wouldn't recommend it for anything mission-critical, or to connect something to send loads of CC data. But for the price, I'd say that's a fair deal. --ORIGINAL REVIEW-- It does connect a MIDI device via USB, but the unit I got drops data. As long as you play one note at a time, all is well, but when playing chords, it only passes a "note off" message for one of the notes, leaving the others playing until you retrigger and release each note individually. I tested the same keyboard computer setup using a different MIDI-to-USB device and experienced no such problems, so the issue is definitely with the MIDI-to-USB cable, not with the devices on either end of it. I'm giving two stars because it DOES allow a MIDI device to talk to a PC via USB. So you could potentially use it to test whether a device is sending receiving MIDI. It would also work fine if you only ever play one note at a time. It's not really usable for performance or data entry beyond that, though.

Don't bother wasting your money on this product unless of course you're okay with throwing half of it away. I purchased one of these about a year ago and had problems with it back then, but totally forgot about it until I purchased another one and received it today. I realized why I don't use this product very often. As soon as you take the plastic off the whole roll poofs up out of control and is a huge pain in the ass to get it back on the spool without it breaking or tangling up! Yeah, and you have to have tension on the spool the whole time or it poofs up out of control and is impossible to feed it into the machine. It really should be on an 11 inch spool because that is where it naturally wants to coil too. If it gets tangled up I assure you it will break and you will scream and want to pull your hair out! It is already extremely expensive and you get half of what you would normally get on a regular ABS roll. If you can get past all of that and get everything working good. The parts turned out rather well and for the most part look really good, but there is a lot of screwing around, swearing and praying that it doesn't break as the machine pulls in the filament. If you have a part that takes more than 10 minutes you might as well stand there and babysit it the entire time! If it should happen to break or jump the spool, well you have to start all over again and wastes yet more time and material. Yeah, and you're back to swearing and screaming.

I recently saw "Our Kind of Traitor" in the theater more on that later . The movie is supported by a beautiful orchestral score, courtesy of veteran movie composer Marcelo Zarvos "Rock the Casbah", "The Choice", "The Words" . I couldn't wait to check it out, and my search led me to this. "Our Kind of Traitor" 20 tracks 62 min. opens with the solemn "The Ballet", which opens the movie as we watch a Russian ballet performance with the Russian mob doing their business in the background while their wives watch the ballet. At 6 min., it is one of the longest pieces on here, and also one of the best. "Journey to Bern" is a lot more 'nervous'. Our two main characters by now have befriended, and play their "First Tennis Match". "Dima's Children" is, as one might expect, light and touching, with beautiful flutes accompanied by the orchestra. I wish it lasted longer than just 1'17", tough. "Family Escape, Pt. 1" and Family Escape, Pt. 2" together form a 7 min. mini-suite that works quite well, with the orchestra reflecting the tension on the big screen. I also love "The Prince", another long 6 min. piece in which the orchestra goes from soft to harder to softer again, and reflecting the complex nature of the character at issue. Some of the very short 1 to 2 min. pieces are also worthwhile, among the best for me being "Paris Detour". The soundtrack closes with the title track, which plays over the movie's end titles, and which nicely reprises several music themes developed in earlier pieces. Bottom line if you like classical music, scored for a film in particular, you cannot go wrong with this. As to the movie itself, I am very happy to report that, after the disastrous 2011 big screen adaptation of "Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy", the big screen adaptation of John LeCarre's "Our Kind of Traitor" is a delightful spy thriller, in the best possible European way, with a great and compelling story line, and one that, unlike Tinker Tailor's screen adaptation, is actually understandable. The lead performances from Ewan McGregor, Naomi Harris and Stellan Skarsgrd only add to the enjoyment of the film. "Our Kind of Traitor", both the soundtrack and the movie, is HIGHLY RECOMMENDED!

I have used this reamer to ream 4 holes on my 1994 Wrangler 2 for the tie-rod ends and 2 for the pitman arm had to do it twice . I made sure I used plenty of oil and the reamer could probably still ream another 4 holes, so plenty for the 1-time use I needed. To give you an idea, I probably spent close to 20 hours hand-reaming overall so the reamer has held up pretty well in my opinion. Reaming became less efficient as the reamer was contacting more and more of the metal. When the reamer had full contact with the hole, I still needed to get going but the reamer was not cutting anymore too much contact area for the force I could exert on the reamer . I thought the blades had gotten dull but when I started the next hole, it would start cutting very well again. To solve that issue, whenever I came to the point where the reamer would stop cutting, I would remote it and roughen the wall of the hole with sandpaper and then the reamer would start cutting again. I did that until I reached the proper depth and it helped a lot. Be careful though, don't go too crazy with the sand paper cartridge and make sure the reamer is going straight the whole time. It's not that easy to tell and I had to get a new pitman arm and start the process all over again. If you can afford it, get a drill bit that is close to the final size you need. It may be more expensive but it will save you hours of reaming. You might want to consider having a machine shop do it for you. It shouldn't be that much more expensive if they have the right tools and it will save you a lot of hand-reaming time.

But I do feel the need to let people know that if you're NOT a professional and for some reason get it into your head that just because you've installed locking tuners on your guitar and watched a bunch of youtube videos you're now thinking to yourself "Yeah I can replace the three worn out frets on this thing," that I hope you're doing it on a guitar you don't mind wrecking. I got it done, and from a distance you can't even tell the difference, but it is NOT an easy or simple tasks. If you don't have a fret press SPECIFICALLY MADE to get frets into the fingerboard, getting them in is NOT going to be easy I improvised one out of a vice-grip, some rubber strips and a bunch of foam . If you're not a VERY handy person who has done this kind of thing before, and you care about your guitar, have a professional do this.

If Fruupp is not familiar to you start here. This UK prog band recorded for Dawn records - releasing four LPs between 1973 1975 and a couple of singles before calling it a day in 1976 - final gig at London's Roundhouse. This sampler has studio tracks from all four LPs plus a previously unreleased single, On A Clear Day. In addition to fine individual LP reissues on Esoteric look for a two CD compilation which digs deeper than this overview. Like Cressida, Gracious, Spring and so many more obscure prog bands, Fruupp was accomplished and highly listenable.

From what I gather about Jeanne Robertson, she doesn't consider herself a "stand-up comic" so much as "public speaker," but make no mistake about it - she is as funny as anyone in the business. She comes from a little corner of North Carolina, and if Mayberry actually existed, you wouldn't have difficulty imagining that her home must be just up the road a spell from Andy and Barney. I recently had my first exposure to the Sirius radio network, and tracks from this "Flat-Out Funny" recording were prominently rotated between Bill Cosby and Jeff Foxworthy. It is no faint praise to say that Jeanne Robertson is as funny as any of them. Her stories are full of wry and hilarious insights into the human condition, and it appears that any obstacle in her life simply becomes "new material". This also seems to mean that anyone in her life is "fair game" for inclusion in her routines. We hear many details about her husband, Jerry "Leftbrain", including his hesitation to accompany her on a near-disastrous eight-day rafting trip through the grand canyon, which included recreational opportunities such as hanging precariously from the side of a cliff, using AARP-vintage hands that at home would have difficulty opening a jar in the kitchen. This recording is an absolute obvious choice for that person or family or group who wants to hear "flat-out funny" comedy that is suitable for all ears, but the truth is that she is also as funny as the comedians who use the full spectrum of language. Flat-Out recommended.

I have the unfortunate experience of dealing with bedbugs in my apartment...the whole building is infected. The landlord is really great at treating the apartments when tenants tell him, but these creatures run the moment heat starts. Where do they go? To the surrounding apartments...next door, upstairs, downstairs...and back again over and over. So I am trying desperately to help myself by doing steam cleanings on my ceilings, walls, floors, and everything in between. The first steamer I bought was a much smaller, hand-held type that broke after a couple of uses and caused severe pain in my hands from holding the trigger. I wasn't able to do the structure but was only doing my bedding and linens and clothing...it was a start. After sending it back, I decided to get the Steamfast SF-275 Canister Steam Cleaner and fell in love with it from the beginning! To give you a better perspective, I will list the pro's and con's Pro's- The trigger has a lock mechanism Water heats up super fast to save you time. Ready-to-use indicator light is located in an easy to see place Long extension hose Multiple attachments...enough for ANY job and then some Replacement brushes included some types Two 2 mop pads, not just one Canister rolls very easily Carry handle also serves as holder Lightweight even when full to the maximum capacity Steam last a very, very long time even when using the jet stream Con's Hose has a tendency to curl if you move too much so keep an eye on it. Hose also can get under your feet if you're not careful...trust me, you don't want to step on it it's a bit fragile. Boiler cap can get extremely hot, but if you leave the compartment open it helps ventilate it a little. Still, be careful! No place to store all the attachments when you're done using them. In conclusion, I find that not only do the pro's outnumber the con's, but the later are not things that one can't work around. Be aware of how you move and where the hose is and don't touch the boiler cap with your bare hands. Attachments can be stored anywhere really - an old shoe box, for example. This machine is fast, lasts, and keeps on moving. I've been using it now for a couple of weeks on a daily basis. If I'm not using, my brother is...it's almost around the clock use because it does such a good job on everything. My home has never been as clean and we are both obsessive about cleanliness. My only regret is not having purchased sooner or first.

First off, not just a great but a fantastic album even if you can hear the production . This brooding, morose but carefully crafted musical experience from a bunch of unknowns who never created a follow-up but re-released a two disc of this album about 20 years later--it's on my to purchase list plumbed the depths of "emo" before I think there even was "emo" music. They took their time and produced songs that would have made The Cure proud. Slow, sometimes tortured moments with deep resonant guitars and implacable synthesizers used to great melancholic effect sustain haunted and pensive and desirous moods. Every song some are just pieces of music carried to forward the emotionally wrought plot of the album here is good but some are stellar. I've never tired of this album and often return to it and two of the songs that I listen to repeatedly are "Drug" and "Wait". "Drug" is a guitar drenched rocker that rises and falls with an impassioned chorus "Love! Love is the drug!" that is stretched out into both heart-wrenching and heart-lifting pangs. It's filled with longing, and regret, and it hits hard. While you could easily hear the sentiments on "Drug" in a variety of other songs and through a variety of other bands I think you'll hardly ever hear a song like "Wait", not then and especially not today, in any mainstream market. "Wait" is a heavy and knowing song, full of tension and a little torture, about keeping oneself resolutely celibate amidst the engulfing flames of sexual desire and encourages the ardent other to do the same. This song floors me and was an important song in my teenage years not just for its subject but for those guitars...! Filled with reverb and distortion this song could make the top 10 list of my all-time favorite songs. Those drenched deep guitars are delicious! There are other songs that are simply great on this release. "Bare My Soul", "Shame Last Sunday", "Those That Kill", and especially "Grieve" are all awesome in their own respects. Special mention goes to how Sincerely Paul closes out this release with the last three songs the short instrumentals "Helpless" and "Close" with the long "Turnaway" being sandwiched inbetween . This trifecta closes the album out in an unhurried and contemplative pace, giving the listener room to breathe and digest the experience they've been through. Smart and good stuff. But with such a great album why haven't you heard it? And why would I tag the title "destined to fail"? Well, first point this was put out by a small independent label, Blonde Vinyl, pseudo-run by the infamous Michael Knott. So a few people very few did hear of it but they were the very small minority who were fans of very small bands within a very small niche within the alternative Christian music scene. These guys didn't have money to mass market and they weren't business people first and foremost anyway. So there's that. And then there's the band itself Sincerely Paul. Who were these guys? No one knew. And their music was not happy, peppy pop with a rock flavor. It was slow serious, brooding, elegiac. The music itself was already niche, playing to fans of what would become goth and darkwave. It had an underground appeal, but how many Christian boys and girls were gravitating toward this type of sound? 100? 200? Not many. You get the picture. So this band, as great as this release was, was simply destined to fall by the wayside, hardly a blip on the overall music scene. And it's a shame because this is a great album, a great release. These guys should have been famous. For fans of The Cure Cush circa "The New Sound" Bauhaus Depeche Mode early Human Drama Jane's Addiction.

It took me a while to make a decision on a keyboard, because I knew I needed one that was going to perform. After carefully reading reviews I had made my decision to go with this keyboard, the CTK-5000 from Casio. I have to say first hand that I am absolutely impressed with this keyboard and all of the features it has to offer. A pitch-bending wheel, 600 sounds, songs, rhythms as well as music intros, fill-ins, variations, endings, etc. It contains a dynamic interface which shows you which keys to press on the keyboard when playing a song, a very intuitive tool for beginners and a great resource in general. The ability to connect to external devices such as a synthesizer, sustain pedal, and even your computer! really sold me as well. If you own Midi software, you can really take your music composition to another level. You can save music to an SD card slot which is a very nice way to back up what you have composed. Multi-track recording, sample creation, layered keys, etc are also just part of this wonderful device. I really have not found any reason not to like the purchase I have made. There are new things to experiment with every time I play.

DAP Caulk be gone worked when the Goo Gone Caulk remover didn't even phase the stubborn caulking on my home. I applied the CBG and then scored the caulking with a sharp Stanley knife. The most I had to let the DAP product sit was 2 hours and the caulk mostly scraped right off and the rest came off with a wire brush. Caveat! Some tubes the liquid separated from the gel so warm the tube in warm NOT HOT water and then kneed it thoroughly before opening to make sure the product is mixed. This was a great time saver for me on both Polyurethane and Latex based caulk. Not perfect but I would use again.

Released on the Telarc label in 1985, this album is amazing! Alright, so I went onto Wikipedia to try to find out anything on Don Dorsey, because I had NEVER heard of him. Come to find out he composed most if not all the music for the evening firework shows at Walt Disney World. Now most everyone can agree that the heavily synthesized music that the park used throughout the 80's is cheesy and is extremely dated. However, this album wipes the dust off of those old digital synthesizers and is quite charming. This album and its sequel are equally wonderful albums. If one enjoys the soothing sounds of Ray Lynch this album should not be ignored!
